title: Electric Vehicles Impact on the Power Grid: A Review of Challenges, V2G Integration, and AI-Based Control Strategies
authors: Sahil V. Bansod, Rudresh P. Kadu, Madhuri A. Chaudhari
publish: 2025 IEEE 4th International Conference on Smart Technologies for Power, Energy and Control (STPEC)
abstract: The increasing use of electric vehicles (EVs) is changing travel patterns and, by extension, has significant implications for the electricity supply system. One of the most important issues related to EVs is their effect on the electrical network when a large number of vehicles are connected at the same time for charging. Although EVs help in lowering greenhouse gas emissions, their sudden demand surge can cause circuit overload. The transmission lines can be loaded with additional demand, especially during the evening peak hours. The voltage levels can show erratic variations if the charging is not managed. These issues are more probable to occur in the local distribution network than in the national network. The charging patterns of consumers show immediate charging as soon as they arrive at work, rather than managing the demand over time. This paper focuses on the implications of EV charging on the local network, particularly on the weaknesses of the local power grids. It covers charging stations, current regulation methods, stability issues, and harmonic distortion. In addition, it explores the idea of vehicle-to-grid (V2G) power flow and artificial intelligence-based smart charging systems. The paper integrates the latest research to identify the key operational issues and their management strategies necessary for the stable and sustainable integration of electric vehicles into the electricity supply system.

title: $\text{MoS}_{2}$ /Barium Ferrite Nanocomposite: a Synergistic Electrode for Advanced Supercapacitor Applications
authors: Shivam Kumar Mittal, Udeshwari Jamwal, K. L. Yadav
publish: 2025 IEEE 4th International Conference on Smart Technologies for Power, Energy and Control (STPEC)
abstract: In this work, a novel multidimensional 2D/0D nanocomposite based on flower-like molybdenum disulfide (<tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\text{MoS}_{2}$</tex>) and barium ferrite (<tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\text{BaFe}_{12} \mathrm{O}_{19}$</tex>) nanoparticles was developed via one-step hydrothermal method for high-performance supercapacitor applications. The incorporation of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$10 \text{wt} \% \text{BaFe}_{12} \mathrm{O}_{19}$</tex> into the <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\text{MoS}_{2}$</tex> matrix significantly enhanced the electrochemical performance by improving electrical conductivity, redox activity, and structural stability. The MS-BFO10 electrode exhibited a <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{s}}$</tex> of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$215 ~\mathrm{F} ~\mathrm{g}^{-1}$</tex> at <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$5 \text{mV} \mathrm{s}{}^{-1}$</tex>. Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy revealed reduced charge transfer resistance, while long-term cycling at <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$10 \text{Ag} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$</tex> showed outstanding stability with <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\sim 104 \%$</tex> capacitance retention and nearly 100 % coulombic efficiency over 5,000 cycles. Kinetic analysis confirmed that the charge storage mechanism is primarily capacitive in nature, supported by a high b-value of 0.91 and a capacitive contribution of up to 92 % at higher scan rates. These findings highlight the synergistic effect of the <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\text{MoS}_{2} / \text{BaFe}_{12} \mathrm{O}_{19}$</tex> architecture, offering a promising strategy for designing advanced supercapacitor electrode materials with superior energy storage performance and long-term durability.

title: Thermal Analysis of Multiphase Electromagnetic Coupler for High Power Contactless Charging System Considering Core and Shielding Effects
authors: Sukumar Das, Apurv Kumar Yadav
publish: 2025 IEEE 4th International Conference on Smart Technologies for Power, Energy and Control (STPEC)
abstract: This paper presents a thermal analysis approach for a high-power multiphase inductive power transfer (IPT) system, with a focus on the thermal behavior of the magnetic core and electromagnetic shielding materials. The study incorporates detailed electromagnetic and thermal co-simulation using FEA in ANSYS to evaluate the spatial heat distribution across core, winding, and shielding structures. The influence of eddy current loss, core loss, and ohmic heating in conductors is analyzed. The thermal model is validated against experimental data and is used to optimize design of the magnetic core, coil geometry and shielding layout to minimize hotspots and improve thermal dissipation. Results demonstrate that appropriate selection of core and shielding can significantly reduce localized heating, enabling the system to operate safely at higher power levels.

title: Impact Assessment of Fast DC EV Charging Station Integration in an Islanded Microgrid Using a Node Sensitivity-Based Approach
authors: Amrutha Raju Battula, Sagar Babu Mitikiri, Dushyant Sharma, Vedantham Lakshmi Srinivas
publish: 2025 IEEE 4th International Conference on Smart Technologies for Power, Energy and Control (STPEC)
abstract: This study proposes a node-sensitivity-based methodology to leverage fast DC electric vehicle (EV) charging stations for enhancing power-system stability during grid-to-island transitions. The IEEE 14-bus system, modeled in PSS®E, serves as the testbed to evaluate the dynamic role of EV charging stations as auxiliary power sources. An analytical framework incorporating the load-reduction effectiveness (LRE) and enhanced load-impact sensitivity index (ELISI) identifies critical nodes vulnerable to voltage and frequency instability. The study also evaluates vehicle-to-grid (V2G) operation, wherein EV charging stations act as temporary power supplies during islanding events. Simulations show that strategically integrated EV charging stations improve transient stability and reduce system stress during grid disturbances. The results indicate a viable pathway for EV charging infrastructure to provide ancillary services, informing planning for resilience and emergency response.

title: Subsynchronous Oscillations Identification in Series-Compensated DFIG-Based Wind Farm Using Machine Learning Algorithms: A Comprehensive Review
authors: Sameer P. Meshram, Jawaharlal Bhukya, Soumyabrata Das
publish: 2025 IEEE 4th International Conference on Smart Technologies for Power, Energy and Control (STPEC)
abstract: In wind-integrated power grids, sub-synchronous oscillations (SSOs) are gaining attention due to their potential impact on system stability, particularly following several reported incidents in the United States and China. These events are primarily associated with the interplay between seriescompensated transmission lines and power electronic converters. SSOs arise when two or more components within the power system (PS) exchange energy at frequencies less than the nominal system frequency. The integration of wind power plants (WPPs) introduces additional complexities, making SSO one of the critical challenges in ensuring system stability. Notably, the SSO phenomena observed in real-world wind farms (WFs) are not yet fully addressed in the conventional SSO literature. This article offers an extensive review of the traditional classifications of SSOs as defined by IEEE, proposes an updated classification framework based on recent research developments, and evaluates various SSO monitoring and identification techniques. Furthermore, the limitations of existing methods are discussed. The paper aims to provide a practical perspective and outline future research directions, assisting PS planners and operators in achieving secure, economical, and sustainable grid operations.

title: Estimating Power System Inertia: A Study with Renewable Energy Integrated Power Networks
authors: Nitisha Sudhir Darwade, S.T. Nagarajan
publish: 2025 IEEE 4th International Conference on Smart Technologies for Power, Energy and Control (STPEC)
abstract: It is widely known that, with adoption of Renewable Energy Sources (RES) in the power system, overall inertia of system decreases. Continuous monitoring of system inertia is essential to prevent the failure of protection schemes and components during low system inertia. This paper summarizes the formulas used for calculation of inertia in power system. Further, it explains quantum of reduction in inertia with numerical calculation, when RES are integrated in the system. In addition, it proposes simple approach for calculation of synthetic inertia to be provided in presence of RES. The proposed approach for calculation of reduced inertia or synthetic inertia has been verified based on IEEE benchmark models.

title: Asymmetrical Dc-Link Voltage Based 51-Level Multilevel Inverter Topology with Less Switch Count
authors: Uday Patel, Siddharthsingh K. Chauhan, P. N. Tekwani
publish: 2025 IEEE 4th International Conference on Smart Technologies for Power, Energy and Control (STPEC)
abstract: A multilevel inverter (MLI) topology based on, both, symmetrical (SYM) and asymmetric (ASYM) dc-link voltages is proposed in this paper. The proposed MLI topology generates 11-level output voltage under SYM dc-link conditions. By employing ASYM dc-link voltages, the same topology produces output voltage levels ranging from 13 to 51, thereby offering enhanced voltage resolution and improved output voltage waveform quality. The number of power semiconductor controlled switches, driver circuits, dc voltage sources, diodes, capacitors, and the total number of components in the proposed MLI topology are compared with those in recently published MLI topologies. The performance of the proposed MLI topology is evaluated using MATLAB. The results show that proposed MLI topology produces a higher voltage level with reduced total harmonic distortion. Additionally, the proposed topology provides a stable output voltage under various dynamic conditions. The multiple dc-link voltage sources of the proposed topology makes it a good choice for photovoltaic applications, microgrids, and also gridtied systems.

title: Five-Level Fault-Tolerant Inverter with Preserved Output Under Multiple Switch Failure
authors: Prajesh Niranjan, Vimlesh Verma
publish: 2025 IEEE 4th International Conference on Smart Technologies for Power, Energy and Control (STPEC)
abstract: As the demand for multi-level inverters (MLIs) increases for high-power applications, the number of electronic switches required by consumers who use these MLIs exceeds that of the conventional 2-level inverter. These semiconductor devices must be protected against any faults to ensure the continuity of the power supply. A new Fault-Tolerant (FT) MLI is proposed in this paper that can overcome both single and multiple opencircuit switch faults while preserving post-fault output power. The proposed inverter is tested over dynamic load conditions and on different modulation indices. The superior features of the proposed FT-MLI are lower voltage stress, less power loss, and higher efficiency, which fit best for industrial and consumer electronics-based applications. The proposed FT topology is tested on the SIMULINK tool and validated experimentally, followed by the comparative analysis. PLECS software is used to calculate the power loss and efficiency.

title: Solar-Powered Boost Converter Using PID Control for Efficient Speed Regulation of a BLDC Motor and Real-Time MPPT
authors: Mohit Singh, Naushad Hussain, Madhu Singh
publish: 2025 IEEE 4th International Conference on Smart Technologies for Power, Energy and Control (STPEC)
abstract: Environmental concerns have led to an expansion in the implementation of renewable energy for a range of applications. The most viable solution appears to be photovoltaic (PV) technology, which converts sunlight into electricity and is an environmentally friendly option. The energy required to operate the BLDC motor is obtained from the solar panel. The speed control method for Permanent Magnet-based BLDC (PM-BLDC) motors is presented in this paper, which combines a P&O-based algorithm and Incremental conductance (Inc) with a PID controller. This method achieves robust speed regulation in changing operational conditions by combining the precision of PID control with the adaptive characteristics of Perturb and Observe (P&O) and Inc. algorithms. The energy required to operate the BLDC motor is sourced from the solar panel. However, the voltage output of a solar panel is not always constant. Therefore, a boost converter is necessary, as the solar panel's voltage may be insufficient to operate the BLDC motor. The converter elevates the voltage required to operate a BLDC motor.

title: Dual Parametric Fractional Controller for Unstable Fractional Order Processes
authors: Rammurti Meena, Sudipta Chakraborty, Vipin Chandra Pal
publish: 2025 IEEE 4th International Conference on Smart Technologies for Power, Energy and Control (STPEC)
abstract: Recent studies in control theory have highlighted the significant interest in fractional order (FrO) plants with dead time. However, tuning fractional order controllers presents challenges due to the numerous tunable parameters and the lack of systematic tuning methods. Controlling unstable industrial processes with dead time is particularly challenging. Control structures incorporating dual-loop configuration have demonstrated greater effectiveness than conventional proportional-integral-derivative (PID) controllers in unity feedback loops for such processes. This paper presents a Smith-predictor (SP) based dual-loop control structure for FrO unstable processes with time delays. A key advantage of this design is its ability to be analytically tuned based on defined maximum sensitivity (<tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$M_{s}$</tex>) and phase-margin (<tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\phi_{m}$</tex>) specifications. The effectiveness of the proposed design is assessed by testing it with altered plant models, and then quantifiable performance indicators are derived. The findings indicate that the proposed design provides a more robust and improved closed-loop response compared to other contemporary approaches.

title: LTO Battery Fading Prediction Using Deep Learning
authors: Vaibhav Mandhyan, Darshan R, Sanka Saketh Ram, Sreenu Sreekumar
publish: 2025 IEEE 4th International Conference on Smart Technologies for Power, Energy and Control (STPEC)
abstract: The high thermal stability, rapid charge/discharge capability, and extended lifespan of Lithium Titanate Oxide (LTO) batteries enhanced their applicability in electric vehicles, grid storage, and other high-demand applications. However, LTO cells experience gradual capacity fading with repeated charge-discharge cycling, like other lithium battery variants. This impacts the overall performance and reliability. This necessitates accurate degradation behaviour prediction for developing intelligent battery management systems and ensuring operational safety. Therefore, this paper proposes deep learning models for forecasting the capacity fading trend in LTO batteries. Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM), Gated Recurrent Unit (GRU), Convolutional Neural Network (CNN), and four hybrid architectures: LSTM-GRU, LSTM-CNN, LSTM-GRU-CNN, and CNNGRU, are implemented for capacity fading forecasting. These models are trained and validated using real-world battery cycling data, and their performance is assessed based on metrics like Mean Absolute Percentage Error (MAPE), Mean Absolute Error (MAE), and Root Mean Square Error (RMSE). The comparative analysis highlights the strengths and trade-offs of each architecture, providing valuable guidance for selecting the most suitable models in practical battery health prediction scenarios.

title: Hybrid BO-LSBoost Model for Accurate Prediction of Remaining Useful Life of Li-Ion Battery
authors: Shivi Varshney, Bhavnesh Kumar, Alok Prakash Mittal
publish: 2025 IEEE 4th International Conference on Smart Technologies for Power, Energy and Control (STPEC)
abstract: Estimating the Remaining Useful Life (RUL) of lithium-ion batteries is important in maintenance scheduling, safety evaluations, and applications like electric vehicles or renewable energy storage systems. We present a Hybrid Bayesian Optimization - Least Squares Boosting (BO - LSBoost) model to estimate RUL; in addition, the hybrid framework utilizes an original feature selection methodology that combines mutual information (MI), statistically-ranked feature importance, and synthetic features based on battery voltage time series that provide additional predictive capacity and improve RUL estimation accuracy. In addition, Bayesian Optimization is used to optimize the hyperparameters of the LSBoost to reduce prediction error. The combined BO - LSBoost models has improved RMSE of 1.3210 compared to the baseline LSBoost RMSE of 4.2959. This work shows that the BO - LSBoost has greater prediction accuracy while being less computationally intensive than other learning models such as Support Vector Machine (SVM), Random Forest (RF), and Artificial Neural Network (ANN). Overall, the BO - LSBoost architecture provides accurate, custom, and generalized estimates of RUL that could improve the functionality of Battery Management Systems (BMS) for grid scale Battery Energy Storage Systems (BESS).

title: Indirect Field-Oriented Control of a Single-Phase Induction Motor Fed by a Three-Phase Inverter
authors: Boikuntha Bikash Buragohain, Parthajit Dutta, Soumya Samanta
publish: 2025 IEEE 4th International Conference on Smart Technologies for Power, Energy and Control (STPEC)
abstract: This paper presents the design, modeling, and simulation of an Indirect Field-Oriented Control (IFOC) scheme for a Single-Phase Induction Motor (SPIM) which is fed from a three-phase inverter to eliminate the need of the capacitor. A three-phase inverter emulates a balanced three-phase supply to the inherently asymmetric SPIM, allowing it to be controlled like a symmetric machine and making it suitable for lowpower electric vehicle applications. Clarke and Park transformations are employed to achieve decoupled torque and flux control. Additionally, a flux weakening mechanism is integrated with speed and current regulation. The rotor angle and slip speed are estimated mathematically, eliminating the need for flux sensors. The complete control strategy is implemented in MATLAB/Simulink, and the dynamic performance is validated through simulation. Simulation results confirm rapid dynamic response and improved torque smoothness under varying load conditions.

title: Multiple-RIS-Aided Predictable V2V Communications in Graph Structure with Doppler Spread Suppression
authors: Xue Wang, Ruixin Cao, Dongchan Sun
publish: IEEE Transactions on Vehicular Technology
abstract: Doppler spread and multipath propagation leads to delay spread cause severe phase and waveform distortion of the transmitted signal in the Internet of Vehicles (IoV) system. Reconfigurable intelligent surface (RIS) can significantly mitigate phase shifts and channel fading by leveraging their reflective elements. Meanwhile, vehicular networking communication must address various factors such as Doppler effects, multipath delay spread, and received power. Multi-objective optimization incorporating RIS enables a comprehensive balance among these performance metrics. In this paper, we model the RIS-aided network as a graph structure within a multi-objective optimization framework by integrating graph theory, where graph information represents the physical characteristics of RIS. We formulate four objective functions for Doppler spread, delay spread, received power, and system energy efficiency, and reconstruct four objective functions with phase as the core. We construct a multi-objective optimization model based on graph Laplacian regularization and present an improved Graph-NSGA-II algorithm to efficiently solve the optimization problem. Simulation results demonstrate that the Graph-NSGA-II algorithm in multi-RIS cooperative systems achieves significantly reduced computational complexity and improved communication quality and phase optimization while maintaining a low Bit Error Rate (BER).

title: Challenges and Research Directions for Large Language Model Inference Hardware_supp1-3652916.pdf
authors: David Patterson
publish: N/A

title: GSDeformer: Direct, Real-time and Extensible Cage-based Deformation for 3D Gaussian Splatting_supp1-3687062.pdf
authors: Hongchuan YU
publish: N/A
abstract: We present GSDeformer, a method that enables cage-based deformation on 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS). Our approach bridges cage-based deformation and 3DGS by using a proxy point-cloud representation. This point cloud is generated from 3D Gaussians, and deformations applied to the point cloud are translated into transformations on the 3D Gaussians. To handle potential bending caused by deformation, we incorporate a splitting process to approximate it. Our method does not modify or extend the core architecture of 3D Gaussian Splatting, making it compatible with any trained vanilla 3DGS or its variants. Additionally, we automate cage construction for 3DGS and its variants using a render-and-reconstruct approach. Experiments demonstrate that GSDeformer delivers superior deformation results compared to existing methods, is robust under extreme deformations, requires no retraining for editing, runs in real-time, and can be extended to other 3DGS variants. Project Page: <uri xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">https://jhuangbu.github.io/gsdeformer/</uri>

title: Autonomous Causal Discovery: Evaluating LLMs' Priors and Constraint Strategies for Reliability
authors: Lyuzhou Chen, Xiangyu Wang, Taiyu Ban, Derui Lyu, Qinrui Zhu, Xin Wang, Huanhuan Chen
publish: IEEE Transactions on Pattern Analysis and Machine Intelligence
abstract: Expert-guided Causal Structure Learning (CSL) incorporates prior knowledge to improve the accuracy of causal discovery, yet the acquisition of such knowledge is often restricted by the availability of human experts. While Large Language Models (LLMs) provide an alternative source of causal priors, LLM-derived knowledge can be inconsistent with the true causal structure due to hallucinations or contextual misinterpretations. This paper introduces a structural constraint measurement framework, which defines constraint strength and constraint quality to describe reliability and effectiveness, enabling a systematic evaluation of LLM-derived constraints. Using this framework, we evaluate five categories of structural constraints: Edge Existence (EEC), Edge Forbidden (EFC), Path Existence (PEC), Path Forbidden (PFC), and Order Constraints (OC). Our theoretical and empirical analyses demonstrate that while EEC offers high constraint strength, it exhibits low quality when derived from LLMs; conversely, PFC and OC provide a balanced trade-off between search-space pruning and reliability. Building on these insights, we propose a two-level CSL optimization framework that partitions the search space by node order and refines the structure using global path constraints. The results show that this framework provides an effective way to incorporate noisy LLM-derived priors into CSL, particularly in settings where expert knowledge is limited.

title: Distilling Large Language Models for Network Active Queue Management
authors: Shiva Raj Pokhrel, Deol Satish, Jonathan Kua, Anwar Walid
publish: IEEE Transactions on Networking
abstract: We propose AQM-LLM, a framework that distills Large Language Models for Active Queue Management in modern networks. Unlike conventional learning-based AQMs that require extensive feature engineering and struggle with dynamic conditions, our approach leverages the contextual reasoning capability of LLMs to enhance the Low Latency, Low Loss, and Scalable Throughput (L4S) architecture with minimal manual intervention. The L4S-LLM design introduces three key components: (i) a state encoder that transforms heterogeneous network telemetry into token embeddings, (ii) a specialized L4S-LLM head that produces congestion actions in a single inference step, and (iii) a data-driven Low-Rank Adaptation scheme that drastically reduces trainable parameters while preserving accuracy. Our open-source FreeBSD-14 implementation demonstrates improved queue delay stability and higher bandwidth utilization for both DCTCP and UDP Prague traffic. We emphasize that this work demonstrates architectural feasibility through controlled experiments; deployment on router-class hardware will require additional model optimization (e.g., compression, pruning, or quantization) and is left for future work.

title: Nash Equilibrium in Hybrid Impulsive Nonzero-Sum Game: A Weak Convexity Framework
authors: Tong Liu, Bin Hu, Zhi-Hong Guan, Dingxue Zhang, Tao Li
publish: IEEE Transactions on Automation Science and Engineering
abstract: This paper investigates the existence of Nash equilibria in multi-player nonzero-sum differential games governed by hybrid impulsive systems, where one player applies impulse controls while the remaining N players adopt piecewise-continuous controls. The proposed framework captures complex dynamics in which gradual adjustments coexist with abrupt interventions, with applications in traffic management, power dispatch, and emergency response. First, the necessary conditions for the existence of hybrid-strategy Nash equilibrium are rigorously derived using a variational method. Second, under traditional convexity assumptions, a sufficiency theorem for equilibrium existence is provided. By further introducing pseudoconvexity and invexity, a weak convexity framework with broader applicability is proposed, significantly relaxing the regularity requirements on the cost functions. Finally, numerical simulations, including a power system load dispatch case, demonstrate the validity of the theoretical results.

title: A Diversity-Preserving Multistrategy Differential Evolution Algorithm for 3-D Uncrewed Aerial Vehicle Offline and Online Path Planning_supp1-3683527.pdf
authors: Zhenyu Meng
publish: N/A
abstract: With the increasing adoption of metaheuristic algorithms in uncrewed aerial vehicle (UAV) path planning, the problem has evolved into a challenging optimization task. To address offline and online planning under diverse terrains and multiple obstacle conditions, this article proposes a diversity-preserving multistrategy differential evolution-based method for 3-D path planning. The proposed algorithm integrates three key mechanisms. First, the multistage success rate-based adaptive strategy balances global exploration and local exploitation across evolutionary stages. Second, the stagnant individual replacement mechanism enables stagnant individuals to escape local optima, mitigating premature convergence. Third, the diversity maintenance mechanism preserves diversity within the top population, thereby strengthening global search capability. Furthermore, a benchmark suite with 26 terrain scenarios is designed to comprehensively evaluate performance. Experimental results demonstrate that the proposed algorithm outperforms six state-of-the-art methods in generating superior 3-D UAV paths for both offline and online planning. Finally, tests on the CEC2013, CEC2014, and CEC2017 benchmarks confirm its competitive performance on numerical optimization.

title: GBFRS: Robust Fuzzy Rough Sets via Granular Ball Computing
authors: Xiaoyu Lian, Shuyin Xia, Binbin Sang, Guoyin Wang, Xinbo Gao
publish: IEEE Transactions on Neural Networks and Learning Systems
abstract: Fuzzy rough set (FRS) theory is effective for handling uncertainty and vagueness in complex datasets. However, most existing models rely on fine-grained pointwise analysis, resulting in poor robustness to noise. This article integrates granular ball computing into FRSs by replacing individual data points with granular balls of varying sizes. A granular ball FRS (GBFRS) framework is proposed and applied to feature selection for the first time. Each granular ball is labeled by the majority class of its internal samples, reducing the impact of noisy instances and improving noise tolerance. Within this framework, a weighted fuzzy dependency function is redefined based on fixed <uri xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">https://github.com/lianxiaoyu724/GBFRS</uri> structures, where the weight is determined by the proportion of samples within each granular ball. Larger balls have higher fuzzy dependency values and thus receive greater weights, enabling a more stable evaluation of attribute importance. The theoretical foundations, including properties of lower and upper approximations and the convergence of dependency, are formally established. The experimental results on multiple UCI datasets demonstrate that GBFRS outperforms existing methods in classification accuracy. The source codes and datasets are both available on the public link: <uri xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">https://github.com/lianxiaoyu724/GBFRS</uri>

title: A Frequency-Selective Hybrid-Core Architecture for Third-Harmonic Loss Optimization in Wireless Power Transfer Systems
authors: Jiatong Li, Chunsen Tang, Lei zhao, Yuhang Gan, Udaya Madawala
publish: IEEE Transactions on Power Electronics
abstract: This letter proposes a frequency-selective hybrid-core architecture to improve the DC-DC efficiency of a 350-kHz WPT system. In this design, the power from the fundamental-frequency is transmitted through a low-frequency core with high permeability magnetics (e.g., PC95), whilist, the third-harmonic power is transmitted through a high-frequency magnetic material (e.g., PC200). By allocating spectral components to magnetic materials according to their frequency characteristics, the proposed hybrid-core configuration reduces third-harmonic-related loss in the main magnetic path and alleviates thermal stress in the magnetic components. A mathematical model has been established to verify the validity of the proposed method. A 10 kW prototype was built alongside a conventional system for comparison. Experimental results demonstrate that the proposed approach achieves a peak efficiency of 98.22%, compared with 97.96% for the conventional system. These findings indicate a viable pathway to further efficiency enhancement even when conventional designs are widely regarded as highly optimized.

title: PCCNA: A Probabilistic Caching Strategy Based on Strong Content Consistency and Node Attributes
authors: Wenjing Jing, Quan Zheng, Chaoming Huang, Shuangwu Chen, Xiaobin Tan, Jian Yang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Green Communications and Networking
abstract: In response to the growing challenges posed by excessive redundant traffic in cache-enabled networks (cache-enabled networks), this paper presents a novel probabilistic caching strategy termed PCCNA (Probabilistic Caching based on Content Consistency and Node Attributes). A key innovation of PCCNA is its incorporation of strong content consistency between caching proxies and backend servers into the caching decision-making process, addressing a critical gap in existing caching strategies. Unlike traditional approaches that often focus on a singular optimization criterion, PCCNA integrates multiple factors such as content popularity within the network and the centrality of caching proxies based on their attributes. By adaptively computing the caching probability for content of varying popularity while ensuring strong consistency, PCCNA enhances the efficiency of in-network caching. Extensive simulation results demonstrate that PCCNA outperforms established caching strategies in terms of cache hit ratio, server load, and user service latency across diverse network topologies. This research provides significant insights into the development of effective caching mechanisms, paving the way for optimized resource utilization and improved user experiences in cache-enabled networks environments.

title: Quantitative Controllability Analysis and Optimization in Attitude and Altitude Control of High Dynamic Flight Vehicles
authors: Kai Shen, Yingxin Liu, Ding Zhong, Zheng Xu, Hang Xiao
publish: IEEE Transactions on Aerospace and Electronic Systems
abstract: As an extension of controllability theory, degree of controllability (DOC) gives a deeper insight into the quantitative performance of dynamical systems. In this paper, to explore the engineering application of DOC, a definition of DOC from the energy perspective is introduced to analyze the controller performance of a high dynamic flight vehicle (HDFV). The scenario of altitude and attitude control for HDFV with active disturbance rejection control (ADRC) is modeled. In particular, a quantitative indicator based on DOC is designed and embedded into particle swarm optimization (PSO) method to optimize controller parameters. Comparison between the proposed DOC-based indicator and traditional indicators based on error integration is made by numerical simulations. According to the results, the effectiveness of the proposed DOC PSO approach is verified by satisfied state regulation performance and robust anti-disturbance capability, which behaviors better than the classical PSO method.

title: A Hybrid Numerical Simulation–Data Driven Modeling Framework for Municipal Solid Waste Incineration Digital Twin Temperature Prediction
authors: Junfei Qiao, Jiabin Zhuang, Haixu Ding
publish: IEEE Transactions on Industrial Informatics
abstract: High-fidelity computational fluid dynamics (CFD) models can accurately describe the complex thermo–chemical processes in municipal solid waste incineration (MSWI). However, their high computational cost makes them difficult to apply in online monitoring, prediction, and control of industrial incineration systems. To overcome this limitation, this article proposes a fast and dynamic MSWI modeling framework that combines physics-based simulation with reduced-order modeling and data-driven learning to enable efficient temperature field prediction. First, a mechanism-based numerical model of a mechanical grate MSWI furnace is developed by coupling solid-phase waste combustion on the grate with gas-phase combustion simulated in Fluent, generating physically consistent temperature field data. Second, considering mechanism constraints, industrial controllability, and computational cost, key operating variables are selected and an orthogonal experimental design is employed to construct a representative simulation dataset. Third, proper orthogonal decomposition is applied to reduce the dimensionality of high-dimensional temperature fields, extracting dominant thermal modes and corresponding modal coefficients. Finally, an improved interval type-2 fuzzy neural network is used to establish the nonlinear mapping between industrial operating variables and temperature modal coefficients, enabling rapid temperature field reconstruction and short-term prediction. Simulation results show that the proposed method can effectively predict the dynamic evolution of the furnace temperature field within a 3 s horizon after operating condition changes, while reducing the full-field reconstruction time to approximately 10 s. The proposed framework significantly improves computational efficiency while preserving physical consistency, providing a practical solution for online modeling and intelligent operation of MSWI systems.

title: Teaching Radio Waves to Compute: Smart Cities will Treat Interference as a New Kind of Calculator
authors: Ana I. Pérez-Neira, Alphan Sahin
publish: IEEE Spectrum
abstract: Picture a highway with networked autonomous cars driving along it. On a serene, cloudless day, these cars need only exchange thimblefuls of data with one another. Now picture the same stretch in a sudden snow squall: The cars rapidly need to share vast amounts of essential new data about slippery roads, emergency braking, and changing conditions.

title: Liver Couinaud Segmentation: A Review on Existing Segmentation Techniques of the Couinaud Classification of Liver_supp1-3623914.pdf
authors: Gajendra Kumar Mourya
publish: N/A
abstract: Liver Couinaud segmentation is crucial in biomedical image processing for treatment planning and surgical interventions of the liver parenchyma. This is because the liver Couinaud segments help provide clinicians with a clearer and specific diagnosis of the location of the diseased areas within the liver tissue. Technological advancements in the form of machine learning (ML) and deep learning (DL) models have greatly enhanced the process of liver Couinaud segmentation. The efficiency of ML and DL models showcases their performance with incredible results. Hence, this article discusses the state-of-the-art techniques for liver Couinaud segmentation from 2006 to 2024. The segmentation techniques are divided into semi-automatic and automatic. Additionally, the online medical imaging datasets suitable for developing algorithms and frameworks for liver Couinaud segmentation are presented with their descriptions, advantages, and limitations. Furthermore, the performance metrics that are commonly used for evaluating the performance of segmentation algorithms are thoroughly discussed. Finally, the research challenges and recommendations are accentuated for future researchers who intend to bridge the highlighted gaps in liver Couinaud segmentation.

title: RSMA-Assisted Edge Collaborative Inference for Internet of Vehicles
authors: Zhijian Lin, Shaowei Yang, Xiaopei Chen, Daxin Tian, Jiguang He, Yi Fang, Wenhao Wu
publish: IEEE Transactions on Mobile Computing
abstract: With the growing deployment of intelligent applications in the Internet of Vehicles (IoV), collaborative deep neural network (DNN) inference across edge nodes has become essential for satisfying real-time processing and service demands. As a key enabler for 6G networks, rate-splitting multiple access (RSMA) offers improved transmission performance to meet the diverse requirements of multi-user communications. In this work, we propose an RSMA-assisted edge co-inference framework for distributed DNN task offloading in the IoV, where DNN inference tasks are partitioned and offloaded across roadside unit (RSU) and vehicles in a collaborative manner. The objective is to minimize the total task completion time for all vehicles by jointly optimizing the DNN partitioning and offloading strategy, RSMA power allocation, and computing resource allocation. The problem is inherently non-convex due to the strong coupling among communication, computation, and model partitioning variables. To address this, we develop a unified optimization framework and decompose it into three coordinated sub-problems. A triple-iteration joint optimization algorithm is formulated, combining successive convex approximation (SCA) based RSMA power control, genetic algorithm based DNN task scheduling, and Lagrange multiplier method based computing resource allocation at RSU. Simulation results demonstrate that, compared with baseline schemes, the proposed framework can reduce inference latency by up to <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$20\%$</tex-math></inline-formula>.

title: Air-to-Ground Covert Communication With Location and Interference Uncertainty
authors: Hongchi Chen, Junsheng Mu, Na Deng, Haichao Wei, Nan Zhao
publish: IEEE Transactions on Wireless Communications
abstract: As uncrewed aerial vehicles (UAVs) are widely used in many communication scenarios, the issue of security has also caused much concern due to the line-of-sight propagation. Therefore, in this paper, we study an air-to-ground covert communication system, where a UAV transmitter Alice transmits messages covertly to a ground receiver Bob under the communication behavior detection of a ground warden Willie with location uncertainty and concurrent interference from other ground environmental nodes whose locations follow a two-dimensional Poisson point process. Under this setup, we first provide the approximate probability distribution of the aggregated interference power from all environmental co-channel nodes to facilitate the covert analysis. Then, we derive the average covert probability separately for two cases: case 1 assumes that Willie knows the received power from Alice; case 2 assumes that Willie knows the probability distribution of the received power from Alice. Next, we derive the connection probability and the covert throughput which is the maximal transmission rate under the covertness and reliability constraints. Numerical results demonstrate the feasibility of air-to-ground covert communication with location and interference uncertainty. The results also show the average covert probability and covert throughput in case 2 are higher than that in case 1, especially for a sparse deployment of interfering nodes.

title: Large-Frequency-Ratio Dual-Band Shared-Aperture Conformal Transmit-Reflect-Array Antenna with Independent Beam Control for UAV-Based Space-Air-Ground Emergency Communication Networks
authors: Chuanfei Ding, Xiang Gao, Xiangyuan Bu, Jianping An
publish: IEEE Transactions on Antennas and Propagation
abstract: This paper proposes a large-frequency-ratio (140 GHz : 35 GHz) dual-band shared-aperture conformal transmit-reflect-array antenna (CTRA) for unmanned aerial vehicle (UAV)-based emergency communication applications. A 3×3 cascaded multilayer frequency selective surface (FSS) is designed as the low-frequency (35 GHz) ground plane while also enabling transmission of high-frequency waves (140 GHz). Subsequently, a radius-limited double-split ring is placed underneath the cascaded FSS layers to form the transmissive element (TE, defined as Unit-1). In addition, a miniaturized multilayer FSS unit is proposed as another TE (defined as Unit-2) at 140-GHz band and arranged around Unit-1 to form a full ground plane for the split ring, constituting a complete reflective element at 35-GHz band. Independent modulation of the reflection/transmission coefficients can be achieved by rotating the split ring and tuning the TE dimensions, respectively. Moreover, the joint operation of the two TEs can effectively suppress the elevated sidelobe levels caused by the larger frequency ratio. To validate the design, a prototype of the whole CTRA architecture was fabricated and measured. Experimental results have indicated the proposed CTRA exhibits a peak transmitted gain of 34.3 dBi at the 140-GHz band and a peak reflected gain of 22.8 dBi at the 35-GHz band, which demonstrates its significant potential for UAV-based space-air-ground emergency communication networks.

title: YOLO-SAE: Spatial-Adaptive Attention Enhancement for Small-Object Detection With Quantified Accuracy-Efficiency Trade-offs
authors: Syam Widiyanto, Hsiu-Ming Wu
publish: IEEE Access
abstract: Small-object detection in aerial imagery remains challenging due to background clutter, occlusion, and extreme scale variation. This paper presents YOLO–Spatial Attention Enhancement (YOLO-SAE), an attention-augmented extension of YOLOv8 that integrates two complementary components: (i) a multi-head self-attention (MHSA) block inserted at the P5 stage to incorporate long-range contextual cues at coarse feature resolution, and (ii) a compact pre-head refinement module placed before the detection heads, instantiated as Efficient Channel Attention (ECA) or Shuffle Attention (SA) to recalibrate decision-time features. Under identical training protocols and single-scale evaluation at an input resolution of 640 on VisDrone 2019 and DOTA 2.0 (HBB), the most accuracy optimal configuration (MHSA+SA) improves mAP@0.5 from 0.299 to 0.405 and mAP@ [0.5:0.95] from 0.164 to 0.236 on VisDrone, and from 0.208 to 0.333 and from 0.109 to 0.190 on DOTA, respectively. To ensure computational transparency, we report parameters, GFLOPs, and runtime breakdown (pre-/inference/post-processing) for each variant, highlighting the accuracy–efficiency trade-offs of single and composite attention designs. All experiments were conducted Google Colab using Python 3 on an NVIDIA A100 GPU under the same environment for latency and throughput reporting. We emphasize that the individual attention components are established; our contribution is a deployment-oriented composition and placement strategy with quantified accuracy–efficiency trade-offs under a controlled and reproducible protocol.

title: SpineVLM: A Markdown-Guided Structured Fine-Tuning Framework for Spine X-ray Report Generation
authors: Dong Liu, Wenhui Li, Ning Xu, Guoge Han, Rui Hao, Xianzhu Liu, An-An Liu
publish: IEEE Journal of Biomedical and Health Informatics
abstract: Automated medical report generation in specialized fields like spine radiography is constrained by data scarcity and high annotation costs. Consequently, existing multimodal large language models (MLLMs) struggle in these settings, often missing minute, scattered spinal abnormalities. We introduce SpineVLM, a data-efficient framework for structured spine X-ray report generation. The framework is built upon the newly constructed SXRG dataset, comprising 10,468 image-report pairs developed via a hierarchical AI-assisted annotation pipeline. To optimize learning under limited data, we propose Markdown-Guided Structured Learning (MGSL), which reformulates unconstrained free-text synthesis into a structured completion task, acting as a strong regularizer. Furthermore, an unsupervised Region-Focused Inference (RFI) module powered by foundation models (DINOv2) isolates the vertebral column to enhance the perception of subtle lesions without requiring manual spatial annotations. Evaluated on a 7B-parameter vision-language backbone, SpineVLM achieves strong performance against ten baseline multimodal models across standard linguistic metrics. In a double-blind reader study, the system achieved a diagnostic F1-score of 0.866, comparable to specialist performance, while reducing clinical reporting time by over 41%. By open-sourcing the dataset and codebase, we provide, to our knowledge, the first quantitative benchmark for automated spine radiography report generation, together with a structured framework for this data-limited setting. All data and code will be publicly released at <uri xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">https://github.com/LiuDongDaniel/SpineVLM</uri>.
