authors: David Gögelein, Marianne von Schwerin, Thomas Walter
publish: IEEE Access
abstract: Presents corrections to the paper, (Demand Forecasting in Data-Scarce and Resource-Restricted Environments).

title: Energy Efficient Federated Learning Over Wireless Networks: A GNN-assisted Deep Reinforcement Learning Approach
authors: Liang Wang, Zihao Wei, Bomin Mao, Qu Luo, Qihao Peng, Pei Xiao
publish: IEEE Internet of Things Journal
abstract: Implementing federated learning (FL) over wireless networks faces critical research challenges, such as high communication costs, inevitable communication latency, and significant energy consumption for model transmission and training, primarily caused by device heterogeneity and unpredictable dynamic channel conditions. This paper proposes Graph-based Resource Optimization with Compression for FL (GROC-FL), a unified framework that jointly coordinates wireless resource allocation and collaborative model compression. By leveraging Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) to model wireless topology and Deep Reinforcement Learning (DRL) to optimize communication and computation resources together with a globally consistent sparse update mechanism, GROC-FL minimizes the overall energy consumption of clients over wireless networks. In order to address the intrinsic topology dependence of wireless FL, we develop a graph-augmented DRL agent based on a graph convolutional network (GCN) that captures resource competition and network topology. We further develop a collaborative model compression module, termed Federated Parameter Negotiation (FPN), which enables clients to negotiate a global sparse mask and further reduce energy consumption during FL training. Experimental results demonstrate that GROC-FL outperforms the baselines in energy consumption, training performance, and client fairness.

title: Dual-Polarized Endfire Phased Array Antenna with Forward Beam Coverage for IoT Devices
authors: Yuepeng Yu, Changjiang Deng, Ruge Yao, Zilin Qiao
publish: IEEE Internet of Things Journal
abstract: In this paper, a dual-polarized endfire phased array antenna (PAA) based on tilted-beam elements is presented to provide forward beam steering for IoT Devices. Each antenna element comprises two mirror-symmetric modules to generate ±45° polarizations, with their normal directions oriented to the endfire direction. Each module contains two dipoles with unequal lengths, excited by a 1-to-2 feeding network to achieve forward beam tilting. This achievement is helpful to avoid large grating lobes, even with large element spacing of 0.76λ in array design. To further suppress the cross-polarization level at the zenith, a gradient polarization metasurface (GPM) is placed tightly above the elements. It can provide additional polarization for the broadside beam, without influencing the endfire beam. A 1×6 dual-polarized PAA prototype operating in the 4.8 GHz band is fabricated. The measured beam steering range is from 0° to +70° with a 3.8-dB gain fluctuation, exhibiting forward and low-elevation coverage. Within this range, the measured side lobe level is below -7.9 dB, the cross-polarization level is below -9.7 dB, and dual polarizations are consistently maintained.

title: Dynamic Analysis and Vibration Reduction of HTS Maglev Vehicle Using MRD Semi-active Control
authors: Yuchen He, Xucheng Zhou, Haitao Li, Xianhong Hu, Mingxuan Yin, Zigang Deng, Gino D'Ovidio
publish: IEEE Transactions on Intelligent Vehicles
abstract: The limited intrinsic damping characteristics of the high-temperature superconducting (HTS) pinning maglev system pose challenges to effective vehicle vibration reduction under external disturbances such as permanent magnetic guideway (PMG) irregularities. This paper incorporates a magnetorheological damper (MRD) into the secondary suspension of HTS pinning maglev vehicles for semi-active vibration control. First, an MRD dynamic model considering the influence of current is presented, and a vehicle dynamic model developed based on an existing HTS pinning maglev test line and HTS-PMG relationship obtained from experiments. Subsequently, a semi-active vibration reduction co-simulation model is constructed using the skyhook (SH) linear continuous control strategy to evaluate the vertical and lateral dynamic performance of the car body under different operating speeds influenced by PMG irregularities. The results indicate that the semi-active damping strategy based on MRD ensures safe operation while effectively reducing carbody vibration, achieving over 50% vibration reduction rate at higher speeds, demonstrating its clear potential for improving the dynamic performance of HTS pinning maglev vehicles in practical engineering.

title: Hyperbolic Micro-Expression Recognition
authors: Zixi Chen, Boxuan Hu, Junlin Hu
publish: IEEE Transactions on Biometrics, Behavior, and Identity Science
abstract: Micro-expression recognition is a fine-grained classification task that aims to classify brief, involuntary facial expressions into some basic emotion categories. The primary challenge lies in extracting robust feature representations due to the short duration, low intensity, and significant individual variability of micro-expressions. In this paper, we study micro-expression recognition problem in hyperbolic space, and propose a hyperbolic micro-expression recognition (HypMER) method that exploits vision Transformer architecture and learns a deep distance metric in hyperbolic space. Our HypMER designs a hyperbolic mapping module to project Euclidean features into hyperbolic space, leveraging the unique geometric properties of hyperbolic space to better represent hierarchical data. Additionally, we combine a hyperbolic space-based loss and a Euclidean space-based loss to optimize feature learning by exploiting the strengths of both Euclidean and hyperbolic spaces. Extensive experiments on multiple benchmark datasets show that our HypMER outperforms state-of-the-art methods, achieving notable improvements in unweighted F1-score (UF1) and unweighted average recall (UAR) and verifying its effectiveness.

title: Information-Theoretic Sensor Control for Multiple Extended Target Tracking Using Adaptive Hölder Divergence
authors: Yating Liu, Guoqing Qi, Yinya Li, Andong Sheng
publish: IEEE Sensors Journal
abstract: This article proposes an adaptive sensor control strategy within the GGIW-LMB filtering framework for elliptical extended target tracking, aiming to jointly optimize kinematic states, target extent, and cardinality. With improving sensor resolution, shape information (e.g., target size) has become increasingly important for tracking and decision-making. In practice, however, heavy clutter, missed detections, and time-varying target numbers significantly degrade the accuracy of target state and cardinality estimation. To address these challenges, a closed-form approximation of the Hölder divergence is derived specifically for the GGIW-LMB filtering framework, which enables efficient information-theoretic sensor control without particle-based numerical methods. Building on this result, an adaptive Hölder-divergencebased sensor control strategy is developed, in which the divergence parameter is dynamically adjusted according to uncertainties in target existence and state estimates. The proposed algorithm jointly accounts for kinematics and shape parameters of elliptical extended targets and improves robustness in challenging scenarios. Finally, the effectiveness of the proposed approach is validated through simulation results.

title: PVSizer: An Open-Source Python Framework for PV and BESS Sizing and Impact Analysis in Distribution Networks
authors: Yayu Yang, Samuel N. Okhuegbe, Jian Zhang, Apsana Bhandari, Zhengfa Zhang, Haozong Wang, Muhammad Umar Afzaal, Yuru Wu, Junjie Yin, Fangxing Li, Yilu Liu
publish: IEEE Transactions on Industry Applications
abstract: High photovoltaic (PV) penetration in distribution systems may cause voltage violations and thermal overloading, while existing open-source tools do not provide an integrated and automated approach for systematic PV and battery energy storage system (BESS) sizing under feeder constraints. To address this gap, this paper presents PVSizer, an open-source tool for PV-BESS sizing and impact assessment in distribution networks. Built on OpenDSS and a Python-based automation layer, PV Sizer provides three modules: (1) Impact Analysis/Time-Series Simulation for detailed quasi-static time-series (QSTS) analysis of a specified configuration; (2) Traversal for parallel feasible domain mapping under feeder operational constraints; and (3) Optimization based on constraint-guided incremental search (CGIS) to identify the maximum feasible PV capacity together with the minimum required BESS support. Case studies on a real utility feeder with over 700 nodes show strong site dependence in feasible PV-BESS sizing limits. The Optimization module reaches the same final solution as exhaustive Traversal with substantially lower computational burden, demonstrating the usefulness of PVSizer for feeder-constrained PV-BESS integration studies.

title: Rate-Distortion Trade-off in Integrated Communication, Sensing, and Control Closed-Loop Systems
authors: Yinglong Fan, Zhiyong Feng, Zeyang Meng, Zhiqing Wei, Ying Zhou, Kai Niu
publish: IEEE Transactions on Vehicular Technology
abstract: The rapid advancement of wireless communication technologies has accelerated the adoption of networked closed-loop control systems in intelligent manufacturing. Within this context, the integrated communication, sensing, and control (ICSC) framework has emerged as an important paradigm for modeling and analyzing large-scale real-time closed-loop systems. However, existing studies frequently overlook the impact of downlink communication on closed-loop control performance and fail to comprehensively model the interactions between communication, sensing, and control, making it difficult to identify the achievable performance limits under constrained communication resources. Therefore, this paper establishes a rigorous theoretical framework that explicitly accounts for both uplink sensing measurements and downlink control commands. Based on causal directed mutual information theory, we derive a lower bound on the downlink rate-distortion function that decomposes into a dynamics-dependent term and an innovation-dependent term. The results clearly quantify the influences of sensing quality and closed-loop dynamics on the minimum communication rate required to achieve a specified distortion level, and further clarify the connection between downlink communication constraints and achievable closed-loop control performance. The simulation results demonstrate the validity of the bound and further illustrate that the minimum downlink communication rate increases significantly with greater system dynamic complexity, higher sensing errors, and tighter distortion constraints. The findings reveal a fundamental rate-distortion trade-off in closed-loop systems, providing a theoretical foundation for the joint design of communication, sensing, and control.

title: Tournament-Based Index Obfuscation for Secure Space Shift Keying MIMO Systems
authors: Raed Mesleh, Mohammad Abudayah, Saud Althunibat
publish: IEEE Transactions on Vehicular Technology
abstract: A tournament-based index obfuscation framework is proposed and analyzed for space shift keying (SSK) multiple-input multiple-output (MIMO) systems. Algebraic properties of Paley tournaments are exploited to construct deterministic yet nontrivial permutations of the transmit antenna indices. These permutations are refreshed on a per-channel basis through a channel-dependent cyclic shift derived from reciprocity. As a result, a matched index-domain channel is observed at the legitimate receiver, whereas an eavesdropper is forced to operate under a mismatched decoding metric. In contrast to conventional physical-layer security techniques, the proposed framework operates exclusively in the index domain and preserves the inherent energy-efficiency and low-complexity advantages of SSK. In practical SSK implementations where the number of transmit antennas is not a power of two, only a subset of valid antenna indices is used for information mapping, while the remaining antennas participate only in the obfuscation mechanism. Reliability and secrecy are characterized through average bit error rate, mutual information, generalized mutual information, secrecy rate, equivocation, and information leakage. Both passive and active adversarial scenarios are considered. Passive attacks include uninformed mismatched eavesdropping as well as partially correlated eavesdropping channels that emulate spatial proximity and common scattering. Active training-phase attacks, including pilot contamination and forced permutation manipulation, are also examined and mitigated through tournament-structure consistency checks leading to block acceptance/rejection. The proposed cyclic-shift realization is shown to operate effectively when the legitimate nodes observe consistent channel information, while its sensitivity to channel estimation mismatch is also discussed. Numerical results demonstrate that reliable communication is preserved at the legitimate receiver, while a substantial loss in the eavesdropper' achievable rate is induced even under correlated and active attack conditions. For benchmarking purposes, a comparison with artificial-noise based SSK is also included, highlighting the fundamental difference between power-domain and index-domain physical layer security mechanisms.

title: A Review on the Implementation of a Proposed Multi-Biometric Driver's License Verification System in Zimbabwe
authors: Frank Ivine Marara, Tavengwa Masamha, Mary Muhonde, Fungai Jacqueline Kiwa
publish: 2025 4th Zimbabwe Conference of Information and Communication Technologies (ZCICT)
abstract: Zimbabwe faces persistent challenges in verifying driver's license authenticity due to reliance on manual inspections, widespread forgery, and limited digital infrastructure. In response, this study developed a secure, mobile-based driver's license verification system that integrates advanced biometric and computer vision technologies. The system employs facial recognition using OpenCV and DeepFace, fingerprint authentication through the Aratek SDK, and optical character recognition (OCR) via Tesseract for real-time license data extraction. A FastAPI backend, supported by a modular MySQL database and secured with AES-256 encryption, ensures efficient and compliant data processing. It also reduced verification time, enhanced detection of counterfeit licenses, and functioned reliably in low-resource environments. Despite its strengths, challenges remain, including integration with legacy systems, data privacy concerns, and the need for comprehensive legal frameworks governing biometric data usage. Nevertheless, the system aligns with Zimbabwe's Cyber and Data Protection Act and supports broader national objectives such as road safety, public service modernization, and trust in digital identity systems. This paper outlines the system's architecture, implementation, and potential for nationwide scalability.

title: An Evaluation of Zero-Trust Security Models on Institutional Data Protection in Zimbabwean Universities
authors: Gladman Jekese, Thanks Hondoma, Netsayi Chabata, Kundayi G Chinyongo, Bridget Munyoro
publish: 2025 4th Zimbabwe Conference of Information and Communication Technologies (ZCICT)
abstract: With Zimbabwean universities adopting digitalization through online learning environments, cloud-based research data storage and administrative systems, safeguarding sensitive institutional data has become an utmost priority. This research assesses the effect of Zero-Trust Security Model implementation in strengthening data protection among public and private universities in Zimbabwe. Traditional perimeter-based security proves inadequate against the intelligent persistent attacks, insider threats and increasingly mobile and decentralized IT infrastructures. The Zero-Trust model fundamentally revamps the security approach based on an assumption of compromise and the need for continuous authentication of all users, devices and traffic irrespective of location. The research adopts a mixed-methods approach consisting of technical audits of current security architectures and interviews with IT directors, system administrators and cybersecurity officers in five large Zimbabwean universities. Analysis of incident reports, access logs and data breach metrics are further utilized to establish a correlation between Zero-Trust policy implementation and data leak mitigation. Results shows that institutions that have implemented core Zero-Trust features report fewer instances of data breach and better detection of unusual behavior. Yet, there are still obstacles that hinders institutions from rolling out Zero-Trust solutions. This study offers a readiness model and implementation plan specific to higher learning institutions in resource-scarce situations. The findings of this study are important to policymakers, university senates and IT governance boards intent on updating their security posture. The study further contributes to national and regional discussion on cyber-readiness of universities in the pursuit of Fourth Industrial Revolution objectives as well as safe digital learning environments.

title: A Microscopic Traffic Simulation Study on the Impact of Bus-Only Lane Opening on Urban Traffic Mobility
authors: Qihao Zheng
publish: Proceedings of the 2026 2nd International Conference on Big Data, Communication Technology and Computer Applications
abstract: None

title: Leakage Current Minimization in Single Phase Grid Connected Solar PV
authors: Midhun Mohan, Sreeraj E S, Srikanth Kumar S. Nagavelly, Suruchi Jain, Javal Praful Gadher, Pradip M. Magar
publish: 2025 IEEE 4th International Conference on Smart Technologies for Power, Energy and Control (STPEC)
abstract: Leakage current or residual current is the current that flows through an undesired path in an electric circuit via ground during normal operations or it is the one which leaks from the power circuit and flows through the ground. The most vital consideration in transformer-less photovoltaic (PV) inverters is the suppression of residual current. Literature has many transformer-less PV inverter topologies, with residual current suppressed by the means of DC or AC side decoupling method and common-mode voltage (CMV) clamping. Both DCdecoupling and AC-decoupling indicate that isolation is offered on the inverter's DC or AC sides respectively, while the voltage is at zero. As a result of fewer switches in the conducting line, AC side decoupling typically has reduced losses. However, due to the influence of circuit resonance and the parasitic junction capacitance of switches, residual current cannot be totally removed using decoupling and modulation approaches. In order to reduce residual current, certain transformer-less topologies employ CMV clamping. Residual current suppression method in a DC-decoupling topology (H5 topology), an AC-decoupling topology (H6 topology) and a novel HERIC derived topology by considering the influence of switches' junction capacitance are presented in this work.

title: Triple Switch Triple Mode ASL DC-DC Converter for PV System
authors: Sushma Korra, Sahendara Kumar, Avneet Kumar
publish: 2025 IEEE 4th International Conference on Smart Technologies for Power, Energy and Control (STPEC)
abstract: Renewable energy sources such as fuel cells and photovoltaic (PV) cells generate power at low DC output voltages. As a result, high-gain DC-DC converters are required to increase these low voltages to appropriate high voltage levels for efficient power transmission and DC microgrid applications. This manuscript proposes a new Triple-Switch Triple-Mode (TSTM) high-gain DC-DC converter, which is designed to enhance reliability and performance for DC microgrid applications. The proposed converter topology also enhances voltage-boosting capabilities in line with the effective reduction of the voltage stress across the semiconductor devices and capacitors in the circuit. To evaluate the performance of the proposed converter design, a 400 W converter operating with an input voltage 25 V and delivering power at output voltage of 200 V at a switching frequency of 50 kHz is simulated using MATLAB.

title: Performance Investigation and Characterization of High Semiconductor Devices using ERSC
authors: Aman Keshari, Mohammad Asif Ali, Dogga Raveendhra, Suneel Raju Pendem, B L Narasimharaju
publish: 2025 IEEE 4th International Conference on Smart Technologies for Power, Energy and Control (STPEC)
abstract: This paper presents an innovative method for dynamic characterization of wide bandgap (WBG) semiconductor devices using an Energy Recirculation and Storage Circuit (ERSC). Traditional Double Pulse Test (DPT) techniques require high-voltage sources, which are often unavailable in laboratory environments. The proposed ERSC-based approach enables highvoltage and high-current testing using a low-power input source by leveraging energy recirculation and modular feedback. Two topologies—single-ended buck/boost and boost/buck ERSCs—are analyzed, with detailed operation in magnetize and charge modes. Theoretical modeling supported by simulation in Simulink and demonstrates effective performance characterization of power switches, achieving up to 14 kW equivalent load testing from a 1.4 kW input. The setup also addresses challenges related to waveform acquisition, switching loss evaluation, and crosstalk effects in WBG devices. The proposed method provides a costeffective and scalable solution for power semiconductor testing.

title: Design and Development of a Dynamic Wireless Charging System Using a Single Inverter with Constant Voltage and Constant Current Charging
authors: R. Nakkeeran, C. Bharatiraja
publish: 2025 IEEE 4th International Conference on Smart Technologies for Power, Energy and Control (STPEC)
abstract: The growing popularity of Electric Vehicles (EVs), there is a growing demand for efficient, safe, and easy-to-use charging systems. This paper presents the design and development of a wireless charging system that uses a single inverter to manage Constant Voltage (CV) and Constant Current (CC) charging modes. The system is based on Dynamic Inductive Power Transfer (D-IPT) technology, which enables contactless electricity transfer. This makes charging more convenient and safer than traditional wired methods. A key advantage of this system is that it uses a single power stage to seamlessly switch between <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$C V$</tex> and <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$C C$</tex> modes, helping to reduce capacity, size, cost, and complexity. The design is modular, meaning it can be easily adapted to different charging needs and conditions, even by changing the alignment of the charger with the vehicle. Simulations using ANSYS Maxwell and MATLAB, as well as testing on a 3.3 kW laboratory prototype, demonstrated that the system can achieve efficiencies of over 88%. These results demonstrate that the system works efficiently under real-world conditions and is suitable for dynamic charging applications such as electric vehicles. This research supports the future of wireless charging for EVs by providing a reliable and scalable solution.

title: Hybrid Machine Learning for Uncertainty-Aware Net Load Forecasting in Renewable-Integrated Grids
authors: Yogita Singh, Vaibhavi Awasthi, Jyoti Kumari, Deepak Kumar, G. Lloyds Raja
publish: 2025 IEEE 4th International Conference on Smart Technologies for Power, Energy and Control (STPEC)
abstract: The integration of renewable energy sources (RES) into power grids has increased variability and uncertainty in net load forecasting. A novel hybrid Bayesian Neural NetworkRandom Forest (BNN-RF) model is proposed for short-term net load orecasting in high-RES power systems. The model combines BNNs' probabilistic forecasting capabilities with RFs' feature robustness and interpretability. The model achieves a 1.74 % reduction in root mean square error compared to conventional ANN models, providing reliable uncertainty estimates for grid operators managing RES intermittency. The hybrid approach addresses limitations of individual models, highlighting the potential of hybrid machine learning models for improving forecasting accuracy and operational decision-making in renewable-rich power grids.

title: Economic and Operational Analysis of Virtual Energy Storage in Energy Communities
authors: Vishal Sharma, Shubham Patwari, Satish Sharma, Prerna Jain
publish: 2025 IEEE 4th International Conference on Smart Technologies for Power, Energy and Control (STPEC)
abstract: The recent decline in storage technology costs and the rise of economically-driven energy exchange mechanisms are fostering the growth of energy communities. This paper presents an evolved transactive control framework for shared energy communities featuring independent energy suppliers, retailers, and prosumers. The focus is on a virtual energy storage system (VESS), wherein each supplier has an individual battery. Due to underutilization and high costs, prosumers often cannot fully leverage their battery capacity. By aggregating suppliers' batteries, we create a virtual capacity that exceeds the actual physical capacity. This system optimizes battery usage since not all prosumers charge or discharge simultaneously. The control problem discussed involves the efficient scheduling of energy operations for a collection of prosumers, each with their own demand and generation capabilities, and a group of energy storage suppliers that can store and retrieve excess energy from the prosumers. This paper proposes a centralized system in which a central authority oversees and controls all energy transactions, enhancing the efficiency and sustainability of energy usage in these communities.

title: Cost Effective Reconfigurable Induction Motor Stator Windings for Single Phase Integrated Onboard Charger
authors: Deepak Sai Duvvapu, Barry Venugopal Reddy, Srinvas. K
publish: 2025 IEEE 4th International Conference on Smart Technologies for Power, Energy and Control (STPEC)
abstract: Accelerating electric vehicle (EV) battery charging while ensuring long-range remains a pivotal challenge in advancing sustainable transportation. An Integrated On-Board Charger (IOBC) emerges as a compelling solution by repurposing existing traction drive components as an AC-DC front-end converter during charging. This results in significant reductions in charger size, weight, and overall system cost leaving much space for the battery to increase drive range. However, a key challenge lies in managing uneven winding current distribution, which can induce torque ripple and compromise system performance. This paper presents a modified IOBC topology that effectively mitigates this issue, ensuring balanced current flow across motor windings and equal magnetic stress during the charging process. Notably, the proposed solution requires only minimal hardware reconfiguration, enabling seamless operation in both vehicle-to-grid (V2G) and grid-to-vehicle (G2V) modes. The electromagnetic performance of the proposed architecture is designed and validated through finite element analysis using ANSYS, while steady-state operation and control strategies are verified through detailed simulations in MATLAB/Simulink. The findings highlight the practical viability and efficiency of the proposed IOBC design for next-generation EV applications.

title: Embedded Realization of Deep Learning Based LMPC for PMSM Drives
authors: Akash Patil, Yoshit Tapar, Pramod Ubare, Nirlipta Ranjan Mohanty, D. N. Sonawane
publish: 2025 IEEE 4th International Conference on Smart Technologies for Power, Energy and Control (STPEC)
abstract: In today's time, Model Predictive Control (MPC) has gained attention as a viable alternative to conventional control approaches in the motor control applications due to its capability to handle multi-input, multi-output constraints. However, due to its optimization-based nature, MPC requires significant computational resources, necessitating strong hardware and robust algorithms when implemented on embedded systems. MPC's computational efficiency can be improved by using better optimization solvers or better MPC design, but each has its own set of limitations. This research suggests deep neural network based linear model predictive control (DNN-MPC) as a method to reduce computational burden while maintaining performance characteristics. Additionally, this study demonstrates a systematic process of embedded implementation on low-end microcontrollers. DNN model is trained using data generated through a Monte Carlo method based on the LMPC model. The DNN-MPC attained an accuracy of 97.28%, acquiring all the characteristics of LMPC. Despite this negligible reduction in accuracy, the DNN-based model offers substantial improvements in computational efficiency, making it a viable alternative for real-time applications. The proposed DNN-MPC approach has validated using hardware-in-the-loop (HIL) co-simulation on a low-end STM-32 microcontroller. Furthermore, this study also presents a systematic approach of Embedded code-generation on embedded hardware.

title: Design, Analysis and Control of Multi-Mode Cell Voltage Equalizer Using Fly-Back Converter for Series-Connected Battery String
authors: Abhaya Kumar Sahoo, Keerthi Saladi, Sashwathkumar S, Lizanne Fernandes, V. Sankaranarayanan
publish: 2025 IEEE 4th International Conference on Smart Technologies for Power, Energy and Control (STPEC)
abstract: Conventional direct-cell-to-cell (DC2C) cell voltage equalizers exhibit lower balancing speed, particularly in large battery strings, due to only a single active balancing path at any given time. This sequential charge transfer from high to low voltage cells counterparts continues until voltage uniformity is achieved across all cells, resulting in extended balancing duration. To address the above limitation, this study proposes a multi-mode DC2C cell voltage equalizer designed to enhance balancing speed under various cell voltage imbalance conditions. The proposed architecture employs a single fly-back converter operating in three distinct modes, each tailored to specific imbalance scenarios to optimize energy redistribution. A comprehensive design methodology, operating principle, and control strategy for the multi-mode equalizer are presented, supported by flowchart. Simulation analysis justify that the proposed multimode DC2C equalizer achieves significantly improved voltage balancing performance compared to conventional single-path equalizers, thereby validating its efficacy and suitability for high-performance battery management systems.

title: Design and Evaluation of Carboxymethyl Cellulose-Starch Hybrid Gel Electrolytes Toward Quasi-Solid-State Zinc-Ion Batteries
authors: Sreshtha Ganguly, Dilip Bennada, Sreeraj Puravankara
publish: 2025 IEEE 4th International Conference on Smart Technologies for Power, Energy and Control (STPEC)
abstract: This work presents the development and systematic evaluation of starch-carboxymethyl cellulose (CMC) hybrid gel electrolytes (HGEs) for application in quasi-solid-state zinc-ion batteries (ZIBs). While CMC-based membranes exhibit high electrolyte uptake and ionic conductivity, they suffer from poor mechanical strength, limiting their practical applicability. To overcome this limitation, starch was incorporated at varying concentrations to reinforce the CMC matrix. Comprehensive physicochemical and electrochemical analyses revealed that starch addition significantly influenced both the structural integrity and ionic transport properties. The optimized composition, containing an equal mass ratio of starch and CMC (CS50-50), exhibited the highest tensile strength (35 MPa), moderate electrolyte uptake, and a good ionic conductivity of approximately <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$4.38 \text{mS} \text{cm}^{-1}$</tex>. These findings demonstrate that starch-CMC hybrid membranes are promising, eco-friendly gel electrolytes for flexible and safe aqueous ZIBs.

title: Converter Configuration and Control for Switch Fault Detection and Clearance in PMBLDC Motor Driven EVs
authors: Rahul Arora, Sanjeev Singh, Ujjwal Kumar Kalla, Dheeraj Kumar
publish: 2025 IEEE 4th International Conference on Smart Technologies for Power, Energy and Control (STPEC)
abstract: Electric vehicles (EVs) in low power applications such as two or three wheelers, mostly use permanent magnet brushless DC (PMBLDC) motor. These motors suffer switch fault in the drive converter especially open switch and short switch faults. This paper presents a quick fault detection and isolation of faulty leg while replacing it with a healthy leg. The complete experiment is performed on three leg six switch voltage source inverter, which is commonly used to commutate the phases of PMBLDC motor. The complete logic of switch fault detection, isolation and replacement of faulty leg with healthy leg is realized on MATLAB Simulink platform and verified on experimental hardware setup having a processor microlab box 1201 for a 200V, 3000 rpm, 1 kW PMBLDC motor.

title: Data-Driven Fault Severity Estimation in Permanent Magnet Synchronous Machines
authors: Khushboo Ramawat, Anukriti Sharma, Bharat Singh Rajpurohit
publish: 2025 IEEE 4th International Conference on Smart Technologies for Power, Energy and Control (STPEC)
abstract: Stator inter-turn faults are among the most prevalent and damaging electrical failures in Permanent Magnet Synchronous Machines (PMSMs), necessitating timely detection and accurate severity estimation for reliable operation. This paper presents a data-driven diagnostic framework based on Temporal Convolutional Networks (TCNs) to identify and classify the severity levels of stator faults using raw vibration signals. The proposed TCN architecture is developed to analyze 1D timeseries vibration data collected from three PMSMs with different power ratings (1kW, 1.5kW, and 3kW), under four operating conditions: healthy, low, medium and high fault severities. Comparative analysis with a baseline Convolutional Neural Networks with Gated Recurrent Unit (CNN+GRU) model demonstrates that the TCN achieves superior performance, exceeding 99% classification accuracy while offering faster inference and greater generalization. The results confirm the suitability of TCNs for real-time, scalable, and non-invasive fault monitoring in industrial applications.

title: Intelligent Grasp Planning Using Open-Vocabulary Image Segmentation and Large Language Models
authors: Prithvi Raj Baskar, Bijo Sebastian, Alpana Dubey, Suma Mani Kuriakose
publish: 2025 IEEE 4th International Conference on Smart Technologies for Power, Energy and Control (STPEC)
abstract: Intelligent autonomous grasp planning is a key challenge in real-world deployment of robotic systems. Generating robust grasp require solving multiple challenges such perception, semantic segmentation, motion planning and grasp stability optimization. Over the years, many techniques have been proposed to solve one or more of the above challenges. Recent evolution of end-to-end learning for grasp planning has demonstrated the use of deep learning methods that map sensory data, such as images or point clouds, directly to grasp action. But these approaches require large datasets to train, and often struggle at sim-toreal transfer. Moreover the trained architecture is usually not interpretable and cannot guarantee safety or repeatability, which are important factors in real-world deployments. To address these limitations, we propose a novel grasp planning pipeline that can leverage the inference capability of Large Language Models, along with open vocabulary image segmentation. The depth image segmentation is further processed using classical algorithms that fit primitive shapes to the segmented point cloud data, resulting in stable grasp pose. The overall pipeline results in a repeatable and explainable pipeline for intelligent grasp generation, especially for everyday objects. The proposed pipeline was evaluated on two different scenes consisting everyday objects with varying clutter density. The pipeline was also compared with a data driven model on a set of standard shapes. Results indicate reliable grasp generation for everyday objects as well as comparable performance with the data driven approach on the standard set of objects.

title: 3-Phase Triple-Boosted Multilevel Inverter for Photovoltaic Applications with Seven Levels of Switched Capacitors
authors: Vinayak Kumar, Ruchi Agarwal, Pravindra Kumar
publish: 2025 IEEE 4th International Conference on Smart Technologies for Power, Energy and Control (STPEC)
abstract: A novel 3- <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\phi$</tex> single source triple boost (SSTB-MLI) with <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$3 \times$</tex> voltage boosting capabilities is proposed in this paper for applications involving medium voltage and maximum efficiency. It is designed with the lowest component counts e.g. single DC source, two diodes, only eight switches and two flying capacitors per phase. Without the need for additional circuitry, the used capacitors are capable of self-balancing. The suggested inverters are made up of lower rating switches because of the decreased voltage and current stress. The switches in the architecture are controlled by the level shifted phase disposition pulse width modulation technology (LSPD-PWM). A theoretical study is provided for choosing passive components and assessing power loss. It is also tested under low and high switching frequency also in dynamic condition like modulation index change simulated using the MATLAB/Simulink software.

title: Computational Fluid Dynamics Driven Sensor Optimization for Early Hydrogen Leak Detection Enhancing FCEV Safety
authors: J Nithish, NavinKumar TM, C. Bharatiraja
publish: 2025 IEEE 4th International Conference on Smart Technologies for Power, Energy and Control (STPEC)
abstract: Fuel Cell Electric Vehicles offer sustainable options of transport because it does not have any tailpipe emissions. There is the issue of the flammability of hydrogen, which is very high. The leakage of hydrogen in vehicles is a serious setback that has to be rectified and identified. The traditional methods, like sensors of the hydrogen concentration, the bubble test, and acoustic monitoring, lack the knowledge regarding the dispersion of escaping gas. Computational Fluid Dynamics (CFD) simulations will be used in this work to study cases of hydrogen leakages and the best positions to place sensors to give the best outcomes. With the help of CFD modeling, it becomes much easier to understand the dynamics of the movement of the gas, its distribution in different conditions, and detection time, and by using this tool, it is possible to increase the safety of FCEVs. Simulation results show that the leakage can be identified within short simulation times as <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$503 ~\text{ms}, 500 ~\text{ms}, 550 ~\text{ms}$</tex>, and 600 ms, in particular situations. The reaction time of the sensors can be greatly reduced by applying this methodology. Locating the sensors focused with CFD will cover all the possible areas of leakage. This preventive safety strategy will give greater confidence in the use of hydrogen transportation in commerce and widespread usage.

title: Design and Control of a Self-Balanced Quadruple Boost 17-Level Switched Capacitor-Based Inverter
authors: Abdul Nadeem, Yogesh V. Hote
publish: 2025 IEEE 4th International Conference on Smart Technologies for Power, Energy and Control (STPEC)
abstract: A switched capacitor (SC) multilevel inverter produces high-quality, pseudo-sinusoidal stepped voltage waveforms with reduced total harmonic distortion (THD). It offers substantial voltage amplification capabilities, rendering it suitable for a variety of power conversion applications, including industrial systems and renewable energy integration. Multiple voltage levels are produced from a single DC source through the use of capacitors and switching mechanisms, thereby aiding in the reduction of overall system cost and size. A singlephase switched-capacitor (SC) inverter capable of producing seventeen voltage levels is introduced. The topology operates with one DC input source, eleven unidirectional switches, and two bidirectional switches, enabling a quadruple-boost output voltage. The proposed converter utilises parallel charging and series discharging techniques for capacitors, in conjunction with a DC voltage source, to achieve an output voltage four times the input voltage. A modulation strategy based on nearest-level control is adopted to keep the capacitor voltages balanced even when the load varies. Furthermore, the inverter is compared with existing MLI topologies, demonstrating its superior performance. The feasibility and effectiveness of this inverter are verified through MATLAB simulations under static resistive loads, inductive loads, dynamic load conditions, and different modulation index values.

title: A Compact 75–105 GHz Symmetric-Cell-Based Distributed Attenuator With Broadband Phase Compensation
authors: Yang Liu, Nengxu Zhu, Jinxin Li, Xuan Li, Zhifu Hu, Meilin He, Keyuan Chen, Fanyi Meng
publish: IEEE Microwave and Wireless Technology Letters
abstract: This letter introduces a compact distributed attenuator with low phase error, employing a novel second-order phase correction (SOPC) technique. In each attenuation unit, two switch transistors are symmetrically distributed at both ends of the transmission line to form a second-order network, effectively compensating phase deviations without extra area overhead. The analysis and design of the attenuator are described. The proposed 5-bit attenuator is fabricated in a 130-nm SiGe BiCMOS process. Measurement results demonstrate that over 75–105 GHz, the attenuator achieves an insertion loss of 3.1–4.6 dB and a 12.8 dB tuning range with 0.4 dB step size. The rms phase error is <2° from 75 to 95 GHz, with a maximum value of 3.2° across the entire frequency band, while the compact device area is only 0.08 mm<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup>.

title: Topology-Preserving Deep Hashing for Ultrafast Drone-Dominated Object Detection
authors: Luming Zhang, Guifeng Wang, Zhiming Wang, Ling Shao
publish: IEEE Transactions on Neural Networks and Learning Systems
abstract: Drone (or unmanned aerial vehicle) has been extensively applied in many modern artificial intelligence systems in the past decade. In this work, we propose a novel deep hashing framework that can detect objects from drone-captured pictures extremely fast. Our method can intrinsically and flexibly encode various topological structures from each target object, based on which multiscale objects can be discovered in a view- and altitude-invariant way. Moreover, by leveraging <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$l_{F}$</tex-math> </inline-formula> and <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$l_{1}$</tex-math> </inline-formula> norms collaboratively, the calculated hash codes are robust to low-quality drone pictures and possibly contaminated semantic labels. More specifically, for each drone picture, we extract visually/semantically salient object parts inside it. To characterize their topological structure, we construct a graphlet by linking the spatially adjacent object patches into a small graph. Subsequently, a binary matrix factorization (MF) is designed to hierarchically exploit the semantics of these graphlets, wherein three attributes: 1) deep binary hash codes learning; 2) contaminated pictures/labels denoising; and 3) adaptive data graph updating are seamlessly incorporated. Accordingly, a manifold-regularized feature selector is adopted to further obtain more discriminative deep hash codes. Finally, the selected hash codes corresponding to graphlets within each drone photograph are utilized for ranking-based object discovery. Comprehensive experiments on the DAC-SDC, MOHR, and our self-compiled dataset have demonstrated the competitive speed and accuracy of our method.

title: A Spatial-Spectral Component Representation Network for Fine-Grained Mineral Classification in Hyperspectral Imagery
authors: Zhaoyu Wang, Mingming Xu, Shanwei Liu, Hongxia Zheng, Muhammad Yasir, Qinlong Tong
publish: IEEE Journal of Selected Topics in Applied Earth Observations and Remote Sensing
abstract: Mineral mapping is a significant focus in remote sensing research, which aims to fully utilize spectral data for identifying and describing the composition of surface minerals. To gain a deeper understanding of mineral distribution within the study area, fine-grained hyperspectral image classification (HSIC) has emerged as an important research challenge. In mineral scenes where mineral co-occurrence and severe spectral mixing are prevalent, variations in mineral abundances largely govern discriminability, making abundance information essential for improving the reliability of mineral identification. Motivated by these considerations, we develop a spatial-spectral component representation network for HSIC, called SSCRNet, which introduces an Autoencoder (AE)-structured unmixing network to achieve complementary integration between abundance features and deep features extracted by the network. The SSCRNet enhances deep classification networks by introducing convolutional layers with different dilation rates, which enables the simultaneous capture of fine-grained mineral texture details and large-scale geological structural patterns in mineral areas. This design effectively improves the classification performance for complex surface types. Experimental results demonstrate that SSCRNet outperforms existing state-of-the-art approaches in classification performance and robustness for mineral mapping scenarios. The code and datasets will be available at <uri xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">https://github.com/ZhaoYGeo/SSCRNet</uri>.

title: PSD Estimation Based Enhanced Kalman Filter for Target Tracking With Singer Acceleration Noise
authors: Xiaodi Zhou, Jiaolong Wang, Chengxi Zhang, Choon Ki Ahn
publish: IEEE Signal Processing Letters
abstract: This study proposes a new adaptive Kalman filter for high-precision cooperative target tracking based on the Singer acceleration model. In cooperative scenarios, the maneuver time constant <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\alpha$</tex-math></inline-formula> is known in advance through mission planning or communication between targets, allowing a physically consistent and structure-preserving noise modeling. Traditional approaches often necessitate the estimation of the full noise covariance matrix, which can be computationally intensive and prone to inaccuracies and spurious state correlations. To overcome this limitation, this work elaborates a novel online power spectral density (PSD) estimation scheme. Using known <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\alpha$</tex-math></inline-formula> to reduce the number of unknown variables, the new approach can improve the accuracy and reliability of the estimation. Numerical experiments in cooperative target tracking demonstrate that the refined algorithm achieves robust adaptability to dynamic noise that varies over time, provides high-precision state estimates, and maintains low computational complexity.

title: SFKH-GC: Harnessing the Power of Full-Neighborhoods Information for Large-Scale Attributed-Graph Learning_supp1-3689771.pdf
authors: Wenxuan Tu
publish: N/A
abstract: With the increasing complexity of data in various fields, large-scale graph learning draws significant attention due to its importance in understanding complex relationships and structures. Nevertheless, it faces two major challenges: (1) expensive computational costs caused by frequent message-passing (MP), and (2) limited expressive capacity due to over-smoothing. Most existing methods address the first challenge by employing node sampling techniques to decrease computational costs, which can lead to an incomplete representation of the graph structure, thereby diminishing overall performance. While decoupled Graph Convolution Networks (GCNs) effectively handle the over-smoothing problem, they still fail to solve the computational cost challenge. In this paper, we re-examine the design of the decoupled GCN from a theoretical perspective and reveal that its ability to alleviate over-smoothing largely arises from capturing richer multi-hop neighborhood information, rather than from the decoupled design, which is the prevailing consensus. Motivated by this finding, we propose a Scalable Full-Neighborhoods K-hop Graph Convolution (SFKH-GC) to simultaneously address the aforementioned challenges. The core idea is to allocate separate memory space for the neighborhood representation obtained in each MP iteration. Specifically, by performing K steps of MP on the original feature matrix, the multi-hop neighborhood information is concatenated, providing rich input for subsequent feature transformation (FT). This process is designed to be independent of the training phase, significantly reducing the number of MP operations and preventing the creation of complex integral graphs during forward and backward propagation. Given the absence of MP operations during training, this method not only permits mini-batch learning but also facilitates the flexible application of common visual or sequential models (rather than graph models). Extensive experiments on seven benchmark datasets demonstrate that SFKH-GC achieves state-of-the-art performance, with up to +7.68% ACC improvement on Cora and +4.02% on Computer compared to the best existing methods, while substantially reducing memory usage and training time. Both theoretical and empirical results validate the efficiency, scalability, and performance of the proposed method.

title: PT-Mark: Invisible Watermarking for Text-to-image Diffusion Models via Semantic-aware Pivotal Tuning
authors: Yaopeng Wang, Huiyu Xu, Zhibo Wang, Jiacheng Du, Zhichao Li, Yiming Li, Qiu Wang, Kui Ren
publish: IEEE Transactions on Dependable and Secure Computing
abstract: Watermarking for diffusion images has drawn considerable attention due to the widespread use of text-to-image diffusion models and the increasing need for their copyright protection. Recently, advanced watermarking techniques, such as Tree-Ring, integrate watermarks by embedding traceable patterns (e.g., Rings) into the latent distribution during the diffusion process. Such methods disrupt the original semantics of the generated images due to the inevitable distribution shift caused by the watermarks, thereby limiting their practicality, particularly in digital art creation. In this work, we present Semantic-aware Pivotal Tuning Watermarks (PT-Mark), a novel invisible watermarking method that preserves both the semantics of diffusion images and the traceability of the watermark. PT-Mark preserves the original semantics of the watermarked image by gradually aligning the generation trajectory with the original (pivotal) trajectory while maintaining the traceable watermarks during whole diffusion denoising process. To achieve this, we first compute the salient regions of the watermark at each diffusion denoising step as a spatial prior to identify areas that can be aligned without disrupting the watermark pattern. Guided by the region, we then introduce an additional pivotal tuning branch that optimizes the null-text embedding to align the semantics while preserving the watermarks. Extensive evaluations demonstrate that PT-Mark can preserve the original semantics of the diffusion images while integrating robust watermarks. It achieves a 10% improvement in the performance of semantic preservation compared to state-of-the-art watermarking methods, while also showing comparable robustness against real-world perturbations and four times greater efficiency. The code is available at <uri xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">https://github.com/annpion/PT-Mark</uri>.

title: Low-Voltage Distribution Network Line Loss Rate Prediction Based on Gradient Boosting Decision Trees
authors: Tian Wen, Kai Cuiying, Liu Huiying, Hu Jingyu
publish: IEEE Access
abstract: In the field of line loss prediction for low-voltage distribution networks, conventional models are largely constrained by static feature analysis frameworks, which limits their ability to capture the combined effects of dynamic load fluctuations and topological heterogeneity on line losses. To address this limitation, this study proposes a Distributed estimation of the fusion of Bagging in Gradient Boosting Decision Tree prediction (DB-GBDT) model, which integrates dynamic feature extraction with distributed estimation. First, a high-dimensional hybrid feature space is constructed by temporally aligning static line parameters with dynamic operational data and performing feature interaction, resulting in a feature matrix that explicitly incorporates spatiotemporal coupling information. Next, distribution areas are adaptively grouped based on feature similarity, and lightweight GBDT sub-models are independently trained within each group. Global prediction is then achieved through dynamic weighted aggregation of the sub-model outputs. Experimental results on the LVDN-LLD and PGLP-Bench datasets demonstrate that the proposed model achieves an AUC of 0.965 (95% CI: 0.958–0.972), representing a 4.4% improvement over standard GBDT. The F1-score reaches 92.3% under dynamic operating conditions, while the inference speed is 21% faster than that of LightGBM. Compared with traditional methods such as SVM, the proposed approach reduces generalization error by 62%, significantly enhancing prediction stability under complex network topologies.

title: Robust Brain Tumor Segmentation with Incomplete MRI Modalities Using Hölder Divergence and Mutual Information-Enhanced Knowledge Transfer
authors: Runze Cheng, Xihang Qiu, Ming Li, Ye Zhang, Fei Richard Yu, Chun Li
publish: IEEE/CAA Journal of Automatica Sinica
abstract: Multimodal MRI (magnetic resonance imaging) provides critical complementary information for accurate brain tumor segmentation. However, conventional methods struggle when certain modalities are missing due to issues like image quality, protocol inconsistencies, patient allergies, or financial constraints. To address this, we propose a robust single-modality parallel processing framework that achieves high segmentation accuracy even with incomplete modalities. Leveraging Hölder divergence and mutual information, our model maintains modality-specific features while dynamically adjusting network parameters based on available inputs. By using these divergence and information-based loss functions, the framework effectively quantifies discrepancies between predictions and ground-truth labels, resulting in consistently accurate segmentation. Extensive evaluations on the BraTS 2018 and BraTS 2020 datasets demonstrate superior performance over existing methods in handling missing modalities, with ablation studies validating each component's contribution to the framework.

title: Regulation of Magnetic Properties in Fe-based Composite Ribbons by External Magnetic Field Coating
authors: Z.L. Ye, H.R. Dong, M.Q. Li, Z.J. Zhang, J.R. Wang, X. Li, Z.J. Zhao
publish: IEEE Transactions on Magnetics
abstract: In this study, Fe<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">73.5</sub>Cu<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">1</sub>Nb<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">3</sub>Si<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">13.5</sub>B<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">9</sub> amorphous ribbons were used as substrates to fabricate FINEMET/Fe₅₀Ni₅₀ composite ribbons using three deposition techniques: conventional magnetron sputtering (CMS), transverse magnetic field-assisted sputtering (TMF) and perpendicular magnetic field-assisted sputtering (PMF). We aimed to clarify the mechanism how the orientation of an external magnetic field during deposition regulated surface morphology, magnetic hysteresis behavior, and the giant magnetoimpedance (GMI) effect of the resulting composite ribbons. The results demonstrated that magnetic field-assisted deposition promoted the directional alignment of magnetic particles and significantly enhanced the magnetic properties of the films. TMF facilitated the formation of a well-ordered transverse magnetic domain structure, increasing the maximum GMI ratio to 55.57%, approximately 15 % higher than the 40.50% obtained with CMS. Meanwhile, PMF yielded a more refined magnetic domain structure, resulting in the lowest coercivity among the three methods and a maximum GMI ratio of 62.25%, which substantially improved both soft magnetic characteristics and magnetic-field response speed. The magnetic field-assisted sputtering strategy proposed in this work enabled active control of magnetic domain orientation during the film preparation process, effectively overcoming the limitations of post-deposition treatments. This approach offered a promising pathway for developing high-performance GMI composite materials with great potential in weak magnetic field detection and information storage application.

title: Relative Intensity Noise of Datacom Multimode VCSELs with Different Aperture Shapes
authors: Bedouin A. Sassiya, Michał Wasiak, Tomasz Czyszanowski, Rainer Michalzik
publish: IEEE Journal of Quantum Electronics
abstract: Relative intensity noise (RIN) is a key performance-limiting factor in multimode vertical-cavity surface-emitting lasers (VCSELs), particularly for high-speed data communication applications. This work investigates the influence of oxide aperture geometry on RIN behavior, combining experimental measurements and full-vectorial optical simulations. Frequency-resolved RIN (RINf) and optical spectra were analyzed across varying injection currents and temperatures for devices with circular and elliptical apertures. The results show that circular apertures exhibit increased RIN due to mode beating among quasi-degenerate transverse modes. Introducing rotational asymmetry in the aperture effectively increases the mode spacing, thereby reducing RIN. However, residual noise peaks were observed, attributed to four-wave mixing (FWM) interactions among separated modes. The experimental findings were strongly supported by 3D optical simulations using the PLaSK solver, confirming the impact of aperture-induced mode control on noise suppression. This study provides valuable insight into VCSEL design optimization for reduced RIN and improved high-speed performance.

title: Optimized Ferrite Design for Wireless Charging of Electric Vehicles a Cost-Efficient Approach
authors: Ragul R, R. Nakkeeran, C. Bharatiraja
publish: 2025 IEEE 4th International Conference on Smart Technologies for Power, Energy and Control (STPEC)
abstract: The Wireless Power Transfer (WPT) technology is gaining traction across various applications, from electric vehicles, owing to its inherent reliability, efficiency, and flexibility. Ferrite materials are integral in WPT systems, enhancing magnetic coupling, suppressing electromagnetic interference, and preventing magnetic energy leakage, thereby boosting charging efficiency. This paper explores different arrangements of ferrite materials within WPT systems to optimize performance and considers their cost-effectiveness. Ferrites, characterized by their high-frequency performance, high magnetic permeability, and electrical conductivity, are crucial for channeling magnetic flux, minimizing interference, and reducing eddy current losses. Various arrangements of ferrite can significantly improve the inductions and increase power transfer. Simulation results indicate that strategic ferrite arrangements increase flux through the coil, thereby improving coupling at most orientations. The cost-efficiency of using ferrites is demonstrated through reduced power losses, improved system performance, and enhanced shielding effectiveness. However, precautions must be taken to mitigate mechanical stress, shock, and high-temperature effects on ferrites. By carefully selecting and arranging ferrite materials, cost-effective WPT systems can be achieved with improved efficiency and enhanced power transfer capabilities.

title: Modeling, Control, and Performance Analysis of a Five-Phase Induction Motor with SPWM
authors: Ayantika Guria, Arka Dutta, Kartick Chandra Jana
publish: 2025 IEEE 4th International Conference on Smart Technologies for Power, Energy and Control (STPEC)
abstract: Five-phase induction motors (IMs) are attracting increasing attention in modern drive systems due to their inherent fault tolerance, reduced torque ripple, and improved efficiency compared to conventional three-phase machines. This work presents the complete modeling and simulation of a five-phase IM drive in MATLAB/Simulink. The drive is supplied by a five-phase two-level voltage source inverter (VSI) controlled via sinusoidal pulse-width modulation (SPWM), ensuring balanced output voltages but resulting in stator currents dominated by the 3rd and 7th harmonics. The motor model incorporates detailed electrical and mechanical parameters obtained from experimental characterization tests, enabling realistic performance evaluation. The system is analyzed under various load conditions, with simulation results demonstrating accurate speed tracking and smooth torque response despite the harmonic content. These results highlight the potential of five-phase IM drives for high-reliability and high-performance applications, including electric propulsion, aerospace systems, and advanced industrial machinery.

title: Optimal EPS Control for Minimum Backflow Power and Current Stress for Dual Active Bridge Converter
authors: Jahangeer Ahmad Dar, M. K. Pathak
publish: 2025 IEEE 4th International Conference on Smart Technologies for Power, Energy and Control (STPEC)
abstract: When a dual-active bridge converter (DAB) is operated with single-phase shift modulation (SPS), the converter suffers from high backflow power and current stress. These issues arise, especially during low power operation or wide voltage ratios, leading to losses in the converter and thereby decreasing its efficiency. To simultaneously minimize both backflow power and current stress, the proposed paper introduces the optimal EPS modulation. In this paper, both modes of EPS are discussed, and the optimal combination of phase shifts is determined. The optimal combination is obtained using the method of Lagrange multipliers. Lastly, a hardware prototype is developed to validate the proposed algorithm.

title: Techno-Economic Feasibility Study of a Rooftop Solar PV System for EV Charging at University Campus Building
authors: Krishna Kumar Saini, Diptanjan Sengupta, HD Mathur
publish: 2025 IEEE 4th International Conference on Smart Technologies for Power, Energy and Control (STPEC)
abstract: With India's rapidly evolving electric vehicle (EV) ecosystem and increased adoption in academic campuses, deploying clean energy infrastructure is essential. This paper presents an extended techno-economic analysis of a grid-tied rooftop solar photovoltaic (PV) system with battery backup at a hostel building on a university campus. The system is designed exclusively to cater to EV charging loads through solar energy. PVsyst 8.2 was used for simulation, and a 16.2 kWp PV array, 16.2 kWh battery, and a 20 kW inverter setup were modelled. Simulation outputs include annual energy generation, performance ratio, economic metrics (LCOE), and carbon savings. The study also references broader campus energy sustainability objectives and carbon neutrality goals.

title: Real-Time Lane Assist System Using Computer Vision with Adaptive PID Control
authors: Devadharshan D, Hrithick E, S Shyam Ganesh, Dhanvin S, Karthika R
publish: 2025 IEEE 4th International Conference on Smart Technologies for Power, Energy and Control (STPEC)
abstract: With the growing need for Advanced Driver Assistance Systems (ADAS), this work proposes a real-time lane assist system based on classical computer vision approaches combined with an adaptive Proportional Integral Derivative (PID)controller. Contrary to deep-learning-based lane detection, this system employs edge detection and Hough Transform to detect lanes, followed by real-time lane deviation calculation. One new contribution of this research which makes it novel is introducing a lane confidence value to adaptively adjust PID control parameters in real time while driving. This provides smoother and more stable steering control while, there is a change in road conditions. The system was evaluated in the CARLA simulator (Town10) and operated with steady lane recovery and low-latency visual feedback, thus having potential for embedded real-time applications.

title: Enhanced Two-Port Model-Based Study and CCM/DCM Boundary Condition for LCC-LCC IPT Topology
authors: Jalaj Kumar, Suvendu Samanta
publish: 2025 IEEE 4th International Conference on Smart Technologies for Power, Energy and Control (STPEC)
abstract: Inductive power transfer (IPT) converters are attracting significant attention. The design of the IPT converter is generally done employing the first harmonic (FH) approximation due to the formation of the resonant tank network. However, under certain operating conditions, the harmonics in the converter become more pronounced, especially in the discontinuous conduction mode (DCM). As a result, the FH approximation leads to high inaccuracy in these operating conditions. The performance of the converter in the above-mentioned operating conditions is insufficiently addressed in the literature. This paper uses an enhanced two-port model-based study, which accurately examines the LCC-LCC IPT converter in DCM. The CCM/DCM boundary condition for the IPT topology is reported. The mathematical approach is synthesized so that the actual and estimated output voltage and current have an error below 2 %. A hardware prototype of the topology is built. The experimental results validate the proposed analytical method.

title: Optimal Reactive Power Dispatch Using Incomprehensible but Intelligible-in-Time Logics
authors: Rahul Kumar, S. Sivasubramani
publish: 2025 IEEE 4th International Conference on Smart Technologies for Power, Energy and Control (STPEC)
abstract: Efficient management of power flow is crucial for the effective operation of power systems, aiming to enhance safety, reliability, and efficiency while adhering to operational constraints. This study specifically focuses on improving the coordination of reactive power to achieve energy efficiency and voltage consistency. The research employs two key computational environments: network-related simulations and eigen value based small-signal stability analysis are performed on the DIgSILENT PowerFactory platform, while heuristic optimization tasks are executed in MATLAB using parallel processing techniques. To address the challenge of optimal reactive power dispatch (ORPD), a novel metaheuristic, the Incomprehensible but Intelligible-intime (IBI) logics algorithm, is utilized for multi-objective reactive power optimization (RPO). The effectiveness of the approach is validated on the IEEE 39-bus New England system, where the proposed algorithm is benchmarked against the sine cosine algorithm (SCA) and particle swarm optimization (PSO) algorithm. Comparative results demonstrate that the proposed method not only improves computational efficiency and robustness but also enhances dynamic stability, as evidenced by eigen value analysis of system modes under different dispatch strategies.

title: Current Mismatch-Based Newton-Raphson Power Flow Algorithm for Autotransformer Fed Traction Power Supply System
authors: Pradyumna Pradhan, Biswarup Das
publish: 2025 IEEE 4th International Conference on Smart Technologies for Power, Energy and Control (STPEC)
abstract: Power flow analysis plays a vital role in the planning, operation, and performance evaluation of traction power supply systems (TPSS). However, the unique topology of autotransformer (AT)-fed traction networks presents modelling and computational challenges compared to conventional transmission systems. This paper proposes a current mismatch-based Newton-Raphson (NR) power flow algorithm for AT-fed TPSS, in which the power flow equations are reformulated in terms of current mismatches. The proposed power flow algorithm's accuracy is validated through case studies on representative AT-fed TPSS configurations, with results compared against equivalent MATLAB SIMULINK models. The close agreement between the two confirms the validity of the proposed approach.

title: Eddy Current Sensor Based Smart Control System for Optimizing Industrial Zinc Coating Process Using Adaptive Machine Learning and IIoT
authors: Tarun K. Das, Chinmay Bera, Chandan Dutta, Prem K. Das, Nitish K. Bhakat, Kedar Bhave, Sarmishtha P. Sagar
publish: 2025 IEEE 4th International Conference on Smart Technologies for Power, Energy and Control (STPEC)
abstract: Smart control systems and sensor technology equipped with advanced Machine Learning (ML) algorithms, automation, and Industrial Internet of Things (IIoT) technologies offer transformative potential for optimizing zinc coating processes in real-time. An integrated eddy current sensor is used for in-situ measurement of thin zinc coating weight (∼ 50–150 GSM) in galvanized iron (GI) steel wires at an industrial facility. However, as manufacturing systems grow more complex, zinc coating requires precise control of process parameters such as coating weight, and line speed to ensure optimal quality and efficiency. The present study addresses an industrially relevant problem by developing an advance sensor technology that integrates eddy current sensing, ML-driven automation and IIoT-enabled systems to promote sustainable smart manufacturing. It showcases the successful integration of smart monitoring and control systems into harsh industrial environments, facilitating enhanced optimization of galvanization processes. It uses an adaptive PID controller based on reinforcement learning to assess dynamic response, adaptability, and robustness. In this work, a single RBF neural network has been realized to improve learning efficiency. It approximated both the Actor's policy function and the Critic's value function simultaneously. The inputs of this network are the system error and its first and second-order differences. The simulation results demonstrate that the proposed controller is highly efficient for dynamic and complex nonlinear systems. It exhibits excellent adaptability and robustness, against conventional PID controllers. In line with NDE 4.0 and IIoT, the sensor facilitates precise, real-time assessment of material integrity and coating quality. This research highlights the advances in sensor technology by integrating it into harsh industrial environments, promoting smart manufacturing and optimize industrial galvanization processes.

title: A Resilient NN Assisted Switching Control for Networked Control Systems Under DoS Attacks
authors: Manmahendra Singh Daksh, Puneet Mishra
publish: 2025 IEEE 4th International Conference on Smart Technologies for Power, Energy and Control (STPEC)
abstract: Denial-of-Service (DoS) attack is one of the most common types of cyberattacks which can severely impact the control loop performance. In this work, a DoS attack on a nonlinear process control loop is investigated, and a probabilistic packet loss scenario has been simulated, affecting the feedback path of the control loop. To enhance the Networked Control System (NCS) resilience towards DoS attacks, this work proposes a Resilient NN Assisted Switching Control (RNASC) framework. The RNASC framework consists of an online Neural Network (NN) estimator to mimic the plant dynamics, along with a conventional Proportional Integral (PI) controller. A notable feature of the proposed control scheme is its switching capability from the feedback signal to the estimated plant output from the neural network. Multiple packet loss probabilities (<tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\rho$</tex>) have been considered to mimic the severity scenarios of the DoS attack, and the performance of the proposed controller is assessed with Monte Carlo simulations for setpoint tracking, robustness, and plant operation with measurement noise. Through extensive simulation studies, it was found that the proposed RNASC framework provides 90% lesser integral of absolute error (IAE) compared to the existing Neural Network Assisted PI controller (NAPIC) for the worst case scenario of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\rho=0.90$</tex>, which validates the effectiveness of the proposed RNASC in mitigating the effects of DoS attack on an NCS.

title: Control-Oriented Design of a Standalone Pv-Battery-Inverter System with Dynamic Load
authors: Pranati Rani Purohit, Shaswat Chandra Mishra, Arnab Ghosh, Pravat Kumar Ray
publish: 2025 IEEE 4th International Conference on Smart Technologies for Power, Energy and Control (STPEC)
abstract: Voltage stability is a major difficulty in photovoltaicbased power systems, which must deal with variable solar input and dynamic load profiles. This paper presents a simulation of a PV-battery system that employed a Model Predictive manage (MPC) approach to precisely manage the DC bus voltage. A boost converter is connected to a photovoltaic (PV) array, and an algorithm called Maximum Power Point Tracking (MPPT) controls it. To talk to a battery system, you need a non-isolated bidirectional converter. The proposed MPC algorithm dynamically optimizes control actions in response to predictions of system behavior, and it actively manages the bidirectional converter. Regardless of fluctuations in generation and consumption, this ensures that the DC bus voltage stays constant at 48 V. A series of tests are performed on the system to evaluate its functionality under realworld scenarios. These tests include nonlinear and time-varying loads, among others. The MPC method outperforms the conventional PID-based control in terms of stability, handling system limitations more effectively, and dynamic reaction speed. The system is completed with an inverter that supplies a single-phase AC load and is connected to the DC bus. The simulation results demonstrate that the proposed control architecture accomplishes both effective power sharing and consistent voltage regulation.

title: Federated Multi-Stage Market Clearing with Integrated Probabilistic Forecasting for Decentralized Energy Systems
authors: Surya Karan Singh, Amit Kumar Singh
publish: 2025 IEEE 4th International Conference on Smart Technologies for Power, Energy and Control (STPEC)
abstract: The ongoing decentralization of electricity markets caused by distributed energy resources (DERs), renewable variability and multi-sided participation highlights the shortcomings of the traditional centralized market design. To tackle this challenge, we propose a federated six-stage multi-sided market clearing framework that closes the loops between forecasting, stochastic bidding, intra-day re-dispatch, real-time balancing, congestion and loss reconciliation and settlement. The FL framework is integrated with the state-of-the-art probabilistic forecasting models: DeepAR, PatchTST and N-BEATS. Market clearing integrates stochastic-robust optimization and conditional value-at-risk techniques for economic, operational and fairness efficiency. By continuously linking these forecasting-optimization-settlement feedback loops, the model enables autonomous flexibility allocation, improved price stability and reduced imbalance costs. Simulations on synthetic NREL-based data show an approximate <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{2 6. 3 \%}$</tex> reduction in imbalance costs, lower price volatility and a load RMSE of 0.84 MW, surpassing the centralized and two-stage benchmarks. The method is scalable, privacy-preserving and mathematically rigorous, thereby providing a solid foundation for the next generation of decentralized markets for electricity.

title: Short-Term Electricity Price Forecasting Using RFECV-Based Feature Selection and GA-Optimized BiLSTM
authors: Ashish Prajesh, Prerna Jain
publish: 2025 IEEE 4th International Conference on Smart Technologies for Power, Energy and Control (STPEC)
abstract: Accurate electricity price forecasting has become increasingly important in deregulated power markets, where generators, retailers, and other market participants rely on precise price estimates for bidding strategies and risk management. However, electricity prices exhibit strong volatility, seasonality, and complex nonlinear interactions with demand, generation mix, fuel costs, and other exogenous factors. These characteristics limit the effectiveness of conventional statistical approaches for time-series modeling. To solve these issues we have put forth a deep learning based forecasting framework which is built on a Bidirectional Long Term Memory (BiLSTM) network which is able to capture past and also future temporal relationships in sequential data. Also we report that we used a Genetic Algorithm for hyperparameter tuning of the BiLSTM model which in turn enables us to systemically explore the search space and identify very good configures. Also we used Recursive Feature Elimination with Cross Validation (RFECV) to choose the most relevant input variables which in turn reduced redundancy, improved generalization performance and also we saw a reduction in overfitting. The proposed framework is evaluated through comprehensive experiments and benchmarked against several established forecasting models. Comparative results demonstrate its robustness and competitive accuracy in short-term electricity price prediction.

authors: N/A

title: An AI-Assisted Prompt Generation Method for Theatre Rehearsal: Translating Script Text into Executable Stage Actions
authors: Tingting Zhao
