title: Data-Driven Digital Twin for Real-Time Condition and Stability Monitoring of a PV-Integrated Boost Converter System Using ED-LSTM-A
authors: Naresh Kumar Kumawat, Nishant Kumar
publish: 2025 IEEE 4th International Conference on Smart Technologies for Power, Energy and Control (STPEC)
abstract: This paper proposes a novel data-driven Digital Twin (DT) framework for real-time condition and stability monitoring of a PhotoVoltaic Integrated Boost Converter (PVIBC) system using an Encoder-Decoder Long Short-Term Memory with Attention (ED-LSTM-A) network. Traditional model-based approaches require detailed circuit knowledge and often struggle to adapt to nonlinearities under varying environmental and loading conditions. In contrast, the proposed digital twin is constructed from operational data, enabling adaptive modeling and prediction without extensive analytical derivations. The proposed novel ED-LSTM-A network is trained on time-series data collected under a wide range of irradiance and load variations, allowing it to capture complex temporal dynamics of the PVIBC. In real time, the proposed ED-LSTM-A based DT operates in parallel with the physical system on an FPGA platform, continuously predicting output voltage and current behavior. By comparing predicted responses with actual measurements, early indications of faults, degradation, or deviations from nominal operating conditions can be detected. Furthermore, the overall stability of the PVIBC system is assessed using a Lyapunov-based criterion to ensure reliable operation under dynamic conditions. Experimental results confirm that the proposed approach achieves accurate real-time prediction with minimal latency.

title: An Overlap Timing-Based Control Scheme for Active Current Source Gate Driver
authors: Ashish Wanode, Satish Belkhode
publish: 2025 IEEE 4th International Conference on Smart Technologies for Power, Energy and Control (STPEC)
abstract: The widespread acceptance of wide-bandgap (WBG) devices such as Silicon Carbide (SiC) MOSFETs has imposed stringent demands on the gate drivers. Conventional voltage source gate drivers (VSGDs), though commonly used, suffer in terms of peak current demand, gate driver losses, poor control over the switching dynamics causing electromagnetic interference (EMI), and higher switching losses. This paper explores an interesting alternative as a current source gate driver (CSGD) with a proposed overlap timing (OVT)-based control scheme. The proposed scheme aims to achieve precise control over the gate signal by introducing an adjustable overlap period to control the gate signal, allowing controlled peak gate current during switching transients. In this paper, initially, a method to evenly compare the considered drivers is introduced. This method is then utilized to evenly compare the performance of the considered CSGD with the proposed OVT-based control scheme against the VSGD. Further, detailed investigation is performed to evaluate the influence of overlap timing on key performance indicators such as rise/fall time, peak gate current, gate Charge (Q) requirement, and switching energy. The observations indicate improved switching timings and losses with the CSGD. A design procedure for the CSGD prototype has been presented with the key waveforms.

title: Optimal Sizing of PV, Wind Power, and Battery Storage for Coordinated EV Charging Stations with Distribution System
authors: Digambar Singh Govind, Rashika Agrawal, Khushi Meena, Raghav Ginodia, Chirag Agarwal, Manoj Fozdar
publish: 2025 IEEE 4th International Conference on Smart Technologies for Power, Energy and Control (STPEC)
abstract: This study presents a system-integrated framework for the optimal siting and operation of Electric Vehicle Charging Stations (EVCS), aligning with transportation Electric Vehicles (EVs) charging demand with the operational constraints of the IEEE 69-bus distribution network. By mapping traffic nodes to distribution buses, the framework ensures grid-compatible and user-centric EVCS placement. The methodology incorporates load flow analysis, voltage stability assessment, and spatial accessibility to balance charging infrastructure with network performance. Renewable Energy Sources (RES), including solar and wind, are strategically integrated at candidate locations, supported by Battery Energy Storage Systems (BESS) to mitigate intermittency and support peak demand. A hybrid energy management strategy dynamically allocates power among the grid, RES, and BESS, enhancing system resilience and reducing environmental impact. The proposed approach provides a scalable and sustainable solution for EV infrastructure planning, supporting both mobility and energy transition objectives.

title: Adaptive Field Weakening-Based Advanced DTC for IPMSM Drives with MTPA and Adaptive Flux Hysteresis Control Under Voltage-Constrained Wide-Speed Operation
authors: Arnab Chatterjee, Avik Bhattacharya
publish: 2025 IEEE 4th International Conference on Smart Technologies for Power, Energy and Control (STPEC)
abstract: This paper presents an advanced Direct Torque Control (DTC) strategy for Interior Permanent Magnet Synchronous Motor (IPMSM) drives incorporating Maximum Torque Per Ampere (MTPA), Adaptive Field Weakening Control (AFWC) and Adaptive Flux Hysteresis Control (AFHC). The proposed scheme operates with an adaptive algorithm which dynamically regulates the d-axis current under voltage constraints and improves the dynamic torque response as well as the flux regulation for a wide-speed range. The improved flux sector division and the proposed AFHC result in comparatively smoother transitions and reduced torque ripple. The effectiveness of the proposed scheme has been verified by simulation results, which indicate that it performs superior to classical DTC in terms of dynamic behaviour, efficiency and operational range extension.

title: Power Factor Correction by Phase Angle Regulation and Mitigation of Voltage Fluctuations Using MLI Based DVR
authors: Renuka Dhote, R. J. Satputaley, S. Manmadha Rao
publish: 2025 IEEE 4th International Conference on Smart Technologies for Power, Energy and Control (STPEC)
abstract: This paper focuses on enhancing power factor and mitigating voltage fluctuations using a dynamic voltage restorer (DVR) in a three phase system. The DVR model utilizes a three-phase 5-level T-type inverter. A novel control scheme is devised for the DVR to improve the power factor on the source side while simultaneously addressing voltage sag and swell. It's a closed-loop control method that involves regulating the phase angle by injecting voltage at a specific angle relative to the source voltage. The proposed technique is implemented and simulated in MATLAB/Simulink under various conditions, including power factor enhancement with sag, power factor enhancement without sag, and sag/swell mitigation, as well as voltage regulation enhancement. Results indicate that the proposed controller performs satisfactorily in improving power factor while mitigating voltage sag and swell, and enhancing voltage regulation in MATLAB/Simulink.

title: Evaluating Bifacial Perovskite Solar Cell Performance Under Dual-Side Illumination
authors: Mustak Ahmed Ansary, Shweta Pal, Pawan Kumar, Biswajit Mandal, Gaurav Siddharth
publish: 2025 IEEE 4th International Conference on Smart Technologies for Power, Energy and Control (STPEC)
abstract: Bifacial perovskite solar cells represents promising advancement in photovoltaic technology, offering increased power conversion efficiency (PCE) by utilizing both front and rear light absorption. Investigated the effects of absorber thickness and defect density on key performance metrics under front and rear illumination. Results reveal that absorber thickness critically influences charge carrier dynamics, with an optimal thickness of 600 nm maximizing efficiency by balancing light absorption and reducing recombination losses.

title: Optimized Operation of Multi-Carrier Energy Systems with Renewable and Demand Response Uncertainties
authors: Ankit Garg, K. R. Niazi, Md Fahim Ansari, S.S. Sahoo
publish: 2025 IEEE 4th International Conference on Smart Technologies for Power, Energy and Control (STPEC)
abstract: The Energy Hub (EH) framework is a major instrument for modeling and optimizing the running of multicarrier energy systems (MCES). This paper develops an economic load dispatch model for EHs that merges electrical and thermal subsystems. The main goal is to minimize the total system expenses, which comprise the cost of fuel, the penalty for carbon emissions, and the incentive for demand response (DR). A robust optimization method is used to deal with the uncertainties of renewable generation and DR participation. The uncertain problem is converted into a mixed-integer linear programming (MILP) model that can be solved by duality theory and solved by the CPLEX solver. The three scenarios—considering energy storage, DR, and their combinations—are used to assess the proposed framework. The findings demonstrate that the use of storage systems and DR schemes can greatly cut down on operational costs. However, it requires cost savings to be weighed against the uncertainties of variable renewable energy sources and demand-side flexibility.

title: Comparative Analysis of Six Phase Symmetric and Asymmetric Induction Motor Under DTC
authors: Regu Shruthi, Ramsha Karampuri, Jay K. Pandit
publish: 2025 IEEE 4th International Conference on Smart Technologies for Power, Energy and Control (STPEC)
abstract: Six-phase Induction Motors (SPIMs) offer enhanced torque control, improved fault tolerance, and reduced harmonics, making them suitable for modern electric drives. This paper evaluates the performance of Direct Torque Control (DTC) technique on symmetrical and asymmetrical SPIMs using MATLAB/Simulink. Key metrics including torque ripple, flux stability, THD, and speed response are compared under identical conditions. Results deliver a clear comparison of both motor configurations, outlining their strengths and trade-offs for advanced applications.

title: Improving Grid Compliance and Economic Efficiency Using Energy Storage Under DSM Regulation 2024
authors: Ravindra B. Kuhada, Bharat Singh Rajpurohit
publish: 2025 IEEE 4th International Conference on Smart Technologies for Power, Energy and Control (STPEC)
abstract: In India, the central electricity regulatory commission (CERC) introduced a new deviation settlement mechanism (DSM) regulation in 2024 to enhance grid reliability and stability. Under this regulation, grid users, such as distribution companies (DISCOMs), has been incurred deviation charges based on their divergence from the scheduled drawal. These charges are influenced by the grid frequency fluctuations and computed using a normal rate (NR) which is derived from the area-clearing price (ACP). While the regulation aims to maintain grid discipline, deviations frequently arise due to unanticipated occurrences, such as equipment tripping, forecasting inaccuracies, and human error. Hence, to cater for the deviation in the system and minimize the DSM penalties, this paper proposes the integration of a battery energy storage system (BESS) that charges during overdrawal and discharges on underdrawal of the electricity based on real-time frequency fluctuations. This study presents an optimization framework for scheduling the energy storage systems to minimize the DSM charges. Results demonstrate that the proposed model provides both economic efficiency to buyers and grid compliance.

title: Integration of Solar and Wind Power with Virtual Synchronous Generators for Improved Transient Stability and Virtual Inertia Support
authors: Suparna Maity, Sandip Chanda, Alok Kr. Srivastava, Raja Ram Kumar, Abhinandan De
publish: 2025 IEEE 4th International Conference on Smart Technologies for Power, Energy and Control (STPEC)
abstract: Growing use of renewable energy sources as substitutes for traditional generation is motivated by the pressing necessity to respond to environmental issues, improve energy efficiency and lower emissions of greenhouse gases. While integrating a high penetration level of intermittent resources like solar and wind pose great difficulties owing to their absence of natural rotational inertia, which conventionally allows synchronous machines in conventional thermal power plants to provide real-time damping against load oscillations, generation fluctuations, and electrical fault transients. Virtual Synchronous Generators (VSGs) have presented as an encouraging answer. The integration scheme of solar- and winddriven power generation along with VSG control is proposed in this article, establishing its ability to strengthen system resilience and stability during transient states. The suggested method provides a sustainable solution for the development of high-renewable power systems in a reliable and efficient manner. The novelty of the work lies in control strategy developed to extend the range of voltage and frequency stability and thus improving virtual inertia capacity.

title: Cooperative Energy Exchange of a Heterogeneous Prosumer Microgrids for Mutual Economic Energy Share
authors: Durga K Prasad Gudavalli, Krishna Tejus E M, Alivelu Manga Parimi, Hitesh Dutt Mathur, Abdun Naser Mahmood, Naveen Chilamkurti
publish: 2025 IEEE 4th International Conference on Smart Technologies for Power, Energy and Control (STPEC)
abstract: User-defined energy markets, facilitated by a peer-to-peer energy transaction system, present significant economic advantages for prosumer microgrids (PMGs). However, ensuring a consistent energy supply during transaction periods can pose challenges and may exert pressure on Utility Grids (UG). This paper explores a mutual cooperative energy exchange system tailored for two heterogeneous PMGs, accommodating users with diverse energy requirements. The proposed multi-objective system enables bidirectional energy flow between the microgrids, thereby minimizing the necessity for frequent energy transactions. Furthermore, it incorporates an energy optimization controller at each prosumer's end to effectively balance supply and demand. The case studies of offgrid, grid-connected, mutual cooperative energy exchange, and integrated mode of operation are performed on the MATLAB Simulink environment, and the key findings reveal that a significant reduction in daily UG pricing is achieved, with a 78.51% decrease for PMG 1 and 76.47% for PMG 2. As the cooperative energy shares are allocated between the PMGs, our analysis of net energy pricing indicates a +15.38% economic benefit for PMG 2, while PMG 1 experiences a corresponding economic loss of −15.38%. However, it is important to note that this loss will be fully compensated by the complete reduction of dependency on UG for PMG 1.

title: Switched-Capacitor Based Common-Ground Nine-Level Boosting Inverter (SCCGI)
authors: Kaibalya Prasad Panda, Sumant Kumar Dalai, Pratap Sekhar Puhan, Gayadhar Panda
publish: 2025 IEEE 4th International Conference on Smart Technologies for Power, Energy and Control (STPEC)
abstract: A compact switched-capacitor common-grounded nine-level boosting inverter (SCCGI) is proposed in this work. The SCCGI offers a dual boosting output and eliminates the leakage current, useful for single-stage transformer-less photovoltaic applications. The SCCGI employs a single input with only ten switches, two diodes and three naturally balanced capacitors. Three switched-capacitor modules are integrated to obtain voltage boosting along side the common-grounding connection. Smart charging and discharging of the capacitor in each fundamental cycle enables self voltage balancing. A fair comparative analysis with common-grounded structures is carried out to verify the advantages of the proposed SCCGI. To verify the feasibility, the proposed circuit is further simulated and tested experimentally under standalone and grid-connected modes. Results validate the operation of SCCGI under load change, sudden shift in the input voltage and change in the power factor.

title: Evaluation of a $\pm 5 \text{kV}$ Solid-State-Based 200 Ns Duration Pulse Generator
authors: Aashish Ranjan, Babul Saini, Anshul Kumar Yadav, Anand Abhishek
publish: 2025 IEEE 4th International Conference on Smart Technologies for Power, Energy and Control (STPEC)
abstract: This article presents the design and implementation of a compact high-voltage nanosecond pulse generator utilizing a solid-state switch (SiC-MOSFETs) for generating both positive and negative pulses. Traditional nanosecond pulse generators designed to produce high-voltage pulses of both polarities often rely on complex topologies or dual-generator configurations within a single system, where only one generator operates at a time. This approach increases system complexity, requires multiple switches, and results in bulky designs. In contrast, the proposed single-switch design simplifies the architecture by enabling the generation of at least one positive and negative pulse through a minor modification in the pulse transformer. This innovative method significantly reduces design complexity, enhances efficiency, and maintains a compact and lightweight system. The generator comprises three main components: a controlled DC supply, a pulse formation unit based on SiC-MOSFETs, and a high-gain pulse transformer. Simulations demonstrate the generator's capability to produce pulses with voltages ranging from <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\pm 3 \text{kV}$</tex> to <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\pm 5 \text{kV}$</tex> and pulse widths of approximately 200 ns in both polarities. A hardware prototype has been developed and tested successfully, achieving <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\pm \mathbf{5 ~ k V}$</tex> pulses with a <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\sim \mathbf{2 0 0}$</tex> ns pulse width, confirming the system's performance and reliability.

title: Identifiable Single Particle Model with Electrolyte Dynamics
authors: Saketh Pinapati, Akesh Kotnana, Jose Mathew, Anilkumar Davu
publish: 2025 IEEE 4th International Conference on Smart Technologies for Power, Energy and Control (STPEC)
abstract: Physics-based electrochemical models for lithiumion batteries offer high predictive fidelity but are often nonidentifiable from terminal voltage and current data alone, requiring invasive characterization for parameter estimation. To address this, we propose a Lumped Single Particle Model with electrolyte dynamics (Lumped SPMe) designed to be identifiable using only terminal measurements. The model reformulates the traditional SPMe by using stoichiometry in place of concentration and normalizing spatial domains to eliminate dependence on geometric details. The model is implemented in Python using PyBaMM and custom solvers, and validated against the full-order Doyle-Fuller-Newman (DFN) model across multiple chemistries and operating conditions. Isothermal simulations conducted at C rates ranging from 0.1 C to 1 C and temperatures of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$10^{\circ} \mathrm{C}, 25^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$</tex>, 35° C and 50° C show strong agreement, with a maximum RMSE of 0.52% observed at 1 C discharge under 50° C for Chen2020 parameter set.

title: Advanced Phase-Lead Augmented PI Control for High-Precision 2-DOF Turret Positioning
authors: Girish V. Lakhekar, Vivek Lingade, Dhiraj Pawar, Sarthak Shukla, Shantipal S. Ohol, Ajay Lohia, Ramesh Agnihotri
publish: 2025 IEEE 4th International Conference on Smart Technologies for Power, Energy and Control (STPEC)
abstract: This paper addresses the dynamic modelling and position tracking control problem of an Unmanned Turret System (UTS). A complete dynamic model of the UTS is derived using euler's lagrange equation, incorporating mechanical parameters and system constraints. A major challenge in controlling the UTS is achieving <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{Z}$</tex>-axis stabilization and high tracking accuracy in the presence of model uncertainties and external disturbances. To address this critical issue, sliding mode control technique designed for nonlinear uncertain turret model based on robotic analysis, and an adaptive Proportional-Integral (PI) augmented lead compensator is designed to regulate the azimuth and elevation angles effectively. The study focuses comprehensively on the effects of parameter uncertainties-particularly in the moment of inertia and damping coefficient-which significantly influence inertial tracking performance and overall system stability. The proposed adaptive PI + lead compensator scheme ensures rapid response characteristics and high tracking accuracy while compensating for parameter variations and external disturbances encountered in real-world scenarios. Extensive simulation results validate the robustness and effectiveness of the proposed control technique across multiple operating conditions, demonstrating superior performance compared to conventional control approaches in precision targeting applications.

title: Channel Group-wise Exciting Network for Video-based Dorsal Hand Re-identification in Uncontrollable Environment
authors: Jiaojian Hu, Wu Rong, Ziyuan Yang, Lu Leng, Andrew Beng Jin Teoh, Jun Chu
publish: IEEE Transactions on Instrumentation and Measurement
abstract: Dorsal hand imagery is an emerging biometric modality offering a promising avenue for re-identification (Re-ID) in criminal investigations. Progress, however, is hampered by the scarcity of datasets. Compounding this, video-surveillance acquisition typically occurs in uncontrolled settings, adding confounders that degrade accuracy. To mitigate these gaps, we curate a corpus of dorsal hand images at a certain scale, captured from real-world surveillance, with no constraints on posture or spatial placement. Building on this resource, we present a video-based dorsal hand Re-ID pipeline comprising a frame-proposal module and an identification module. The frame-proposal module localizes dorsal hands and selects keyframes. To facilitate identification, we introduce the Channel Group-wise Exciting Network (CGENet), which models latent relations among grouped channels. Within CGENet, Channel Group-wise Attention and a Channel Group-wise Soft Mask enrich representations by amplifying intra-group cues while probing inter-group dependencies. Extensive experiments and head-to-head comparisons with state-of-the-art systems validate the effectiveness of our approach. Results consistently show improved robustness under unconstrained conditions and reliable identification across varied surveillance scenarios, underscoring the overall effectiveness of our framework.

title: An IEEE 802.15.4/4z-Compliant UWB All-Digital Transmitter With Hybrid FIR Filtering
authors: Ziying Huang, Wei Deng, Haikun Jia, Lixue Kuang, Yue Liu, Baoyong Chi
publish: IEEE Transactions on Microwave Theory and Techniques
abstract: This article presents an IEEE 802.15.4/4z-compliant ultrawideband (UWB) all-digital transmitter that employs a compact hybrid finite-impulse-response (FIR) pulse-shaping architecture to meet worldwide spectral-mask constraints without off-chip filters. A synthesizable polyphase FIR interpolator generates root-raised-cosine (RRC) pulses at a sampling rate harmonically related to the RF carrier, while a transformer-combined RF FIR implemented in a parallel-combining transformer (PCT) introduces spectral zeros near dominant aliases to further suppress residual out-of-band (OOB) emissions. A fine quantization followed by high-precision processing and final truncation scheme reduces quantization noise while maintaining a manageable 6-bit switched-capacitor power amplifier (SCPA) array. The SCPA and PCT are analyzed using compact models to optimize output power and efficiency over 6.5–8 GHz. Fabricated in 28-nm CMOS, the transmitter delivers 14.6-dBm peak output power with 28.5%/24.7% power-added efficiency (PAE) at 0-dB/6-dB PBO at 6.5 GHz, and 13.6-dBm peak output power with 30.0%/21.6% PAE at 0-dB/6-dB PBO at 8 GHz. The measured power spectral density (PSD) fully complies with worldwide UWB masks without external filtering and achieves 47-dBr sidelobe suppression relative to the main lobe.

title: Reference Model-Based Cyber-Attack Detection for Stochastically Uncertain Wind Turbine Systems
authors: Álvaro Martín Gómez, Sina Hassani, Szymon Greś, Rafał Wiśniewski, Steven X. Ding
publish: IEEE Transactions on Industry Applications
abstract: As wind turbines become integral to critical infrastructure, ensuring their resilience against faults and cyber-attacks is essential. This work implements a reference model-based anomaly detection scheme for wind turbines with stochastic parametric uncertainty. An <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\mathcal {H}_{i}/\mathcal {H}_{\infty }$</tex-math></inline-formula> reference filter balances fault sensitivity and disturbance rejection, while a robust filter is designed via an <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\mathcal {H}_{\infty }$</tex-math></inline-formula> model matching problem to track reference residuals while bounding the effect of uncertainty. This bound is made less conservative by weighting residual components based on their sensitivity to uncertainty. Residual covariance due to parametric uncertainty is derived from a statistical analysis of the residual generator dynamics and applied in a Generalized Likelihood Ratio test. This provides a rigorous alternative to heuristic covariance inflation by capturing the directional properties of uncertainty in the residual subspace. Experimental results compare the residuals and uncertainty bounds of the reference and robust filters. The proposed method reduces residual sensitivity to changes in operating conditions as well as decision function variability due to parametric uncertainty.

title: Enhancing Durability and Payload Capacity of Tendon-Driven Continuum Robots Using Multimaterial Soft-Rigid Hybrid Structure_supp1-3683602.mp4
authors: Yilun Sun
publish: N/A
abstract: Tendon-driven continuum robots (TDCRs) are increasingly used in complex environments for their flexibility. However, in rigid-segmented (disk-based) TDCRs, a tradeoff often arises between positional precision and compliance. In this article, we present a soft-rigid hybrid TDCR integrating silicone layers with 3D-printed units to balance performance. We develop a quasistatic kinematic/kinetostatic framework that incorporates tendon and joint friction. The model reproduces measured tip-deflection and curvature trends, and we report quantitative errors in tip position and tip angle across tested loads and actuation conditions, measured experimentally. We further benchmark the hybrid architecture against five alternatives of comparable geometry under standardized payload, hysteresis, and durability tests. Across these tests, the hybrid design offers the best overall tradeoff: smaller tip deflection under equal payload, lower hysteresis, and preserved bending amplitude with less drift over repeated cycles, yielding the highest composite score. As a proof of concept, the hybrid TDCR performed delicate manipulation; the central lumen supports through-lumen instrumentation (e.g., biopsy forceps). Clarifying modeling assumptions and providing quantitative validation, this study outlines a path to combining controllability with safe physical interaction in continuum manipulation, with potential applicability to human–robot interaction and medical engineering.

title: Soft Robotic Proprioceptive Posture and Positioning Enhancement via Distributed Multifield Magnetic Inductive Sensing
authors: Benkang Lou, Juan Yi, Yang Yang, Sicong Liu, Jian S Dai, Zheng Wang
publish: IEEE/ASME Transactions on Mechatronics
abstract: Soft robots’ highly flexible and adaptive movements drive demand for sensing methods to control their posture and position. Recently, magnetic sensing has shown promising perspectives in advancing soft robotic applications, offering high design adaptability and exceptional sensing performance by eliminating the need for soft-material substrates. In this article, we further explore the utilization of magnetic inductive sensing mechanisms with multiple magnetic fields in soft pneumatic robots to track distributed key points, enabling flexible and versatile configurations of soft robotic modules over a large sensing range. We introduce a soft robotic distributed multifield magnetic inductive sensing approach that configures magnetic coils at key distributed points within the soft robotic bodies to obtain proprioceptive posture information and extend the sensing range by overlapping multiple magnetic fields. It facilitates a modular design capable of assembling and disassembling different soft robotic modules without compromising the overall system or requiring a redesign, expanding the sensing functionality from proprioceptive posture to mutual positioning, and demonstrating the modularity and scalability of the system. The methodology, from concept, modeling, design, fabrication, to validation, enables motion feedback control in pneumatic soft robotic systems. We demonstrate the potential for expanded applications in a soft two-segmented robot, a soft finger-assembled arm, and a two-crawling robot system, showcasing its high flexibility in modular design, as well as high sensing performance in large ranges for robotic motion control.

title: UnderwaterDG: Domain Generalized Semantic Segmentation in Underwater Scenes via Decoupling Visibility and Appearance
authors: Yuhang Zhang, Yi Tang, Shiyu Deng, Zhengyu Zhang, Xin Lin, Guoguang Hua
publish: IEEE Transactions on Geoscience and Remote Sensing
abstract: Domain Generalized Semantic Segmentation (DGSS) has made significant progress in urban scenes, but remains unexplored in marine environments. Underwater images present diverse appearance and visibility variations caused by water quality, lighting, and habitat conditions, which significantly differ from those in urban scenes, making urban DGSS methods less effective in such environments. To fill this gap, we formally investigate DGSS for underwater scenes, termed UnderwaterDG, and propose a novel method focusing on reducing visibility and appearance domain shift. Specifically, we first propose a visibility-appearance decoupling modeling framework that integrates the Atmospheric Scattering Model (ASM) with Adaptive Instance Normalization (AdaIN), enabling the disentanglement of underwater images into appearance and visibility components. Second, we propose a decoupled consistency loss that enforces prediction consistency under variations in decoupled appearance and visibility, facilitating the learning of appearance-invariant and visibility-invariant representations. Third, we propose a curriculum-guided style-masking perturbation strategy, which enhances generalization by dynamically modulating the difficulty of the disentanglement process. To the best of our knowledge, this is the first DGSS framework tailored for underwater environments, advancing semantic understanding in open and dynamic marine settings. Extensive experiments demonstrate the effectiveness and generalization capability of the proposed approach across diverse underwater datasets.

title: Leveraging Image-text Pairs for Generalized Category Discovery in Medical Image Classification
authors: Wei Feng, Bingjie Wang, Zhonghua Wang, Sijin Zhou, Zongyuan Ge
publish: IEEE Transactions on Medical Imaging
abstract: Generalized category discovery aims to identify known medical categories and unknown new medical categories from unlabeled data by migrating knowledge from labeled datasets containing only known categories, which is crucial for disease understanding and precision medicine. Many methods have been proposed and significantly improved the performance of GCD in medical images. However, most of the existing methods discover new categories based on image modalities only, ignoring useful information in the large amount of textual data related to diseases. In this paper, we propose M<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">3</sup>GCD (Medical Multi-Modal Generalized Category Discovery), which exploits image– text pairs to jointly recognize known classes and discover novel categories in medical images. To address the varying contribution of different modalities across samples, we develop a Dynamic Expert Fusion module to automatically learn sample-specific modality weights, and further design a Local Experts Balancing mechanism to preserve the discriminative power of individual modalities. By integrating global and local perspectives, our framework adaptively balances modality contributions and enhances multi-modal robustness. Subsequently, to enable the discovery of novel unknown categories during training, we propose a Category Diffusion module grounded in the Metropolis– Hastings framework. This module adaptively merges and splits categories, allowing the model to simultaneously recognize known classes and uncover previously unseen categories during training, without requiring any prior knowledge about the unknown categories. Extensive experiments on two public multi-modal datasets (MIMIC-CXR and PatchGastric), together with a private multi-modal fundus dataset, MM-Retina, demonstrate that our method consistently improves clustering performance on both known and unknown categories compared with existing approaches.

title: Equivalence of Left- and Right-Invariant Extended Kalman Filters on Matrix Lie Groups
authors: Finn G. Maurer, Henrik M. Schmidt-Didlaukies, Erlend A. Basso, Torleiv H. Bryne
publish: IEEE Transactions on Automatic Control
abstract: While previous literature has discussed implications of choosing a left- or right-invariant error formulation, we show that the extended Kalman filter (EKF) on matrix Lie groups produces the same state estimate under both choices when a covariance reset is applied after each measurement update. To establish this result, we derive the continuous-discrete EKF for systems evolving on matrix Lie groups, modeling process noise on the Lie algebra and using a Stratonovich interpretation for the stochastic differential equation (SDE). Building on the classical EKF formulation, our approach includes a reparameterization step after each correction, which induces a covariance reset. Monte Carlo simulations on an aided inertial navigation problem confirm the invariance property and demonstrate its failure when the covariance reset is neglected.

title: Uncertainty-Aware Channel Modeling and Riemannian Joint Optimization of BS Position and Antenna Orientation for Multi-UAV mmWave Communication
authors: Qian Qiao, Hong Jiang, Qiuyun Zhang, Min Zeng, Qiumei Guo, Ying Luo
publish: IEEE Signal Processing Letters
abstract: Improving the downlink throughput of multi-UAV air-to-ground mmWave systems under rapid motion and channel uncertainty is a critical challenge. In this letter, UAV states are estimated and the resulting position uncertainty is propagated to the channel statistics through closed-form Jacobians. We propose an alternating optimization mechanism, where a surrogate sum-rate metric is used for inner-layer precoder design and a Riemannian trust-region solver is employed for outer-layer BS reconfiguration. Simulation results demonstrate downlink sum-rate improvements over non-optimized and Euclidean optimization baselines in dynamic multi-UAV mmWave deployments.

title: A Cavity-Length-Matched Phase Demodulation Scheme for High-Fineness Fabry-Pérot Sensor
authors: Shengquan Mu, Ziyu Zhang, Dingwen Bai, Naitao Liu, Yufeng Pan, Yi Jiang, Shuai Guo, Changyuan Yu, Ping Lu
publish: Journal of Lightwave Technology
abstract: A cavity-length-matched interference phase demodulation scheme has been proposed for high-fineness Fabry–Pérot (FP) sensors. This method matches the optical path of a selected high-order cavity round-trip component with that of an external reference arm within the coherence window of a low-coherence light source. This is achieved by using a low-coherence ASE source and external optical-path matching. In this way, the order-dependent optical-path variation in the FP cavity is converted into an externally demodulated phase signal. The utilization of an ASE light source, characterized by its low coherence, in conjunction with a dual-beam interferometer, effectively facilitates optical path matching for multiple reflections. The proposed scheme was experimentally validated using a fifth-order FP sensor with a high-fineness factor. The fifth-order matching case achieved a 5.51 dB improvement in signal-to-noise ratio compared with the first-order matching case. It offers advantages including high sensitivity, large dynamic range, and broad frequency range. This scheme effectively supports the practical implementation and application of high-fineness sensors.

title: Breaking the Black Box: Interpretable AI Achieves Superior Hemorrhage Detection with the Compensatory Reserve Measurement
authors: Michael L. Tibbs, Benjamin McCloskey, Eric J. Snider, Victor A. Convertino, Lonnie G. Petersen
publish: IEEE Journal of Biomedical and Health Informatics
abstract: Hemorrhage remains the leading cause of preventable trauma death, with traditional vital signs failing to detect blood loss until 25-30% volume depletion occurs. Compensatory Reserve Measurement (CRM) enables earlier hemorrhage detection but current estimation methods force a tradeoff between performance and interpretability. We present the first Vision Transformer (ViT) for CRM estimation that achieves both superior accuracy compared to previous models and mechanistic explainability from arterial blood pressure (ABP) waveforms. Using data from 208 human subjects who underwent progressive lower body negative pressure, we developed a single-layer ViT that processes 20-second waveform segments as token sequences. Rigorous 10-fold cross-validation compared the ViT against state of-the-art Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) and manual feature-based models using identical train-validation-test splits. With all models undergoing equivalent Optuna hyperparameter optimization, the ViT achieved higher R2 (0.80 vs 0.77) with fold-level paired t-test p = 0.052 (N = 10) and subject-level p = 0.008 (N = 208). The ViT also demonstrated superior robustness to signal corruption, with the CNN's performance degrading progressively faster under increasing noise and sample dropout. Attention analysis revealed learned patterns converging with established physiological knowledge, prioritizing half-decay and dicrotic notch regions identified as critical by manual feature extraction from the ABP. The model shifted from focused attention at high CRM to distributed monitoring at low CRM, matching known hemodynamics near decompensation. Ablation experiments confirmed half-decay regions as functionally critical. This work bridges the performance-interpretability tradeoff, providing the first interpretable deep learning approach for hemorrhage monitoring and CRM estimation.

title: ClinPatch-AD: Token-Level Fusion of 3D MRI Patch Transformers and Clinical Scores for Subject-Wise Alzheimer’s Disease Screening
authors: Harsh Vardhan Bansal, Tajinder Kumar, Purushottam Sharma, Xiaochun Cheng
publish: IEEE Access
abstract: Alzheimer’s disease (AD) is a leading cause of dementia, and early subject-level screening is important for timely clinical support. However, automated MRI-based AD diagnosis remains difficult because many deep learning studies rely on 2D slices or non-subject-wise splits, which can overestimate performance, and clinical variables are often fused only at the final stage. This study aims to develop a more reliable multimodal framework that uses full 3D MRI volumes and clinical information under strict subject-wise evaluation.We propose ClinPatch-AD, a 3D patch-based Transformer that converts each MRI scan into volumetric patch tokens and embeds age, sex, and MMSE as a dedicated clinical token. The clinical token is inserted into the same Transformer sequence as the MRI tokens, allowing token-level fusion through self-attention. An auxiliary MMSE regression head is also used during training to guide the shared representation with cognitive severity information. Experiments on the OASIS-1 cross-sectional cohort with a stratified subject-wise split achieved an AUC of 0.951, accuracy of 0.898, precision of 0.857, recall of 0.818, and F1-score of 0.840 on the held-out test set. Ablation results further showed that both the clinical token and the auxiliary MMSE objective improved performance over the MRI-only Transformer baseline. These findings show that token-level multimodal fusion improves realistic patient-level AD screening and supports the use of clinical context during MRI-based diagnosis.

title: On the Improvement of Gate Fidelity in Spin Qubits with Two-Level Fluctuators at Higher Temperatures
authors: Yudai Sato, Hidehiro Asai, Takahiro Mori, Takayuki Kawahara
publish: IEEE Access
abstract: The importance of the role of two-level fluctuators (TLFs) in causing qubit frequency (fq) shifts has been highlighted in previous studies to improve the gate fidelity in silicon spin qubits at higher temperatures. In this study, we employed a simulation technique to investigate the origin of the temperature dependence of fq shifts in silicon spin qubits. Among the various possible microscopic origins, we focused on the charge noise, which is attributed to the TLFs at the semiconductor/oxide interface, for which the experimental features of the fq shift have been theoretically reproduced in previous studies. We analyze the TLF characteristics required to improve the gate fidelity by simulating the effect of charge noise, considering the spatial and energetic distributions, as well as characteristic parameters. We observed that the exponential distribution of the activation energies was preferable for reproducing the fq shift and fidelity improvement. Moreover, in terms of parameter selection, a short transition time and a steep temperature transition can improve fidelity during higher-temperature operations. Based on these findings, we discussed the origin of TLFs and observed that electronic transitions are more likely to produce TLFs than transitions involving atomic displacement.

title: Resilient and Interpretable Power Fingerprinting for IoT DDoS Defense
authors: Qingyu Zeng, Mingyu Yang, Yuko Hara
publish: IEEE Open Journal of the Communications Society
abstract: The proliferation of Internet of Things (IoT) devices has introduced severe vulnerabilities, notably Distributed Denial-of-Service (DDoS) attacks. Traditional network-layer intrusion detection relies on deep packet inspection and flow analysis, which struggle against modern encrypted traffic and raise severe privacy violations. To address these limitations, we propose a novel physical-layer framework that strictly preserves payload privacy by exploiting power side-channel signals. This study elevates the detection paradigm through three core pillars. First, we design a dynamic data acquisition and multi-domain feature engineering pipeline. Leveraging this robust feature space, our optimized XGBoost-based engine achieves an exceptional Macro-F1 score of 0.9988 for macroscopic anomaly detection and a robust 0.9658 for a multi-class identification task, seamlessly disentangling stealthy attacks from diverse legitimate IoT workloads (e.g., OpenHAB, ThingsBoard) across varying sampling scales. Second, we systematically evaluate the system’s resilience under extremely injected additive white Gaussian noise (AWGN). Even at 0 dB Signal-to-Noise Ratio, our system sustains a high anomaly detection F1-score of 0.9553, significantly outperforming traditional baselines. Third, to bridge the trust gap inherent in “black-box” machine learning, we integrate the SHapley Additive exPlanations (SHAP) framework. The comprehensive interpretability analysis demonstrates that our model generates highly transparent and trustworthy alerts, explicitly mapping complex classification decisions back to physical root causes. Furthermore, zero-shot evaluations against unseen attack tools confirm the system’s generalization, achieving a Macro-F1 of 0.9755 for anomaly detection and a Macro-AUC of 0.8928 for attack identification. Ultimately, this research establishes a transparent, highly resilient, and privacy-centric methodological framework for IoT edge security.

title: JustListIt: A Trust-Enhanced Digital Marketplace Transforming Zimbabwe's Real Estate Through Emerging Technologies
authors: Isheanesu Tafirenyika, Eva Tsitsi Chigodo
publish: 2025 4th Zimbabwe Conference of Information and Communication Technologies (ZCICT)
abstract: Zimbabwe's real-estate sector is crippled by systemic mistrust, fragmented information channels, and a near-absence of verifiable stakeholder histories. This study employs Design Science Research (DSR) methodology to develop JustListIt, a full-stack, cloud native marketplace converging artificial intelligence, real-time communication, and cryptographic verifiability. Through iterative design cycles, we developed a microservice architecture (Node.js, React, MongoDB, Firebase, Supabase) enabling role-based onboarding, AI-curated listings, bilateral messaging, multiaxial reputation scoring, and digital contract signing. The methodology comprised five phases: problem exploration with ethnographic surveys <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$(\mathbf{n} \boldsymbol{=} \mathbf{1 6})$</tex>, artifact design, development over 18 months, pilot deployment with 20 users, and comprehensive evaluation using mixed methods. Key findings demonstrate an 88% increase in transaction confidence, 57% reduction in median time-to-close (from 14 to 6 days), and 70% landlord retention within 30 days. The platform validates that converging cloud, AI, and cryptographic technologies can overcome institutional voids and create high-trust marketplaces in resource-constrained emerging markets, offering replicable design patterns for similar contexts.

title: Explainability-Guided Untargeted Attacks on Knowledge Graph Embedding_supp1-3689651.pdf
authors: Xinwang LIU
publish: N/A
abstract: Knowledge graph embedding (KGE) aims to learn high-dimensional representations of graph structures for various downstream tasks. However, knowledge graphs (KGs) are vulnerable to adversarial attacks. The adversary can delete key triplets or inject malicious triplets, thereby compromising the quality of learned representations. Existing target-oriented attack methods rely on the predefined target set, but the adversary can hardly obtain this information in realistic scenarios. In contrast, existing rule-oriented attacks only consider short logic rules, and their reliance on random walk sampling often yields many low-quality rules, degrading attack effectiveness. Surprisingly, we find that reasoning paths can represent multi-hop relations among entities in KGs and provide intuitive explainability. Therefore, we introduce PathAttack, the first path-based explainable untargeted attack framework for KGs. Concretely, a triplet discovery approach is proposed by combining selection using different types of KGE models to replace the predefined target set, which overcomes the limitation of unrealistic assumptions. In addition, we extract and weight the multi-hop relational paths between entities for adversarial deletion and addition, overcoming the bottlenecks of rule length and quality. Extensive experiments on FB15k-237, WN18RR, and the massive OGBL-WikiKG2 benchmark demonstrate the superior effectiveness of our method. Notably, PathAttack is scalable to large-scale KGs containing millions of entities, and improves adversarial attack effectiveness by 5% on WN18RR.

title: Effectiveness of Machine Learning for Water Demand Forecasting: A Case Study of Beitbridge Town
authors: Akim Nyoni, Sibonile Moyo, Naison Gasela
publish: 2025 4th Zimbabwe Conference of Information and Communication Technologies (ZCICT)
abstract: Water demand prediction is key in successful water management in towns and urban areas. However, its success in semi-arid regions faces a significant challenge due to inherent uncertainty in water supply and the increasing demand brought about by everchanging economic activity. This study develops and assesses the performance of machine learning models on water demand prediction using Beitbridge Town in Zimbabwe, as a case study. The machine learning models are drawn from a systematic review of the literature. The Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM), Support Vector Regression (SVR), Extreme Gradient Boosting (XGB) and Random Forest models, which emerged as the most popular models for the purpose, were assessed for handling the task at hand based on a dataset of historical water consumption collected from Beitbridge Municipality. Model implementation used two approaches, that is, a baseline, which is the default settings and a hyperparameter tuning using grid search and cross-validation. An ensemble model was then developed by combining XGB predictions with Random Forest predictions to ensure the study benefits from the hybrid modelling approaches. Three assessment metrics: Root Mean Square Error (RMSE), Mean Absolute Error (MAE) and the coefficient of determination (<tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathrm{R}^{\mathrm{2}}$</tex>) were used to evaluate the resulting models. XGB demonstrated the most accurate predictions through all performance metrics, followed by Random Forests. Support Vector Regression and LSTM produced underwhelming results because of dataset limitations and time-dependent sensitivity. The study results showed that climate variables, together with population expansion, mainly control water consumption patterns in Beitbridge town.

title: Towards Energy-Efficient Collaborative Inference and Fine-tuning: Matching Model Compression and Offloading with Resource Availability
authors: Yue-e Zhou, Lianbo Ma, Xingwei Wang, Qing Li, Carla Fabiana Chiasserini, Guangjie Han
publish: IEEE Transactions on Networking
abstract: We consider the collaborative inference acceleration task via cloud-edge-end collaboration, which involves a series of tightly coupled decision-making steps, including which DNN model to be selected, how much to compress model, how to partition model, and where to offload partitioned submodels. In practical deployments, these decisions jointly affect both fine-tuning and inference performance, and must jointly account for such aspects as the model being used, the computational resources and local datasets available at each device, as well as network latencies, which significantly increases the complexity of optimizing the problem. Yet, no existing studies focus on such joint optimization problem for these tightly coupled decisions. In this paper, we model this problem as a multi-dimensional optimization problem, jointly optimizing collaborative inference and fine-tuning by selecting the DNN model, compression level, partition strategy, and computational resource allocation, with the objective of minimizing the overall energy consumption of the learning-inference process, subject to accuracy and latency constraints. To this end, we propose an algorithmic framework called JQODI combining a time-energy tree diagram to represent the learning process, a dynamic programming solution strategy, and a data-driven theoretical approach to predict the expected total number of training epochs that meet the accuracy requirements. We prove that JQODI approximates the optimal solution with polynomial complexity. Numerical results demonstrate that JQODI surpasses state-of-the-art methods in both energy efficiency and latency.

title: Annotating Spatial Multi-Omics Spot-Level Niche Types Using Bi-View Retrieval-Augmented Generation With SpotTypeLLM_supp1-3689143.docx
authors: Dong Xu
publish: N/A
abstract: Spatial multi-omics techniques generate extensive spot-level profiles without accompanying spot -type labels, forcing biologists into labor-intensive manual annotation. Although large language models (LLMs) promise automated annotation, they are poorly equipped to handle high-dimensional numeric inputs, struggle to convert complex spatial-omics structures into interpretable text, and lack the specialized biological knowledge needed to avoid hallucinations. Moreover, the intrinsic sparsity and heterogeneity of spatial omics data undermine robust feature extraction and accurate spot-level niche label assignment. To address these challenges, we propose SpotTypeLLM, a framework for annotating spatial multi-omics spot-level niche types using a Bi-view Retrieval-Augmented Generation (BiRAG) tailored for LLMs. Specifically, SpotTypeLLM encodes spatial multi-omics data through scLLM-based embeddings, graph convolutional networks (GCNs), and multi-view attention module, and then selects spot-specific genes to construct the prompts for LLMs. We validate the accuracy and practical utility of our framework by comparing it with ten state-of-the-art methods on labeled spatial multi-omics datasets. The results demonstrate the effectiveness of SpotTypeLLM in overcoming the limitations of current annotation methods and enhancing spatial multi-omics analysis. All code and datasets used in this paper are available at <uri xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">https://github.com/liiilongyi/SpotTypeLLM</uri>.

title: Reinforcement Learning Control with Self-Regulated Prescribed Performance for Input-Saturated Systems under External Disturbances
authors: Kaixin Li, Ke Xiao, Yu Xia, Zsófia Lendek, Radu-Emil Precup, Imre J. Rudas
publish: IEEE Transactions on Fuzzy Systems
abstract: This paper presents an optimal control scheme for a class of nonlinear systems subject to input saturation and external disturbances, achieved through the integration of reinforcement learning (RL) and a self-regulated prescribed performance control (SRPPC) algorithm. The proposed architecture employs an Identifier-Critic-Actor RL structure, implemented via interval type-2 fuzzy logic systems (IT2FLSs), to accurately approximate unknown nonlinear dynamics while optimizing control performance. To circumvent the singularity issues inherent in conventional PPC, the SRPPC strategy is developed to dynamically initialize the prescribed performance bounds (PPBs) and autonomously relax them during severe disturbances or actuator saturation, thereby maintaining the tracking error within a feasible envelope. By synergizing RL-based optimization with the SRPPC safety mechanism, the resulting RL-SRPPC controller not only minimizes operational costs but also guarantees transient safety and strict adherence to performance specifications. Numerical simulations on a one-link manipulator demonstrate the robustness and superiority of the proposed scheme compared to existing methodologies.

title: Inflection Frequency and Defect Density Based Assessment of Acid-Aged Nanofluids
authors: G. Saha, N. Chakraborty, S. K. Paul, B. Chakraborty, A. K. Pradhan
publish: IEEE Transactions on Dielectrics and Electrical Insulation
abstract: This paper primarily focuses on the transformer oil ageing sensitive diagnostic approach utilizing the inflection frequency derived from the differential capacitance spectrum. Continuous transformer operation results oxidation in oil, leading to the formation of low molecular weight (LMW) acids which accelerate cellulose hydrolysis and insulation degradation. Therefore, accurately monitoring of this acid-induced ageing is crucial. Considering this fact, for emulating different acid aged stages (0-30 years), formic, acetic and levulinic acids with different proportions are mixed with mineral oil and its different nanofluids (doped with 0.01 g/L of Alumina (Al<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sub>O<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">3</sub>), Titania (TiO<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sub>) and Hexagonal Boron Nitride (h-BN) separately). Then, Nuclear Magnetic Resonance (NMR) spectroscopy and Frequency Domain Spectroscopy (FDS) are performed to analyze the effect of acid ageing on the physicochemical changes and the dipolar orientation of liquid dielectric. From FDS spectra, the inflection points in differential capacitance spectrum are derived from which the defect densities of the prepared oil samples are estimated. From the results of inflection point and defect density, three ageing sensitive parameters are identified and their empirical correlations with ageing severity were established.

title: Beyond Cultural Targeting: The Role of Cultural and Intersectional Identity among Hispanic Users of Fitness Applications
authors: Gregory Rogel, Ike Gogolin, Maria D. Molina, Saeed Abdullah, Pallav Deka

title: Maritime Dual Band Multifunctional Radar System: Effective Resource Allocation and Management
authors: Inoh Choi, Sangbin Cha, Min Kim, Sanghong Park, Jun-Ho Huh
publish: IEEE Aerospace and Electronic Systems Magazine
abstract: Maritime S-band multifunctional radar (MFR) has been recognized as the most important system in a naval vessel to detect surveillance areas and track unknown targets. However, the increase in search and tracking (SAT) tasks in modern war inevitably causes a deficiency of radar resources in the MFR system. Recently, the use of a dual band antenna module has been focused on solve this problem. However, resource allocation and management (RAM) protocol to implement lots of SAT tasks in a maritime dual band MFR system, has not been fully considered. In this article, to allocate and manage simultaneously radar resources of the maritime dual band MFR system, we propose a RAM protocol that consists of two stages: 1) resource allocation that optimizes radar parameters (i.e., dwell time and dual band) to maximize the search capability and tracking accuracy of the SAT tasks within a limited time resource; and 2) resource management that determines the priority level for the SAT tasks and schedules multiple beams corresponding to the SAT tasks with the radar parameters which have been optimized by the above resource allocation process. Finally, some potential research issues for future work are discussed.

title: Deep reinforcement learning with action chunking for long-horizon flexible job shop scheduling
authors: Qi Xu, Hongyue Chen, Haihua Lu, Shunkai Sun, Hao Wang, Zhihui Ye, Tao Peng
publish: IEEE Access
abstract: Flexible Job Shop Scheduling Problem (FJSSP) stands as one of the most intricate challenges in intelligent manufacturing, demanding coordinated decision-making across interdependent operations. Deep Reinforcement Learning (DRL) methods, such as Deep Q-Network (DQN) and Proximal Policy Optimization (PPO), typically rely on single-step action selection based on the current system state. Though they have shown effective for short-term decision-making, their formulation struggle to capture long-horizon features, often resulting in unstable convergence when facing production dynamics. There is a critical gap in adaptive, long-horizon reasoning for such complex production shop-floor conditions. To overcome these limitations, this study proposes an Action Chunking Trans-former (ACT)-enhanced DRL framework, which formulates FJSSP as a sequential decision process endowed with multi-step temporal inference. In this framework, the ACT module predicts coherent action chunks from recent state trajectories, allowing agents to plan and execute temporally consistent decisions rather than isolated one-step actions. A confidence-gated control mechanism adaptively balances ACT-generated sequences and conventional DRL policy outputs, ensuring reliable behavior even under environmental un-certainty. A joint optimization strategy is then introduced, integrating DRL learning loss and ACT prediction loss, to promote stable convergence and bidirectional adaptation be-tween the decision and prediction modules. The proposed ACT-DRL architecture is implemented and evaluated using benchmark FJSSP datasets. Comparative experiment results reveal that the ACT-DRL achieved faster convergence, smoother scheduling trajectories, and robust decision.

title: Torque Performance Improvement via Amplitude-Phase Collaborative Modulation From the Perspective of Excitation-Source Offset in Asymmetric Salient-Pole PMSMs
authors: Bitan Wang, Wenliang Zhao, Cong Liu, Wenqi Xu, Wu Ren
publish: IEEE Transactions on Transportation Electrification
abstract: Salient-pole permanent magnet synchronous machines (SP-PMSMs) are characterized by high torque density owing to the simultaneous production of PM torque and reluctance torque. However, in conventional symmetric pole structures, the peak values of these two torque components cannot coincide at the same current phase angle, resulting in their underutilization. This paper establishes a multi-working-harmonic model for both the initial and the excitation source offset SP-PMSMs by the magnetic field modulation analysis method, which includes the excitation source, the relative permeance of the modulators, and the airgap flux density, the contribution of the amplitude and phase of each working harmonic to torque enhancement is derived, i.e., amplitude-phase collaborative modulation. Furthermore, from the perspective of the fundamental wave, the PM torque and reluctance torque are modeled, and the difference in current phase angles corresponding to their respective maximum values is determined, which subsequently guides the topological design of the asymmetrical SP-PMSM. Finite element analysis (FEA) and experimental tests demonstrate that the proposed method effectively improves torque performance: the average torque is increased by 20.74% without additional PM material, while torque ripple is reduced by 6.82%. The proposed method can effectively improve the machine torque quality and provide direction for the design of asymmetrical SP-PMSMs.

title: Real-Time Automatic Train Regulation of Metro Lines with Bifurcations and Short-Turning under Continuous Communication.
authors: Álvaro Cidoncha, Adrián Fernández-Rodríguez, Asunción P. Cucala, Antonio Fernández-Cardador, Jorge Gorostiza-Herrero
publish: IEEE Access
abstract: This paper introduces a novel Real-Time Model Predictive Control (MPC) framework for automatic train regulation in complex metro lines featuring bifurcations, short-turning operations, and continuous communication systems (CBTC or ERTMS), enabling real-time information exchange between trains and control centers for traffic supervision and control. This framework addresses a critical gap in existing approaches: enabling the restoration of nominal operations after moderate disruptions when the line is not limited to a simple or looped infrastructure. The proposed approach operates in two stages: first, a predictive mathematical algorithm generates running-time and dwell-time control actions, balancing timetable adherence and headway regularity subject to topological constraints. Second, these actions are processed by a module that generates real-time automatic driving commands. A key contribution is the incorporation of a granular optimization strategy that enhances energy efficiency while preserving operational performance. The algorithm was validated on a simulation platform based on a real Spanish metro line and, compared to traditional regulation, the results demonstrate a 30.00% improvement in headway adherence and a 7.80% reduction in passenger waiting time in high-demand areas, along with a 10.37% reduction in energy consumption. The computational efficiency of the proposed model confirms its suitability for real-time application in large-scale, complex transit infrastructures.

title: A Near-Field ISAR Measurement Method Using Array-Synthesized Cylindrical Waves with Environmental Coupling Analysis
authors: Da Huang, Chengcheng Guan, Ming Bai
publish: IEEE Sensors Journal
abstract: This paper proposes a comprehensive simulation and experimental method for inverse synthetic aperture radar (ISAR) imaging of rotating targets based on synthetic cylindrical waves. The proposed approach is implemented through three key steps. First, a linear array combined with the Alternating Projection Method (APM) is adopted to optimize excitation coefficients for approximating cylindrical wavefronts. Second, the Iterative Physical Optics (IPO) algorithm is utilized to compute near-field scattering efficiently and extract environmental coupling effects. Finally, the Back-Projection (BP) algorithm is applied for image reconstruction. To rigorously validate the method, a comparative analysis of imaging results is conducted across three scenarios: simulation using the field synthesized by the Gaussian beam array, theoretical benchmarking using analytical cylindrical wave, and experimental measurement using a phased antenna array. Experimental validation using an 8-element X-band array demonstrates that the amplitude error of the synthetic field is less than 0.2 dB and the phase error is less than 2.5◦ within the phase-flat region. The imaging results from the array synthesis experiment align closely with those obtained from both the Gaussian beam simulation and the analytical benchmark. Furthermore, the simulations successfully extract environmental coupling components and provide theoretical guidance for the analysis of imaging interference. These results confirm the feasibility and effectiveness of the proposed low-cost array synthesis technique for near-field imaging and environmental coupling analysis.

title: Using Information and Communication Technology (ICT) to Deter Birds in Crops in the Rural Areas of Zimbabwe
authors: Sibangiso Ngwenya, Samkeliso Suku Dube, Andile Dube, Thembekile Ndebele, Caroline Mhlanga, Fadzai Dzehonye, Nonhlanhla Mthethwa
publish: 2025 4th Zimbabwe Conference of Information and Communication Technologies (ZCICT)
abstract: Optimizing renewable energy deployment is essential for reducing the carbon footprint of energy systems and accelerating the transition toward sustainable power generation. Conventional photovoltaic (PV) site assessment methods rely on coarse satellite-derived Digital Elevation Models (DEMs) and generalized Geographic Information System (GIS) datasets, which fail to capture fine-scale terrain variability affecting solar irradiance, shading, and panel orientation. These limitations often result in inefficient land use, reduced energy yield, and longer carbon payback periods. This paper proposes an AI-driven framework that integrates Unmanned Aerial Vehicle (UAV) photogrammetry with machine learning to enable precision micro-siting of PV installations in complex terrain. High-resolution UAV imagery is processed using Structure from Motion (SfM) and Multi-View Stereo (MVS) techniques to generate centimeter-scale Digital Surface Models (DSMs) and orthomosaics. Terrain and solar-related features, including slope, aspect, elevation variation, and horizon-based shading indices, are extracted through spatial analysis and sun-path modeling. These parameters are used as inputs to a Random Forest regression model for site-specific solar energy yield prediction. Model evaluation using cross-validation demonstrates improved predictive accuracy compared to conventional GISbased approaches, with preliminary results indicating an estimated 15% improvement in deployment efficiency. By maximizing energy output per unit area and minimizing suboptimal installations, the proposed approach reduces material waste and shortens the carbon payback time of PV systems. The integration of UAV-derived 3D terrain modeling and AI-based analytics offers a scalable, data-driven solution that directly supports sustainable energy planning and carbon footprint reduction.

title: A Piecewise Linearization Approach of Inherent Decoupled Triple-Active Bridge for Power Management of Grid-Connected Renewable Energy Sources
authors: Giuseppe Bossi, Nicola Campagna, Rosario Miceli, Alfonso Damiano
publish: IEEE Transactions on Industry Applications
abstract: This paper presents a piecewise linearisation approach for synthesising a voltage controller for inherently decoupled Triple Active Bridge (TAB) converters. A case study, capable of managing on DC side renewable energy sources in conjunction with an energy storage system for grid connected application, has been considered. Specifically, a double-PI voltage control architecture has been developed. The control strategy has been outlined, and the control parameters synthesised. A co-simulation study verifies that the design targets have been achieved in terms of control performance. The results are validated by an experimental investigation on a 2.2 kW, 400 V TAB prototype. The resulting controllers demonstrate high dynamic performance and improved coupling rejection across the two TAB output ports, with a broad operational range and step loads of up to 77% of the rated power. Comparing the results with those of a single PI control synthesised using straightforward linearisation shows that the proposed control achieves better performance, preserving control simplicity

title: Addressing Structural Distribution Shift in Explanations for Graph Neural Networks
authors: Zhuomin Chen, Hojat Allah Salehi, Esteban Schafir, Xu Zheng, Jiaxing Zhang, Hua Wei, Jingchao Ni, Farhad Shirani, Dongsheng Luo
publish: IEEE Transactions on Pattern Analysis and Machine Intelligence
abstract: Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) are essential for processing graph-structured data and have wide applications in critical domains. The increasing use of GNNs in high-stakes scenarios requires robust explainability to ensure trust and transparency in decision-making. A common approach to explaining GNNs is to identify subgraphs, a.k.a. explanations, that significantly influence model predictions. However, this task is challenging due to the distribution shifts from the original training graphs to the explanation subgraphs, a factor that is largely overlooked in the existing research. These shifts arise because GNNs are trained on original graphs, while explanation subgraphs often differ in properties such as the number of nodes or structural patterns. As a result, GNNs may struggle to generalize to explanation subgraphs with a different distribution from its training data. In this paper, we systematically investigate the Out-Of-Distribution (OOD) problem through theoretical analysis and empirical studies. To address this challenge, we first develop a theoretical framework that formalizes the notion of explanation subgraphs through sufficiency and minimality criteria, ensuring both prediction preservation and structural compactness. Our analysis reveals a fundamental distributional disparity between explanation subgraphs and original graphs, leading to a novel concept of proxy graphs proposed in this work. Proxy graphs maintain the essential explanatory information while conforming to the original data distribution through a combination of parametric and non-parametric optimization approaches. Empirical evaluations on diverse datasets show that our method improves the quality and reliability of GNN explanations, advancing the field of GNN explainability.

title: Petrophysical-Parameters Prediction via Viscoelastic Prestack Waveform Inversion based on Stratified Media
authors: Zhengyang Kuai, Danping Cao, Chao Jin, Yu Xie, Qiang Liu, Jihao Feng, Lian Zhao
publish: IEEE Transactions on Geoscience and Remote Sensing
abstract: Accurate prediction of petrophysical parameters, particularly saturation, is vital for reservoir characterization. However, saturation's low sensitivity to elastic parameters causes high ambiguity in conventional elastic inversion. Since the inverse quality factor (1/Q) correlates strongly with saturation due to seismic attenuation effects, we propose a novel method combining elastic and viscoelastic parameters (1/Q) to estimate porosity, clay content, and saturation. We established a well-log-based rock physics model to quantify parameter sensitivities. Using the viscoelastic reflection matrix method (VRMM) to accurately simulate the propagation effects and attenuation characteristics of seismic waves in viscoelastic layered media, we implement a Bayesian framework-based prestack waveform inversion to simultaneously invert for Vp, Vs, density, 1/Qp, and 1/Qs. Subsequently, probabilistic rock-physics inversion is applied to predict petrophysical properties. Synthetic tests demonstrate robust parameter recovery even under random noisy conditions, and field application confirms the method’s stability. Compared to conventional inversion based on three elastic parameters for estimating physical properties, the proposed five-parameter inversion strategy which incorporates viscoelastic attributes significantly enhances the prediction accuracy of water saturation. By mitigating the non-uniqueness of elastic-only inversion, this approach offers a reliable pathway for fluid identification in attenuative reservoirs.
