title: Application of Artificial Intelligence Techniques in Smart Interactions Between the EV and Grid Using V2G and G2V Modes: A Comprehensive Review
authors: Farha Khan, Shirazul Islam, Lazhar Ben-Brahim, Adel Gastli, Farhad Ilahi Bakhsh
publish: 2025 IEEE 4th International Conference on Smart Technologies for Power, Energy and Control (STPEC)
abstract: The spread of electric vehicles (EVs) and the increasing integration of renewable energy sources have emerged as driving forces behind the development of smart grid-interactive systems. This review paper explores the role of artificial intelligence (AI) techniques in enhancing the Grid-to-Vehicle (G2V) and Vehicle-to-Grid (V2G) functionalities by focusing on smart charging/discharging coordination, energy management systems, and grid services. Machine learning (ML), deep learning (DL), reinforcement learning (RL), and multi-agent systems (MAS) are AI techniques relevant to predictive control, optimization, and privacy-aware scheduling. This review summarizes recent advancements in the field, identifies research challenges, and highlights emerging opportunities in AI-enabled bidirectional EV-grid interactions.

title: Fuzzy Logic-Based Hybrid Electric Vehicle with Battery and PEMFC Source for Performance Enhancement
authors: Swathi Lavudya, Sachidananda Sen, Basetti Vedik, Chandan Kumar Shiva
publish: 2025 IEEE 4th International Conference on Smart Technologies for Power, Energy and Control (STPEC)
abstract: Hybrid electric vehicles (HEVs) are an effective alternative to conventional internal combustion engine vehicles. However, pure battery electric vehicles suffer from limited driving range and battery degradation. To address this, a hybrid system combining a Proton Exchange Membrane Fuel Cell (PEMFC), battery, and ultra capacitor (UC) is developed. The UC supports the system during sudden load variations, while the PEMFC provides backup power at low battery state of charge (SOC). All converters are coordinated by a central Energy Management System (EMS). In conventional EMS, PI regulators are used, which produce higher voltage ripple and slower response. In this work, the PI regulator is replaced with a Fuzzy Logic Controller (FLC) for improved performance. Simulation results using MATLAB/Simulink show that the FLC reduces DC-link voltage ripple from 17.54 % to 7.14 %, decreases motor speed ripple by 69 %, and extends battery SOC operating time, confirming improved efficiency, stability, and reliability of the proposed hybrid EV system.

title: MachineLearning - Based State of Charge Estimation for Li-Ion Batteries and Validation by Historical Data and Synthetic Data
authors: Tikam Bhardwaj, V S Kale, Makarand Sudhakar Ballal, Om Rameshwar Kharat, Girish Mittal, T S R Nrusimha Murali Manohar
publish: 2025 IEEE 4th International Conference on Smart Technologies for Power, Energy and Control (STPEC)
abstract: The performance and dependability of lithium-ion batteries under increasing demand for portable electronic gadgets, renewable energy storage systems, and electric cars has drawn closer attention. The exact estimate of the state of charge (SoC), which is a term that describes the amount of energy that is stored in a battery cell, is absolutely essential for battery management. Usually depending on idealized battery models, traditional SoC estimate methods including Coulomb Counting, Open Circuit Voltage (OCV), and Kalman Filtering encounter difficulties providing real-time accuracy due of problems including model dependency, sensitivity to noise, and degradation over time. The implementation of machine learning (ML) algorithms as a strong and flexible substitute for conventional SoC estimation techniques is investigated in this work. Decision Tree Regression (DTR), Support Vector Regression (SVR), Random Forest (RF) and Hybrid Neural Networks (HNN) are the four machine learning models that are the primary focus of this study. An first step in the process involves the training and validation of models by utilizing the Mendeley 18650 battery dataset. Built from a simple battery charging and discharging circuit with a measurement block to track simulation model voltage, temperature, and current readings, The trained Random Forest model was fed the gathered data in order to project the SoC. Multiple 18650 cells with varying capacity were used for validation; the SoC calculations came quite within allowed error limits.

title: Techno-Economic and Environmental Analysis of a Stand-Alone Hybrid PV-Wind-Diesel Microgrid for Electrification Using PSO, EHO, and QPSO
authors: Ritu K R
publish: 2025 IEEE 4th International Conference on Smart Technologies for Power, Energy and Control (STPEC)
abstract: This study models and optimizes a standalone hybrid renewable energy microgrid for a load of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{5 0}$</tex> households in Bhopal, central India (<tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$23.2599^{\circ} \mathrm{N}, 77.4126^{\circ}$</tex> E). Hourly local data on the performance of the hybrid system is examined using wind speed and solar insolation. For maximizing the size of the system's constituent parts, including PV panels, wind turbines, and battery storage, three metaheuristic algorithms are employed: Elephant Herding Optimization (EHO), Particle Swarm Optimization (PSO), and Quantum-behaved Particle Swarm Optimization (QPSO). A precise simulation of the load consumption pattern serves as the primary input for the optimization. Convergence time, Total Annualized Cost (TAC), and Loss of Power Supply Probability (LPSP) are important performance indicators that are used to compare the consequences. The fact that QPSO and EHO show superior convergence speed and solution quality over traditional PSO is one significant finding. A PV-wind renewable hybrid energy system is a technically viable and environmentally sustainable alternative to conventional energy sources for a standalone electric power distribution, according to the optimization results. Its environmental benefits are highlighted by the fact that the system reduces greenhouse gas emissions and fuel expenditures by <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{7 0}-\mathbf{8 0 \%}$</tex> when compared to a traditional diesel generator.

title: Optimum Off-Grid Hybrid Renewable Energy System with Hydrogen Generation Using a Chaotic Brown Bear Algorithm
authors: Henry Blankson, Himadri Lala
publish: 2025 IEEE 4th International Conference on Smart Technologies for Power, Energy and Control (STPEC)
abstract: In this paper, a smart and efficient metaheuristic optimization framework is applied for sizing a standalone Hybrid Renewable Energy System (HRES) with hydrogen storage. The system integrates solar PV, wind turbines, biomass, batteries, electrolyzers, hydrogen tanks, and fuel cells. CBBO enhances the original Brown Bear Optimizer (BBO) through chaos-based dynamics to improve global search capability, convergence speed, and solution diversity. Applied to a case study in Abrafo in Ghana, using a full year of real load and weather data, the objective is to minimize the Levelized Cost of Energy (LCOE) while maintaining high reliability, measured through Loss of Power Supply Probability (LPSP). CBBO outperforms several state-of-the-art algorithms in accuracy, speed, and robustness, confirming its effectiveness for off-grid renewable system design.

title: Hexagonal Linear Electromagnetic Actuator for Vehicular Suspension Systems
authors: Ben Hur Bandeira Boff, Matheus Palm da Silva, Gabriel Behling da Silveira, Jeferson Vieira Flores, Aly Ferreira Flores Filho, Paulo R. Eckert
publish: 2025 IEEE 4th International Conference on Smart Technologies for Power, Energy and Control (STPEC)
abstract: This paper is motivated by the growing interest in advanced vehicular suspension systems aimed at improving dynamic performance, control flexibility, and energy efficiency, key drivers in modern transportation electrification. In this context, it proposes a novel hexagonal linear electromagnetic actuator with permanent magnets, designed to deliver high force density while simplifying manufacturing. The methodology includes defining performance requirements for vehicular suspension applications, developing the electromagnetic design of the actuator, and validating it via finite element method simulations. A systematic approach is used to derive dynamic model parameters through numerical techniques. The performance of the actuator is assessed through simulations in active and semi-active suspension configurations. Results demonstrate that the proposed actuator provides high force density and effective dynamic response in a semi-active suspension system, indicating strong potential for application in next-generation automotive technologies.

title: Optimization of Battery Thermal Management in Electric Vehicles: a Model Predictive Control Approach Integrated With Particle Swarm Optimization
authors: Ambrish S, Sindhu M R
publish: 2025 IEEE 4th International Conference on Smart Technologies for Power, Energy and Control (STPEC)
abstract: Model predictive control(MPC) has found a wide usage in real-time systems because of its ability to control multi-variable constraints, predict future inputs. However its high computational power and sensitivity to the setting of the parameters restrict its applications in real-time systems, particularly in nonlinear and time-varying systems. This paper suggests an improved MPC framework with the inclusion of Particle Swarm Optimization (PSO) to overcome these restrictions. PSO, a population-based stochastic optimization algorithm is utilized for the optimization of basic MPC parameters, such as weight matrices and prediction horizons to achieve better convergence and better control performance. The PSO-MPC controller is applied to a case study of the thermal management of the battery cooling system of an electric vehicle where several thermal states are controlled under different load conditions. Simulation results show that the MPC with PSO-tuned controllers effectively demonstrate the reduction in the power consumed to manage the temperature of battery, a critical component in electric vehicles. The technique works well to minimize computational complexity and to improve control robustness, making it appropriate for real-time embedded systems in electric mobility.

title: Cascaded H Bridge Converter for STATCOM and Multiport EV Charging Applications and Its Capacitor Voltage Balance Technique During Uneven Loading Conditions
authors: Ibhan Chand Rath, Siba Kumar Patro
publish: 2025 IEEE 4th International Conference on Smart Technologies for Power, Energy and Control (STPEC)
abstract: This paper aims to extend the function of STAR Cascaded H-Bridge (CHB) based static compensator (STATCOM) for multiport electric vehicle-(EV) charging applications as well in addition to providing reactive power support to the ac grid. The CHB converts the ac grid voltage into dc, which is distributed across its sub-module (SM) capacitors. A maximum of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$N$</tex> number of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$E V s$</tex> can be fed by each arm of CHB where, each SM capacitor feeds active power to an EV unit via a dc-dc converter such as dual active bridge (DAB). However, the capacitor voltage of the CHB belonging to different phases tends to divert from reference value during uneven loading conditions-(ULCs). The ULC may arise due to unbalanced EV loading on its three phases and/or while compensating an unbalanced ac grid/load. This paper performs a detailed power analysis of the CHB while considering all the aforementioned unbalances and subsequently proposes a capacitor voltage control technique using zero-sequence voltage. The generalized expression of the reference zerosequence voltage is established using both the feed-back and feed-forward control terms. The operating principles of the developed CHB based multiport EV charger and the proposed capacitor voltage control technique is verified using simulation results.

title: Interpretable Deep Learning Models for Battery Soh Estimation Using Explainable AI
authors: Riya Sharma, Rahul Kumar Kamboj, Anju Bala, Ashima Singh, Sanju Singh, Mukesh Singh
publish: 2025 IEEE 4th International Conference on Smart Technologies for Power, Energy and Control (STPEC)
abstract: Accurate State of Health (SoH) prediction is crucial for improving the performance of electric vehicles in terms of safety while managing their lifecycles. Many existing SoH estimation methods produce limited, accurate results while being difficult to interpret, which results in poor capabilities for complex degradation modelling and unpredictable future outcomes. A comparative analysis utilising three deep learning models Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM), Bidirectional LSTM (Bi-LSTM), and Gated Recurrent Unit (GRU) evaluates sequential operational data from lithium-ion battery cells that contain voltage, current, temperature, and cycle count information. The models possess mechanisms that identify specific long-term relationships naturally occurring within battery ageing patterns. The test data prediction accuracy reaches its peak with the Bi-LSTM model when compared to other available models. The SoH predictions are interpreted through Shapley Additive Explanations (SHAP) technology, an Explainable Artificial Intelligence (XAI) method, to understand the effects of individual input features. The combination of Bi-LSTM SHAP produces highly accurate output that enhances both interpretability and enables informed battery decisions, leading to longer battery longevity.

title: Improved High Gain Next-Gen DC-DC Converter for DC-BUS Applications
authors: Aida Shahriardoust, Gaurav, Sukanta Halder, Kaibalya Prasad Panda, Uppalapati Sudheer Kumar, Nilanjan Das
publish: 2025 IEEE 4th International Conference on Smart Technologies for Power, Energy and Control (STPEC)
abstract: An improved voltage gain single switch switchedcapacitor (IVGSSSC) topology is proposed in this paper, an improved version of the single switch high gain switched-capacitor (SSHGSC) topology. The proposed IVGSSSC topology and compared SSHGSC topology voltage gain can meet the common 800V high-voltage DC bus requirement. However, the SSHGSC has to run at a high duty ratio (0.86) value to achieve the common DC BUS requirement. To overcome the duty ratio which is directly proportional to conduction losses, the IVGSSSC is proposed, which meets the DC-BUS requirement by adding a few components having a simple and compact structure. The various features associated with proposed IVGSSSC converter topology includes, continuous input inductor current, common ground, low value of operating duty ratio (0.64) compared to SSHGSSC and other topologies, also reduced voltage and current stress and low losses which enhances the reliability of components and overall efficiency, which can play a key role in reducing the carbon content in the environment to some extent when integrated with a fuel cell or photovoltaics (PV). DSPACE 1104 along with hardware prototype is used for the performance validation for the proposed IVGSSSC converter.

title: Energy Management of a PV-Battery-Fuel Cell-Diesel Hybrid System for DC Microgrid Applications
authors: Pranati Rani Purohit, Shaswat Chandra Mishra, Arnab Ghosh, Pravat Kumar Ray
publish: 2025 IEEE 4th International Conference on Smart Technologies for Power, Energy and Control (STPEC)
abstract: This paper presents the design and simulation of a hybrid energy management system incorporating photovoltaic (PV) panels, a battery energy storage system, a solid oxide fuel cell (SOFC), and a diesel generator to ensure a stable DC bus voltage under varying generation and load conditions. The system is modeled using MATLAB/Simulink, where the PV panel is connected to a boost converter controlled by a maximum power point tracking (MPPT) algorithm to optimize energy extraction. A bidirectional non-isolated DC-DC converter connects the battery to the DC bus, enabling both charging and discharging modes based on the state of charge (SoC) and load demand. The bus voltage is regulated at 48 V using a Model Predictive Controller (MPC), ensuring fast dynamic response and reduced steady-state error. A fuel cell system supported by a PI-controlled boost converter and a diesel generator interfaced through a threephase controlled rectifier act as auxiliary power sources. These sources are prioritized based on real-time energy availability and storage conditions. Simulation results demonstrate the system's ability to maintain voltage stability during PV fluctuations, and ensure uninterrupted power delivery through coordinated energy sharing. The proposed control strategy enhances system resilience, operational efficiency, and power quality in standalone or off-grid applications.

title: Three-Phase ETOSSI-Controlled Solar-Battery Grid System for Power Quality Enhancement and Seamless EV Charging Transition
authors: Sarthak Mohanty, Shubhranshu Mohan Parida, Pravat Kumar Rout, Buddhadeva Sahoo
publish: 2025 IEEE 4th International Conference on Smart Technologies for Power, Energy and Control (STPEC)
abstract: The integration of a solar photovoltaic conversion system (SPCS) and battery energy conversion system (BECS) into a three-phase weak AC distribution grid affected by nonlinear loads poses serious challenges in terms of power quality and stability. During standalone operation, voltage distortion and imbalance at the point of common coupling (PCC), along with harmonics from electric vehicle (EV) charging loads, degrade performance and complicate grid synchronisation. To address this, a filter-based control strategy is proposed that extracts the fundamental components of PCC voltage and load current to serve as references for the switching of the SPCSBECS voltage source converter (VSC). A three-phase thirdorder enhanced sinusoidal signal integrator (ETOSSI)-based algorithm is adopted to filter out harmonics in real time and enable accurate reference signal generation. These signals drive a hysteresis current controller and ETOSSI-based phase-locked loop (PLL) to ensure robust synchronisation and smooth mode transitions between islanded and grid-connected operations. The proposed framework improves current quality, handles unbalanced and non-linear loads effectively, and maintains stable operation under varying environmental conditions. Simulation results using MATLAB/SIMULINK confirm the control strategy's effectiveness in enhancing power quality and ensuring grid code compliance.

title: A Seamless Transition of Microgrid Using Dedicated Resynchronization Distributed Generation Based on Droop Control
authors: Pallavi Rani, Suman Murugesan, Asheesh Kumar Singh
publish: 2025 IEEE 4th International Conference on Smart Technologies for Power, Energy and Control (STPEC)
abstract: When microgrids switch between grid-connected mode and island mode, transient over-current cause major problems. In this paper, a novel methodology for smooth and seamless mode transition from grid connected to islanded mode and resynchronisation of islanded microgrid to utility grid is proposed. In this methodology, a Dedicated Resynchronization Distribution Generation (DRDG) is connected for smooth and seamless mode switching at the point of common coupling (PCC). It eliminates the need for communication lines between the distributed generators (DGs) within the microgrid. During the resynchronization process (i.e., transitioning from islanded mode to grid-connected mode), a significant impact can occur due to the unsynchronization of electrical parameters (voltage magnitude and their phase angle and frequency) of the microgrid and utility grid. This unsynchronization may even lead to system collapse. To prevent this scenario, a pre-synchronization control has been incorporated into the droop control system. This pre-synchronization control allows for the rapid synchronization of electrical parameters, enabling a seamless transition to be achieved. All cases are simulated in MATLAB R2024b.

title: A Non-Isolated Hybrid DC-DC Converter with Continuous Current and High Efficiency for EV Chargers
authors: Pranathi Karra, Kiran Kumar Nallamekala, Pavani Menda, Rambabu Motamarri
publish: 2025 IEEE 4th International Conference on Smart Technologies for Power, Energy and Control (STPEC)
abstract: This paper presents a high-performance, nonisolated DC-DC converter topology designed for electric vehicle (EV) charging applications. The proposed converter is developed by integrating the Landsman and Boost converter structures. This combination harnesses the advantages of both converters, enabling high voltage gain, continuous input and output currents, and minimized stress on switching components. To simplify the control process and reduce the switching complexity, the converter operates with two synchronously controlled switches at the same duty cycle. A parallel inductor configuration enables balanced current sharing and reduce the current ripple. A key feature of the topology is to enhanced voltage gain and reduced current stress. A comprehensive theoretical analysis is presented to show performance improvements in steady-state behavior, voltage gain, component stress, and power losses. The simulation results validate the theoretical predictions, confirm the high efficiency and demonstrate the high reliability and practical suitability of the converter for the next generation EV charging infrastructure.

title: Secure Data Exchange in Smart Metering: A DLMS/COSEM TCP/IP Wrapper Based Approach
authors: Dhanavath Shankar, Hari Prasad Musham, S. Sudha, P. Kaliappan
publish: 2025 IEEE 4th International Conference on Smart Technologies for Power, Energy and Control (STPEC)
abstract: As the world moves towards digitalization, upgrading to Advanced Metering Infrastructure (AMI) enhance the efficiency and reliability of electric, gas, and water utilities. AMI enables real-time monitoring, accurate billing, and proactive issue detection through smart meters, communication infrastructure, Head-End Systems (HES), and Meter Data Management Systems (MDMS). Additionally, web and mobile applications empower consumers by facilitating load management and energy awareness. Smart meters within AMI support bidirectional communication, remote connect/disconnect fraud detection, and firmware upgrades. Data transmission occurs securely via multiple communication technologies, including Optical Fiber Communication (OFC), Power Line Communication (PLC), Radio Frequency (RF), Cellular Networks (GSM/GPRS), and others, as per IS 16444 standards. The reliability of these technologies directly affects the success of smart metering in the RDSS scheme. This paper provides a comparison of communication technologies used in AMI, evaluating their suitability for different geographical and operational conditions. The findings aim to assist utilities or DISCOMs in selecting the most efficient and cost-effective communication technology for their smart metering infrastructure.

title: Order Diminution of Higher-Order Islanded Microgrids Using Hybrid Truncation-Routh and Factor Division Approach
authors: Richa Chaudhary, V. P. Singh, A. Mathur
publish: 2025 IEEE 4th International Conference on Smart Technologies for Power, Energy and Control (STPEC)
abstract: Microgrid systems incorporate several subsystems with a variety of energy sources and storage devices, which often result in complex higher-order systems (HOSs). To address this complexity, this paper proposes a model order reduction approach for a seventh-order islanded microgrid system, which aims to derive simplified lower-order models (LOMs). The order diminution process involves the use of a mixed technique: the direct truncation technique (DTT) is applied to the numerator, while the Routh approximation technique (RAT) is employed for denominator. Additionally, the factor division technique (FDT) is also employed to obtain LOM for the same HOS. A comparative evaluation is carried out between the original HOS and the resulting LOMs obtained from direct truncation-Routh technique (DRA) and FDT to assess the efficacy of the utilized techniques. The assessment includes time-domain specifications and error indices, which are systematically tabulated. To enhance clarity and transparency, graphical responses, including step, Bode, and Nyquist plots, are presented for both the original and LOMs. The results confirm the practical applicability and compatibility of the obtained LOMs.

title: Reverse Engineering of Industrial Protocols From Network Traffic
authors: Chuan Sheng, Shan Jiang, Qing-Long Han, Wei Zhou, Wanlun Ma, Xiaogang Zhu, Sheng Wen, Yang Xiang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Industrial Informatics
abstract: Reliable protocol knowledge is often difficult to obtain in industrial networks, as industrial communications come with limited documentation, vendor-specific encodings, and opaque payloads. This lack of transparency hinders message interpretation and protocol analysis. To recover this missing protocol knowledge, network-trace-based protocol reverse engineering (PRE) infers message structure, field roles, and interaction logic directly from recorded traces. This enables protocol-aware intrusion detection, process monitoring, and protocol testing and fuzzing without access to device internals. Although PRE has advanced rapidly, existing techniques are developed under diverse objectives and assumptions. As a result, it is often unclear how isolated results relate to an end-to-end reverse-engineering workflow, and how evaluation outcomes should be compared across tasks and protocols. In this article, we cast reverse engineering of industrial protocols from network traces as a task-driven pipeline and articulate a unified task decomposition spanning message type identification, protocol syntax and semantic inference, payload pattern recognition and semantic inference, and protocol state machine reconstruction. For each task, we describe key methodological themes, common evaluation practices, and practical limitations that affect robustness and deployability in industrial settings. We further discuss security, privacy, and ethical risks that accompany increasingly capable PRE, and identify promising research directions toward more systematic, dependable, and deployment-oriented PRE methodologies.

title: The Simons Observatory: Characterization of All DC/RF Routing Wafers for Detector Modules
authors: Alicia Middleton, Kyuyoung Bae, Cody J. Duell, Shannon M. Duff, Erin Healy, Zachary B. Huber, Johannes Hubmayr, Ben Keller, Lawrence T. Lin, Michael J. Link, Tammy J. Lucas, Michael D. Niemack, Eve M. Vavagiakis, Yuhan Wang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Applied Superconductivity
abstract: The Simons Observatory (SO) is a cosmic microwave background experiment with over 67,000 polarization-sensitive transition-edge sensor (TES) detectors currently installed for use in observations and plans to increase the total detector count to <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\sim$</tex-math></inline-formula>98,000 detectors with the Advanced SO upgrade. The TES arrays are packaged into Universal Focal-Plane Modules (UFMs), which also contain the multiplexing readout circuit. Within a readout module, a DC/RF routing wafer provides a cold interface between the detectors and the readout multiplexing chips. Each routing wafer hosts twelve bias lines, which contain the <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\sim$</tex-math></inline-formula>400 <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\mu \Omega$</tex-math></inline-formula> shunt resistors that are part of the TES bias circuitry. More than 70 routing wafers have been fabricated and tested both at room temperature and 100 mK before integration into UFMs. The lab measurements for all screened wafers have been compiled to show the distribution of measured average shunt resistance <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$R_{sh}$</tex-math></inline-formula> for each bias line, both across bias lines on a single routing wafer and across all routing wafers. The mean average shunt resistance for all wafers was found to be 396 <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\mu \Omega$</tex-math></inline-formula> with a standard deviation of 16 <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\mu \Omega$</tex-math></inline-formula>, or <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\sim$</tex-math></inline-formula><inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$4 \%$</tex-math></inline-formula>. For each wafer, we note good uniformity of average <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$R_{sh}$</tex-math></inline-formula> between bias lines, with a slight downward trend with increasing distance from the center of the wafer. The fabrication data collected at room temperature shows agreement with the cryogenic measurements of <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$R_{sh}$</tex-math></inline-formula> distribution.

title: A New Adaptive Model Order Reduction Technique for Fast Frequency Domain Electromagnetic Computation
authors: Shihao Wu, Zefei Sun, Ke Chen, Xujing Lin, Mingwei Zhuang, Qing Huo Liu
publish: IEEE Transactions on Antennas and Propagation
abstract: This research focuses on electromagnetic frequency domain computational problems and innovatively proposes a new adaptive model order reduction algorithm. It can adaptively adjust the dimension of the Krylov subspace when calculating different models based on the required frequency bandwidth and precision tolerance, maximizing the calculation speed while ensuring computational accuracy. Based on the second-order Arnoldi reduction (SOAR) algorithm, this paper introduces new theories such as reliable bandwidth, prior estimation error, and posterior estimation error to achieve adaptive and rapid calculation. Through computational tests on various models including antennas and filters, compared with traditional reduction methods, the new algorithm not only realizes adaptive intelligent calculation but also can provide error estimation. Moreover, its computational efficiency is significantly improved compared to other adaptive algorithms. The newly developed adaptive second-order Arnoldi reduction algorithm is designated as the ASOAR algorithm.

title: Breaking the Communication-Accuracy Trade-off: A Sparsified Information Diffusion Framework for Multi-Agent Collaborative Perception
authors: Jirong Zha, Chenyu Zhao, Nan Zhou, Zhenyu Liu, Tao Sun, Bin Zhang, Xiaochun Zhang, Xinlei Chen
publish: IEEE Transactions on Mobile Computing
abstract: The growing relevance of multi-agent systems has drawn increasing focus on communication-efficient filters for collaborative perception to alleviate the system's communication burden. While the event-triggered (ET) mechanism can improve communication efficiency in collaborative state estimation, an inevitable trade-off exists between estimation accuracy and communication cost in ET filters. This paper proposes a fast and accurate ET diffusion-based filter for real-time multi-agent collaborative target tracking, aiming to reduce the system's data transmission without compromise in tracking performance. The proposed filter achieves improved tracking accuracy, reduced data transmission, and accelerated convergence using an error-minimized ET cubature information filter (CIF) for local estimation, and a correlation-aware diffusion strategy for global fusion. The experimental results confirm the scalability of the proposed EDC-CIF algorithm and demonstrate its efficacy in simultaneously reducing estimation error and computation time while significantly enhancing communication efficiency.

title: 5 Questions: Jon Wagner: What it takes to Design eVTOL Motors
authors: Elan Head
publish: IEEE Spectrum
abstract: Electric vehicles, whether they're cars on the road or electric vertical take-off and landing (eVTOL) aircraft, are built around similar electric motors. But there are vital differences including component costs, mass, and redundancy.

title: Entanglement: A Brief History of Human Connection
authors: Danica Radovanović
publish: IEEE Spectrum
abstract: It started with word, cave, and storytelling, A line scratched on stone walls: “Meet me when the young moon rises.” The first protocol for connection.

title: Near Real-Time Spike-Driven Synaptic Plasticity for CTT-Based Neuromorphic Systems
authors: Amirtha Chandrasekaran, Samuel Dayo, Boris Vaisband
publish: IEEE Journal on Emerging and Selected Topics in Circuits and Systems
abstract: Neuromorphic hardware systems employing compute-in-memory devices, such as charge-trap transistors (CTTs), are highly promising approaches for a variety of applications. These systems are highly scalable and dissipate orders of magnitude lower power, as compared to conventional architectures. Including biologically plausible unsupervised learning mechanisms within scalable neuromorphic hardware proves, however, a significant challenge. In this work, a novel near real-time weight modification unit (WMU) modeling spike-dependent synaptic plasticity (SDSP) in neuromorphic hardware systems with CTT-based synaptic arrays, is presented. The SDSP WMU can be divided into two groups: neuron-specific blocks, that interface with the post-synaptic neuron to configure the CTT array for potentiation, depression, or inference, and generate potentiation and depression pulses for weight modification; and synapse-specific blocks, that modify the synaptic weights stored in the CTT array based on the spiking activity of the post-synaptic neuron and other neurons in the network. The circuit is designed and simulated using GlobalFoundries 22 nm FDSOI CMOS process. The proposed SDSP WMU occupies an area of 13.72 μm<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup> per synapse (WMU synapse-specific blocks) and 85.63 μm<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup> per neuron (WMU neuron-specific blocks). Comparing to other state-of-the-art neuromorphic systems, the proposed system exhibits a reduction in area of 1.6X to 17.9X (for one neuron with 100 synapses), making it highly scalable. The WMU neuron-specific blocks dissipate 19.61 μW of power, and the WMU synapse-specific blocks dissipate 0.14 μW per synapse.

title: Performance Evaluation of a High-Sensitivity Preclinical PET/CT Scanner
authors: Yu Cheng, Tianjiao Hu, Ximeng Li, Ang Li, Lingchang Zeng, Shiye Zhang, Yiqing Ling, Yuexuan Hua, Bin Huang, Yiding Li, Bingxuan Li, Bo Zhang, Lei Fang, Lin Wan, Qiegen Liu, Juhani Knuuti, Qingguo Xie, Xiaoyun Zhou
publish: IEEE Transactions on Radiation and Plasma Medical Sciences
abstract: The Trans-PET Explorist 180 Positron Emission Tomography/Computed Tomography (PET/CT) (E180) represents the most recent generation of the RAYCAN preclinical Trans-PET series, incorporating multi voltage threshold (MVT) readout technology. It features a transaxial field of view (FOV) of 160 mm and an axial FOV of 200 mm. This study aims to evaluate the performance of the E180 system according to the National Electrical Manufacturers Association (NEMA) NU4-2008 standards, alongside multi-phantom and animal studies to assess its performance in high-throughput scenarios. Employing 2D-filtered back projection (FBP) reconstruction, the system achieved a transaxial full width at half maximum (FWHM) resolution of (1.69 ± 0.12) mm at the center of the FOV. Derenzo phantom imaging resolved 1.20 mm size features. The peak absolute sensitivity was (7.6 ± 0.16)% within a 350 to 650 keV energy window. Scatter fractions were measured at (16 ± 1.3)%, (28 ± 1.8)%, and (47.0 ± 2.6)%, while peak noise equivalent count rates (NECRs) achieved (1540± 21) kcps at (129±3) MBq, (727±17) kcps at (94±4) MBq, and (100± 11) kcps at (114 ± 2) MBq for mouse-, rat-, and monkey-like phantoms, respectively. In the image quality phantom study, recovery coefficients (RCs) for the 1 mm to 5 mm rods ranged from (11.0±1.7)% to (113.8±2.8)%, with image uniformity of (7.0 ± 0.4)% and spill-over ratios (SORs) of (5.1 ± 1.3)% and (4.2 ± 0.9)% for the water- and air-filled regions, respectively. Dynamic imaging in both individual and multiple mice enabled clear visualization of ¹⁸F-FDG uptake kinetics within major organs, such as the brain and myocardium. Concurrent imaging of two and four image quality phantoms, alongside four mice, demonstrated the system's capability for simultaneous multi-target imaging. These features render the E180 exceptionally suitable for high-throughput dynamic imaging of experimental animals.

title: Quarternion Maximum Versoria Criterion Algorithm for Channel Estimation Under Non-Gaussian Noise
authors: Shivam Yadav, Sandesh Jain, Rangeet Mitra, Ayush Ranjan, Vimal Bhatia, Ondrej Krejcar, Peter Brida
publish: IEEE Access
abstract: Quaternion adaptive filtering algorithms have emerged as promising candidate for addressing system identification and channel estimation problem for 4D signals. The existing methods such as quaternion least mean square (QLMS) algorithm relies on minimum mean square error (MMSE) based learning, which delivers suboptimal performance under non-Gaussian/impulsive noise due to the consideration of only second-order error statistics. To overcome these shortcomings, quaternion maximum correntropy criterion (QMCC) algorithm have been introduced, which is found to be robust against impulsive/non-Gaussian distortions. Despite its robustness, QMCC algorithm suffers from high steady-state error and heavy computational loads from exponential error calculations. To circumvent this limitation, we propose a novel quaternion adaptive filtering algorithm based on maximum versoria criterion (MVC) called QMVC, which alleviates the requirement of exponentiation and enhances the estimation accuracy and robustness against non-Gaussian distortions. To further improve the estimation accuracy over sparse channel, a novel sparsity-aware QMVC (SA-QMVC) algorithm is proposed by invoking a novel approximation of L0-norm. Simulations performed for a quaternion channel estimation problem indicate that the proposed QMVC and SA-QMVC algorithms outperform the existing state-of-art approaches. Lastly, convergence analysis of the proposed algorithm is also performed and validated by simulations.

title: A Low-Cost IoT and Machine Learning Enabled Automated Irrigation System for Precision Agriculture in Resource-Constrained Environments
authors: Zvinodashe Revesai, Kudakwashe Maguraushe, Belinda Ndlovu
publish: 2025 4th Zimbabwe Conference of Information and Communication Technologies (ZCICT)
abstract: This research addresses the critical issue of unsustainable water use in developing countries, which exacerbates water scarcity, reduces agricultural productivity and threatens food security. Leveraging advances in Precision Agriculture, the study presents the design and implementation of a cost-effective, IoT-enabled automated irrigation system integrated with a machine learning model to optimize water usage in resource-constrained environments. The system incorporates soil moisture sensors, an Arduino microcontroller, a solenoid valve, and a Gradient Boosting Classifier algorithm to determine irrigation needs based on real-time environmental data. An incremental development methodology was adopted, enabling the systematic implementation and testing of each system component, including IoT programming, secure data storage, and website-based monitoring and control. The system dashboard provides farmers with live soil moisture and temperature readings, irrigation status, and analytical insights such as water efficiency, depletion rates, and savings. Experimental results indicate a 16.7% reduction in water use compared to traditional methods, while maintaining crop health. Model evaluation achieved an Area Under Curve (AUC) of 0.98 and an accuracy of 91.5%, confirming robust performance in irrigation decision-making. The proposed solution demonstrates that inexpensive IoT hardware and sophisticated algorithms can deliver effective and affordable precision farming capabilities, bridging the digital divide for smallholder farmers. Since it utilizes targeted water application, the system supports sustainable farming practices, enhances resource efficiency, and contributes to climate-resilient agriculture. Future work should integrate renewable energy sources and mobile application interfaces to increase accessibility and resilience.

title: Unveiling Misinformation Sharing on Social Media: A Framework and Research Agenda_supp1-3676157.pdf
authors: Christie Pei-Yee Chin
publish: N/A
abstract: While the use of social media can be beneficial; its dark side, particularly in spreading misinformation, is a growing concern. As misinformation is becoming omnipresent on social media during a crisis, studies on understanding how and why misinformation diffuses on social media have also recently risen across multiple disciplines. However, existing scholarships have neither conceptualised in clarity all the determinants that may trigger the spread of misinformation, nor has it investigated how people process the misinformation they receive on social media and share it. This paper presents a systematic review and synthesis of sixty-four studies of misinformation-sharing behaviour on social media, conducted following the PRISMA guidelines. This paper employs the Stimulus-Organism-Response (SOR) framework as an organising structure to systematically integrate communication, psychological, and technological theoretical insights and capture the complex interplay between content-related and context-related stimuli with the psychological state of individuals for understanding misinformation sharing behaviour in a social media setting. Nevertheless, we articulate six propositions that serve as a roadmap for empirical studies investigating the relationships between stimuli, organism, moderator and misinformation sharing behavioural response. This study also provides practical insights and actionable recommendations for addressing misinformation and identifies several opportunities for future research.

title: Binary Dispersed Array Codes for Distributed Storage Systems
authors: Qin Huang, Guanchen He, Fuqiang Sun
publish: IEEE Transactions on Information Theory
abstract: This paper proposes to construct binary array codes from nonbinary codes by binary matrix dispersion. Thanks to their low-density and quasi-cyclic generator matrices, these binary dispersed array codes (BDACs) enjoy low encoding complexity. Their reconstruction property is characterized through matrix transformation. Several explicit constructions of BDACs from minimum-bandwidth regenerating codes, piggybacking codes, and locally recoverable codes are presented, as well as their similar single-node repair strategies to their nonbinary counterparts.

title: Distilling 10-m Land Cover Maps from Multi-Source Consensus via AlphaEarth Embeddings and Noise-Aware Weak Supervision
authors: Fan Xiao, Tang Liu, Yong Chen, Ling Yao, Chenghu Zhou
publish: IEEE Journal of Selected Topics in Applied Earth Observations and Remote Sensing
abstract: Accurate 10-m land use and land cover (LULC) maps are essential for environmental monitoring, yet their scalable generation is often constrained by the high cost of manual annotation and the systematic label noise present in existing global products. To address this challenge, we propose a weakly supervised LULC mapping framework that exploits semantic consensus from multiple noisy products using foundation model embeddings. Instead of relying on raw optical imagery, the framework adopts AlphaEarth embeddings as input representations. A probabilistic consensus strategy is developed to transform conflicting discrete labels from seven global LULC products into soft pseudo-labels. To further suppress label noise, a symmetric dual-branch Co-teaching network is employed, guided by a dynamic curriculum learning scheme. The proposed method is validated using high-precision National Land Use Investigation data over Taiwan. Experimental results show that the framework achieves an Overall Accuracy of 80.61%, a Macro F1-score of 65.01%, and a Kappa coefficient of 0.7543, substantially outperforming traditional majority voting and individual global products. In particular, notable improvements are observed for minority and highly confused classes such as shrubland. These results indicate that combining foundation model embeddings with noise-aware Co-teaching provides an effective solution for regional-scale LULC mapping under weak supervision.

title: Detecting Land Use Change in Protected Areas Using Deep Learning and Big Earth Observation Data
authors: Gladman Jekese, Thanks Hondoma, Bridget Munyoro, Kundai G Chinyongo, Netsai Chabata, Colletor TC Kangara
publish: 2025 4th Zimbabwe Conference of Information and Communication Technologies (ZCICT)
abstract: Protected areas help in maintaining biodiversity and ecological equilibrium. Mapping and assessment of protected areas have widely used quantitative methodologies to identify, evaluate and monitor protected areas. However, monitoring land use changes in protected areas remains a critical challenge amid growing deforestation, urban encroachment and agricultural expansion. The research offers a deep learning model that utilizes Big Earth Observation (EO) data to identify and map land use change within the protected areas in Zimbabwe at high temporal and spatial accuracy. Utilizing Sentinel-2 and Landsat imagery, the method uses a convolutional neural network (CNN) optimized for semantic segmentation to automatically identify both gradual and sudden changes in forest cover, wetlands and biodiversity corridors. A cloud pipeline on Google Earth Engine and AWS S3 was developed, and data training and validation were performed with field data from local conservation agencies, aerial survey and high-resolution satellite markings. The model attained detection accuracy better than conventional models in distinguishing anthropogenic change from seasonally or naturally occurring variations, marking the transition from reactive conservation to predictive and data-driven environmental management. Spatial heatmaps and classification overlays generated will provide amiable tools to park managers, environmental authorities and land use planners. This research provides a practical and cost-effective solution for countries such as Zimbabwe, where limited resources often hinder comprehensive environmental surveillance. Through the integration of artificial intelligence with big geospatial data, the system supports the Fourth Industrial Revolution by enhancing ecological intelligence and smart decision-making.

title: Design and Analysis of a Triple-Gain Seven-Level Switched Capacitor Inverter for PV Application
authors: Biswajit Sarangi, Varsha Singh
publish: 2025 IEEE 4th International Conference on Smart Technologies for Power, Energy and Control (STPEC)
abstract: Renewable energy consumption is increasing rapidly, creating a strong demand for efficient power conversion systems to ensure reliable and stable operation. This paper presents the design of a high-efficiency solar PV inverter to ensure reliable and optimized energy conversion. This Seven Level Common Ground Triple Boosting (7LCGTB) inverter utilizes two capacitors and a PV source to generate seven distinct voltage levels. The inclusion of a common ground in the proposed 7LCGTB inverter ensures zero leakage current within the circuit. Various parameters of the circuit are analyzed, including cost function, power losses, THD, TSV and efficiency. The results are also compared with recently developed seven-level inverter topologies. The proposed 7LCGTB inverter is simulated in MATLAB Simulink and circuit losses are calculated using PLECS software.

title: Design and Simulation of $15 \text{kV} / 5 ~\mathrm{A}$ DC Power Supply for Residual Ion Dump Using Modular Multilevel Converter
authors: Kumar Saurabh, Vivek Patel, Ashok Mankani, Aritra Chakraborty, Aditya Naugraiya, Meddi Tharun, Amal S, Paul D Christian
publish: 2025 IEEE 4th International Conference on Smart Technologies for Power, Energy and Control (STPEC)
abstract: This paper presents the design and simulation of a modular multilevel converter (MMC) configured as a single stage DC power supply for a residual ion dump application, aiming for a <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$15 \text{kV} / 5 ~\mathrm{A}$</tex> output. The proposed power supply system leverages the inherent advantages of MMCs, including modularity, scalability, and fault tolerance, to meet the highvoltage requirements of the residual ion dump. This paper determines the parameters for critical components of an MMC-based DC power supply with a half-bridge topology. It focuses on computing the necessary number of submodules and their capacitance value, the inductance of the arm inductors, and the key parameters such as output voltage and current on the AC side of the converter. The system, with its determined parameters was simulated in MATLAB to validate the performance and stability of the converter under three different conditions. The design ensures minimum output ripple, demonstrating the feasibility and efficiency of the MMC approach for delivering a stable DC power supply.

title: Enhanced State of Charge Estimation for Electric Vehicle Batteries using Temperature-Integrated Coulomb Counting Method
authors: Venkatesh Boddapati, Prakash DB, Nindrasekhar S, Nethravathi S, Raghavendra Santosh Naik, Ullas R Gowda, Darshan K P
publish: 2025 IEEE 4th International Conference on Smart Technologies for Power, Energy and Control (STPEC)
abstract: Accurate estimation of the State of Charge (SoC) is crucial for lithium-ion batteries' efficient operation and longevity in electric vehicles (EVs). Traditional SoC estimation methods, including Open Circuit Voltage (OCV), Kalman Filter, and impedance spectroscopy, often struggle to account for the influence of temperature variations, leading to inaccuracies in dynamic environments. This paper introduces a temperature-integrated Coulomb Counting (CC) method, which adjusts the battery's nominal capacity in real time based on ambient temperature data. Unlike conventional CC methods, which assume a constant capacity, the proposed approach incorporates a temperature coefficient to refine the capacity estimation dynamically. Through detailed numerical analysis and comparisons, our results demonstrate that the temperature-compensated CC method significantly improves SoC accuracy, especially under varying thermal conditions. This enhancement in SoC estimation contributes to more reliable battery management, reduced degradation, and increased efficiency, supporting sustainable electric vehicle operations.

title: Machine Learning Enhanced Phase-Shift Optimization Control in Bidirectional Multi-Level DAB Converter for EV Charger Application
authors: Subhabrata Pal, Avik Bhattacharya
publish: 2025 IEEE 4th International Conference on Smart Technologies for Power, Energy and Control (STPEC)
abstract: This paper presents a machine learning-based (ML) control strategy for optimizing the performance of a three-Level neutral point clamped dual active bridge (3 L-NPC-DAB) converter, designed for fast charging applications in electric vehicles (EVs). A five degree-of-freedom (DoF) modulation scheme comprising four independently tunable duty cycles and a phase shift is employed to generate 5-level switching waveforms, enabling fine-grained control of bidirectional power flow. A key contribution is the phase-shift optimization using a support vector regression (SVR)-based ML approach, where it is also trained on a simulation-derived dataset to predict the minimized RMS current of the leakage inductor. A random forest (RF) classifier complements this by ensuring zero voltage switching (ZVS) across all operating points. The combined model forms a fast and effective optimization framework that minimizes current stress while maintaining soft-switching. Simulation results from MATLAB/Simulink confirms accurate ZVS prediction, reduced inductor current, and smooth multilevel waveforms.

title: Integrating Water-Solid TENGs into Green Energy Systems: Insights from Experiment and Machine Learning
authors: Mohammad Mohsin Khan, Khursheed Ahmad Sheikh
publish: 2025 IEEE 4th International Conference on Smart Technologies for Power, Energy and Control (STPEC)
abstract: This research explores the integration of water-solid triboelectric nanogenerators (TENGs) into sustainable energy systems. Experimental tests were conducted by varying the droplet height (<tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$10-50 \text{cm}$</tex>) and impact angle (<tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$30^{\circ}-50^{\circ}$</tex>) to evaluate their influence on electrical output. The highest voltage output of 0.262 V was achieved using polystyrene at a droplet height of 50 cm and angle of 45°, while polyethylene glycol samples recorded a maximum voltage of only 0.085 V under the same conditions. Current output for both materials ranged from 1.0 to <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$1.3 \mu \mathrm{A}$</tex>. Through Machine learning modelling, the output performance of the TENG was predicted. The Gradient Boosting Regressor (GBR) model demonstrated strong predictive capability, achieving an <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{R}^{\mathbf{2}}$</tex> value of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{0. 8 7 8}$</tex> for voltage and 0.664 for current. Feature importance analysis identified material type and droplet height as the most influential parameters affecting the electrical output performance of the TENG. The study demonstrates that increasing droplet height significantly increases energy output, while the angle between the water and TENG surfaces has a lesser impact. This technology has significant potential for powering low-energy devices, hydropower systems, and self-sustaining sensors.

title: A Novel Data-Driven Machine Learning Framework for SOC Estimation in EV Batteries: A White-Box Approach Using Elastic Net Regression
authors: N. Vigneswar, R. Manivannan
publish: 2025 IEEE 4th International Conference on Smart Technologies for Power, Energy and Control (STPEC)
abstract: Accurate state-of-charge (SOC) estimation remains a pivotal challenge in the advancement of electric vehicle (EV) technologies, particularly under the variability of real-world driving conditions. This study proposes a robust and interpretable white-box solution by employing the elastic net (EN) regression technique for SOC estimation using real-time data from a BMW i3 EV equipped with a 60 Ah lithium-ion battery pack. Unlike conventional black-box models such as ensemble methods and deep neural networks (DNNs), the EN framework offers enhanced transparency and computational efficiency while maintaining high predictive accuracy. A comprehensive grid search strategy was utilized to fine-tune the EN hyperparameters, ensuring optimal model performance. The proposed model was thoroughly tested using k-fold cross-validation and performed better than previous standards, reaching a root mean squared error (RMSE) of 5.0622% and a mean absolute error (MAE) of 3.6850%. These results not only surpass the accuracy metrics reported for random forest (RF) and extreme learning machine (ELM) models but also highlight the practical utility and effectiveness of the proposed EN approach. The comparative analysis against existing methods in the literature underscores its competitive edge in both accuracy and robustness. This study clearly demonstrates the advantages of adopting a transparent, white-box modeling strategy for SOC estimation and offers a compelling case for the integration of EN into battery management systems (BMSs) in modern EVs, bridging the critical gap between model interpretability and performance in real-world energy management applications.

title: Digital Twin Enabled Adaptive Control and Thermal Management for EV Power Converters
authors: Anubhav Agrawal, Ranbir Singh, Aditya Rastogi, Tanmay Gupta
publish: 2025 IEEE 4th International Conference on Smart Technologies for Power, Energy and Control (STPEC)
abstract: The rapid evolution of the Electric Vehicle ecosystem is fundamentally reshaping modern transportation by enabling sustainable, intelligent, and efficient mobility. At the heart of every Electric Vehicle lies the power converter system, a critical component responsible for driving energy between the battery and the electric motor. These power converters must not only manage power flow but also operate reliably under varying load and thermal conditions. However, power electronic systems remain vulnerable to faults such as overcurrent, overvoltage, overheating, and state of charge depletion, which can degrade performance or lead to system failure. Digital Twin technology has emerged as a transformative solution, enabling real-time monitoring, fault anticipation, and adaptive control. Authors, in this research, present a Digital Twin-Driven Smart Electric Vehicle Power Converter System that integrates high-fidelity Simulink-based modeling with artificial intelligence-enhanced control, fault classification, and predictive thermal management. The system introduces adaptive proportionalintegral, and model predictive control, fault-aware forecasting, and proactive cooling logic based on machine learning. Realtime feedback from the Digital Twin loop adjusts pulse width modulation strategies, triggers fan control, and initiates shutdown protocols under unsafe conditions. Results demonstrate significant improvements in fault detection accuracy, thermal stability, and control robustness, validating the effectiveness of Digital Twin integration for next-generation Electric Vehicles.

title: Comparative Analysis of Classical MOR Methods for MIMO Hydro Power System
authors: Salik Ram Dewangan, P.D. Dewangan, V.P. Singh
publish: 2025 IEEE 4th International Conference on Smart Technologies for Power, Energy and Control (STPEC)
abstract: In this paper, a higher-order (HO) multi-input-multi-output (MIMO) system is simplified to reduced-order models (ROMs). Two classical model order reduction (MOR) techniques are utilised to obtain reduced order models (ROMs). Truncation-based approach (TBA) and Routh stability array (RSA) are applied for comparative analysis of order reduction. The two-area MIMO hydro power system is taken into account as a numerical example to obtain second-order models. Plotting time and frequency responses and comparing the time domain specifications (TDSs) are methods used to evaluate the validity of ROMs. Additionally, the suggested model's performance is compared using performance error indices (PEIs), like integral absolute, integral square, and integral time absolute error.

title: Traveling Wave Based Fault Type Classification for Networks Connected with Inverter Based Resources
authors: O. D. Naidu, Neethu George
publish: 2025 IEEE 4th International Conference on Smart Technologies for Power, Energy and Control (STPEC)
abstract: Fault classification constitutes a critical module in line protection. Conventional fault classification methods including traditional impedance-based, current angle-based and voltage angle-based methods are primarily developed for transmission and distribution networks with traditional synchronous generators. These methods typically rely on the assumption that the local current measured at the relay location is in phase with the fault current through fault resistance. However, this assumption does not hold for lines connected to inverter-based resources where the fault current characteristics are governed by converter control strategies and grid codes. The resultant system non-homogeneity has significant negative impact on the reliability of the above conventional methods. This paper introduces a novel fault classification methodology based on current travelling wave analysis. The proposed technique leverages distinct patterns observed in the alpha, beta, and zero sequence mode traveling waves corresponding to various fault types. The method demonstrates robust performance irrespective of the nature of the connected source, whether a synchronous generator or an inverter-based renewable energy source. Comprehensive simulation and validation studies confirm the superior reliability and accuracy of the proposed approach in comparison to existing methods.

title: A Comprehensive Review on Pantograph-Catenary Arc Detection and Classification in High-Speed Railway Traction Systems
authors: Kriti Mathur, Sandeep Kumar Yadav, Bharat Singh Rajpurohit
publish: 2025 IEEE 4th International Conference on Smart Technologies for Power, Energy and Control (STPEC)
abstract: Arcing in pantograph-catenary systems poses a critical challenge for high-speed railways, particularly in neutral sections where sudden voltage transitions occur. These transient discharges accelerate mechanical wear, induce electromagnetic interference, and degrade traction power quality. Over the past decade, extensive research has explored real-time detection and mitigation of pantograph-catenary arcing using electrical, optical, and hybrid sensing approaches. More recently, digital twin based simulation models have been employed to analyze arc dynamics, enabling safer and more cost-effective evaluation of operating conditions. This paper presents a comprehensive review of recent developments in arc detection and surge protection for high-speed railway traction systems, with emphasis on applicability to Indian Railway environments. The study critically compares existing methodologies in terms of detection accuracy, computational efficiency, dataset requirements, and field validation. Finally, key research gaps and implementation challenges are outlined to guide the development of next-generation intelligent monitoring frameworks for reliable pantograph-catenary operation.

title: Low-Cost Flat-Type Interior Permanent Magnet Machine with Hybrid Permanent Magnet Material
authors: Sagar Gupta, Vaibhav Bhardwaj, Amit Kumar Jain
publish: 2025 IEEE 4th International Conference on Smart Technologies for Power, Energy and Control (STPEC)
abstract: Internal Permanent Magnet Synchronous Motors (IPMSMs) are abundantly utilized in electric vehicles like erickshaw due to their high torque density, efficiency, and broad speed range. This article proposes a Flat-magnet based hybrid permanent magnet material (HPMM) IPMSM where the highenergy rare-earth PM and the cost-effective ferrite-PM are combined. The resultant motor has parallel magnetic circuit (PMC) of hybrid magnets. The proposed HPMM-IPMSM design seeks to lower the cost of permanent magnet machines by reducing reliance on costly high-grade rare-earth magnets while maintaining high performance. To offset any loss in performance from using fewer rare-earth magnets, the design incorporates low-cost ferrite magnets. Optimization focuses on maximizing cost efficiency by minimizing rare-earth magnet usage and enhancing the rotor's saliency. A comparative analysis was performed to evaluate the benefits of the proposed design, with the flat rare earth magnet based IPMSM serving as the reference. Findings demonstrate that the new design matches the torque and power density of the baseline while reducing rare-earth magnet usage by 15.4%. The Bi-Magnet machine concept retains the high performance of traditional rare-earth permanent magnet machines but at a reduced expense.

title: A Variable Duty Cycle-Controlled Hybrid Metaheuristic MPPT Approach for Photovoltaic Power Optimization
authors: Sampatirao Nanibabu, Pampana Surya Kamaraju, Prakash Marimuthu, Meenakshi Varsha, Shakila Baskaran, Anish Ahmad
publish: 2025 IEEE 4th International Conference on Smart Technologies for Power, Energy and Control (STPEC)
abstract: In today's evolving energy landscape, solar photovoltaic (PV) systems play a pivotal role in delivering clean and sustainable electricity. However, the non-linear P-V and V-I characteristics of the photovoltaic system and the environmental variations such as incident radiation and temperature are severely affecting the power output of the PV panel. In order for extraction of the optimal power output and to ensure the PV system operating at the Maximum Power Point (MPP), it is required to adjust the necessary parameters of the PV systems. The proposed model aims in presenting a hybrid metaheuristic MPPT approach, achieved by integrating the Puma Optimization Algorithm (POA) with the Addax Optimization Algorithm (AOA). The proposed hybrid POA&AOA method is designed to enhance the speed of convergence in achieving MPPT, reduce power loss with improved accuracy under dynamic conditions. Thus, is particularly necessary in systems with variable load demand and where variable step-size duty cycles are considered. Through MATLAB-Simulink simulations, the proposed hybrid algorithm is demonstrating efficient performance than compared to the traditional and stand-alone optimization algorithms, that offers a reliable solution for PV systems.

title: Modeling of Series-Connected Split-Phase Induction Motor Under Open-Circuit Fault
authors: Deeksha Bhule, R. Sudharshan Kaarthik
publish: 2025 IEEE 4th International Conference on Smart Technologies for Power, Energy and Control (STPEC)
abstract: Multi-phase machines are known for their enhanced fault tolerance and suitability in high-reliability applications - Among these, split-phase induction motors are particularly advantageous due to their ability to eliminate sixth harmonic torque pulsations through appropriately phase-shifted winding configurations. Series-connected split-phase induction motors have been studied for their harmonic cancellation and compact implementation. However, an open-circuit fault in any phase disrupts the current symmetry, leading to low-frequency torque oscillations that affect both machines. This paper presents a detailed modeling framework for series-connected induction motors under open-circuit fault conditions. A fault-tolerant control strategy is introduced to achieve decoupled operation of the two motors, maintaining a balanced magnetomotive force profile even during faults. The proposed system is simulated and validated in MATLAB/Simulink, demonstrating effective suppression of low frequency torque ripple.

title: Performance Analysis of Non-Isolated Bidirectional DC-DC Converters for Renewable Energy Application
authors: Riju Nandi, Arnab Ghosh, Krishna Roy
publish: 2025 IEEE 4th International Conference on Smart Technologies for Power, Energy and Control (STPEC)
abstract: In this study, four different topologies for non-isolated bidirectional DC-DC converters are reviewed; each of these topologies is then modified to prompt renewable energy. Four distinct DC-DC converter types—NCBBBC, NBCC, NBSZC and NHIBC, are the subject of this investigation. The input voltage of renewable power systems, such as wind and photovoltaic (PV) panels, can be greatly exaggerated by changes in precipitation and temperature. All things considered, these have traditionally commemorated the overall amount of power produced by renewable sources on a global scale to a minimum. A non-isolated DC-DC converter, which is highly efficient in low-voltage applications, would be a great fit for DC microgrid integration. Even when input conditions are volatile, the NHIBC design is able to maneuver all of these and more, resulting in increased efficiency. Efficiency, ripple in voltage and current, component size, and circuit intricacy are among the critical performance metrics used to assess the converters. According to MATLAB 2023a simulation findings, the NHIBC is better for contemporary renewable energy applications due to its balanced performance.

title: Performance Evaluation of Cuckoo Search-Based MPPT in Ladder-Configured PV Arrays Under Partial Shading Conditions
authors: Abhilash Sakhare, Suresh Mikkili
publish: 2025 IEEE 4th International Conference on Smart Technologies for Power, Energy and Control (STPEC)
abstract: This paper presents a comparative evaluation of the Cuckoo Search (CS) metaheuristic Maximum Power Point Tracking (MPPT) algorithm against the classical Perturb & Observe (P&O) method in an <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$8 \times 8$</tex> ladder-configured photovoltaic (PV) array under partial shading conditions (PSCs). MATLAB/Simulink simulations and hardware validation using a Chroma Solar Simulator with NI DAQ system are conducted to assess tracking efficiency, convergence time, and mismatch power loss (MPL). Results demonstrate that CS consistently achieves higher power extraction across all shading patterns, improving global maximum power point (GMPP) by <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$0.48-0.96 \%$</tex> compared to <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathrm{P} \& \mathrm{O}$</tex>. Tracking efficiency with CS exceeded 99.98% in all cases, whereas <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathrm{P} \& \mathrm{O}$</tex> ranged from 99.0499.50 %. Hardware validation further confirmed (CS) superiority: in the Frame case, efficiency improved drastically from 86.17 % (P&O) to 99.99% (CS), while in Diagonal, LongWide, and Random conditions, CS delivered smaller but consistent gains of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{0. 2}-\mathbf{0. 7 \%}$</tex>. Although <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathrm{P} \& \mathrm{O}$</tex> converged faster under three shading conditions, it frequently became trapped in local maxima, limiting overall performance. The findings confirm that the combination of ladder PV configuration and CS-based MPPT significantly improves robustness and accuracy under PSCs, providing practical insights for realworld solar energy deployment.

title: Torque Ripple Reduction of Switched Reluctance Motor with Stator Pole and Rotor Pole Shaping
authors: Om Mistry, Amit N Patel
publish: 2025 IEEE 4th International Conference on Smart Technologies for Power, Energy and Control (STPEC)
abstract: Switched Reluctance Motors are gaining popularity in a variety of industrial and home applications due to their durability and simplicity. However, one of the most significant issues with switching reluctance motors is the large torque ripple. It is a common problem in switching reluctance motors, causing unwanted vibrations and noise that affect the motor's overall performance. This study contributes to current attempts to optimize switching reluctance motors and gives useful information for motor design engineers and researchers. This study focuses on selecting the stator pole tip and rotor pole tip to reduce torque ripple in switching reluctance motors. The effect of stator and rotor pole tip variations on torque ripple reduction is examined using electromagnetic analysis and simulation. The results show that pole shaping reduces torque ripple, which improves the overall performance and stability of switching reluctance motors. These findings demonstrate the efficiency of this design modification strategy in reducing torque ripple without compromising other motor performance characteristics.

title: Design of a Robust Nonlinear Attack Estimator for Grid-Tied Inverters using a Friction Observer for Grid-Tied Inverters
authors: Sandford Stebbings, Shama Naz Islam, Enamul Haque, Apel Mahmud
publish: 2025 IEEE 4th International Conference on Smart Technologies for Power, Energy and Control (STPEC)
abstract: This paper presents the design of a nonlinear attack estimator for grid-tied inverters, where the attack estimation problem is formulated for an inverter model with a nonlinear backstepping control. The proposed attack estimation process is developed using a simplified friction observer that requires to realize the dynamics of grid-tied inverters and local measurements of input and output variables. The unknown friction in the system is then determined as estimated attacks. The robustness of the proposed scheme is ensured considering the effects of noises into the observer design process. The performance of the friction observer in terms of estimating attacks over a range of scenarios is analyzed and simulation results clearly demonstrate the estimation capability of the estimator irrespective of attack types.

title: Damping of Sub Synchronous Oscillation Serving to DFIG Based Type 3 Wind System
authors: Vijay Mohale, Sunny Sonandkar, Madhukar Waware, Rahul M. Chanmanwar, Vitthal Bandal
publish: 2025 IEEE 4th International Conference on Smart Technologies for Power, Energy and Control (STPEC)
abstract: The steadily increasing penetration level of wind turbine-generated power affects the frequency stability of the main grid, leading to congestion. Therefore, it is essential to efficiently transfer the generated wind energy to the main grid. The cost-effective and most popular approach for improve the long transmission line power exchange capacity with the utilization of series capacitive compensation. But SubSynchronous Resonance (SSR) is the important problem that may happen in series capacitive compensation. SSR affect the mechanical shaft to cause failure as well to damage the components in the generator. This paper investigates the subsynchronous resonance problem that occurs in series capacitive compensation and explores the proposed control techniques for the Grid-Side Converter (GSC) and Rotor-Side Converter (RSC). The proposed controller of Proportional Integral (PI) regulator is tuned properly to mitigate SSR. The outlined approach be there designed as well as validated in MATLAB/Simulink, in addition the presentation of proposed novel is analysed in different compensation levels. The proposed method result prove that the control techniques is more effectively dampen the sub-synchronous resonance issues as compared to previous methods.

title: Genetic Algorithm-Optimized Fuzzy Control of Battery Storage Systems for Virtual Inertia Emulation in Microgrids
authors: Yashodan Prabhu Borkar, Soumitra Das
publish: 2025 IEEE 4th International Conference on Smart Technologies for Power, Energy and Control (STPEC)
abstract: This study proposes an effective approach to address the decline in system inertia within microgrids integrating renewable energy sources and electric vehicles. A Fuzzy-Logicbased Virtual Inertia (VI) controller, optimized using a Genetic Algorithm (GA), is developed and evaluated. MATLAB/Simulink simulations demonstrate that the GA-optimized controller outperforms both conventional derivative control and manually tuned fuzzy controllers, even under substantial load disturbances. Unlike studies relying on simplified battery models, this work incorporates a comprehensive nonlinear Battery Energy Storage System (BESS) model, capturing system nonlinearities essential for accurate VI emulation. The proposed method achieves a 44.89% reduction in the Rate of Change of Frequency (RoCoF) compared to derivative control and a 42.75% improvement over manually tuned controllers without BESS dynamics. When BESS dynamics are considered, RoCoF reduction improves to 59.47% and 52.31%, respectively.
