title: DRS-UAD: A Defect–Response Evaluation Framework for Unsupervised Anomaly Detection Under Missing Data and Noise
authors: Abdullah Al Mamun, Nic Herndon
publish: IEEE Access
abstract: Unsupervised anomaly detection is widely used in security monitoring, fraud detection, and cyber–physical systems, yet empirical evaluations often assume clean, fully observed data and fixed or known contamination levels. In practice, deployed data streams exhibit missing values, measurement noise, and unknown or drifting anomaly prevalence, causing clean-data benchmarks to overestimate deployment reliability. This paper presents DRS-UAD, a Defect–Response Study framework for failure-aware, deployment-oriented evaluation of unsupervised anomaly detection. DRS-UAD injects controlled missingness and numeric noise over progressive defect budgets and evaluates how detection quality, alert behavior, and execution viability evolve as data quality degrades. The framework is model-agnostic, avoids labeled calibration, and enforces fixed alert-rate constraints to reflect operational alert budgets. Experiments across multiple real-world datasets, anomaly detection methods, defect types, and randomized seeds indicate that detectors display distinct defect–response profiles. While some methods show gradual degradation under data corruption, others display abrupt performance collapse, unstable alert behavior, or controlled execution failures under missingness. These findings show that clean-data accuracy alone is an incomplete proxy for deployment robustness and highlight the importance of failure-aware, statistically grounded evaluation. DRS-UAD provides a structured deployment-oriented evaluation methodology for robustness-oriented assessment of unsupervised anomaly detection under realistic data imperfections. The study evaluates 3000 logged experimental runs spanning 5 datasets, 6 detectors, 2 defect classes, 2 execution modes, and 5 randomized seeds.

title: MONI: Toward Competition Softening and Congestion Mitigation for Federated Learning in MEC-Enabled IIoT
authors: Bangqi Pan, Jianfeng Lu, Shuqin Cao, Wei Wang, Riheng Jia, Zhiwei Ye
publish: IEEE Transactions on Industrial Informatics
abstract: Federated learning (FL) facilitates privacy-preserving collaborative intelligence, making it ideal for mobile edge computing (MEC)-enabled Industrial Internet of Thing (IIoT). However, the autonomy of participants leads to unstable edge associations, hampering FL deployment. Existing studies typically prioritize device incentives but overlook price competition and network congestion at the server level. To tackle these issues, we propose co<underline xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">M</u>petition s<underline xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">O</u>ftening and co<underline xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">N</u>gestion m<underline xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">I</u>tigation (MONI), a communication-efficient incentive mechanism for service pricing. Specifically, MONI employs a dynamic multiteam Bertrand game model to capture boundedly rational interactions among edge servers. It leverages capacity constraints to alleviate price competition and mitigate network congestion, while preserving the uniform stability of the game. Furthermore, MONI incorporates a genetic algorithm augmented with a truncated Gaussian distribution to minimize the disconnection of roaming devices. Experiments on synthetic and real-world industrial datasets demonstrate that MONI reduces recruitment costs and network congestion, increases the number of devices by 18.37%, and boosts model performance by up to 6.39% compared to state-of-the-art benchmarks.

title: CARVE: Content-Adaptive Rate-Variable Encoding for Neural Speech Codecs
authors: Wenjie Zhang, Yukun Qian, Yinghan Cao, Changjun He, Shiyun Xu, Mingjiang Wang
publish: IEEE Signal Processing Letters
abstract: Neural speech codecs that convert continuous wave forms into discrete tokens are an essential component for building compact speech representations in downstream applications. However, most existing codecs require both fixed and high frame rates to maintain reconstruction quality, which reduces the efficiency of downstream applications. To address this issue, we propose CARVE, a Content-Adaptive Rate-Variable Encoding strategy that performs compression at an externally specified frame rate within the latent space of a neural speech codec. CARVE comprises three modules: (i) Unsupervised Density Aware Scoring Module that combines inter-frame similarity with redundancy-aware cues to quantify the relative importance of each frame; (ii) Sparse Frame Segmentation Module that allocates finer segmentation to high-density information regions and coarser segmentation to low-density information regions based on these scores; and (iii) Context-Aware Frame Reconstruction Module that maps segment-level context back to frame-level latent representations. Integrating CARVE into the neural speech codec, extensive experiments show that it can perform compression at an externally specified frame rate while maintaining high reconstruction quality, thereby validating its effectiveness and practicality in low-frame-rate neural speech coding scenarios. Audio samples are available at: https://ethuil.github.io/CARVEdemo/

title: Resilience Analysis of Closed-Loop Multiagent Systems Under Replay Attacks in the Context of Consensus Tracking
authors: Youqing Wang, Yiheng Shangguan, Xiaole Yang, Wenjing He, Yukun Shi
publish: IEEE Transactions on Cybernetics
abstract: At present, extensive research efforts have been devoted to investigating network attacks on control systems. However, comparatively limited attention has been directed toward the resilience of multiagent systems (MASs) under such attacks, particularly in the case of replay attacks. Addressing the challenge of resilience analysis in strict-feedback nonlinear MASs under replay attacks, this article proposes a dynamic stability analysis method based on a classical distributed adaptive consensus control framework. To evaluate the resilience of the MASs in the context of aperiodic replay attacks, a dynamic compact set model is designed as a resilience metric. An iterative algorithm is then developed to compute the upper bound of tracking error jump at the beginning and end of the attack. In the scenario where the control signals under replay attacks cannot be explicitly modeled, this study derives an upper bound on the variation of the tracking error during the attack period using the Lyapunov stability analysis. It is proven that resilience can be maintained when the resting time between two consecutive replay attacks satisfies a given sufficient condition. Finally, simulation results illustrate the effectiveness of the proposed analysis method.

title: University Faculty Perceptions of Core Concepts in Introductory Programming Courses
authors: Alberto Gómez, Maria-Jesús Marco-Galindo, Julià Minguillón
publish: IEEE Revista Iberoamericana de Tecnologias del Aprendizaje
abstract: Selecting the basic concepts that should be presented in a first-year university course on programming is essential for planning the course and structuring the rest of the curriculum, along with other aspects such as the programming paradigm and the language used. This work reports an exploratory analysis of the syllabi of first programming courses at Spanish universities with the highest number of students enrolled in computer engineering. Using the list of the most frequent concepts, we designed a survey in which expert instructors were asked to identify essential, optional, and unnecessary concepts, as well as to indicate the perceived difficulty of each concept. More than fifty instructors from different universities participated in the study. The results reveal broad consensus on a core set of fundamental concepts in an introductory programming course and highlight those that, according to faculty perceptions, present greater learning difficulty for students. These findings provide a basis for building a map of concepts showing how they relate to one another, helping students to detect gaps in their knowledge and identify learning challenges.

title: Unsupervised Aspect-Based Sentiment Analysis through LLM: A Case Study of an Unlabeled Portuguese Beer Database
authors: Denis M. A. Eiras, Adriana Camargo De Brito
publish: IEEE Access
abstract: This study investigates the application of Large Language Models (LLMs) to Unsupervised Aspect-Based Sentiment Analysis (ABSA) in Portuguese, focusing on consumer reviews of Brazilian beers. We construct a novel domain-specific dataset comprising nearly 60,000 filtered reviews collected from a major Brazilian beer forum, along with a manually annotated gold-standard subset containing 1,712 labeled Beer Characteristics (BC), categories, and sentiment polarities. Two LLM families—one monolingual (Sabiá-3) and one multilingual (GPT-4o mini)—are systematically evaluated under zero-shot, one-shot, and few-shot prompting strategies. Results show that the best configuration achieves F1-scores of 0.857 for aspect extraction, 0.826 for category identification, and 0.552 for sentiment classification, with sentiment detection proving the most sensitive to prompt design. Using the optimal model and prompt configuration, a large-scale annotated dataset of over 880,000 extracted BC instances is generated, enabling longitudinal analysis of consumer perceptions from 2008 to 2025. Findings indicate that positive attributes such as refreshing mouthfeel and persistent foam dominate consumer praise. The proposed pipeline demonstrates that LLM-based ABSA can effectively uncover fine-grained consumer preferences in low-resource languages without extensive labeled data, offering a scalable and cost-effective tool for market intelligence and product development in the brewing industry.

title: Stochastic-Sampling-Based Event-Triggered Control for Switching Reaction–Diffusion Neural Networks
authors: Jinnan Luo, Jun Cheng, Wanying Wei, Leszek Rutkowski, Huaicheng Yan, Jinde Cao, Yuanyuan Shen
publish: IEEE Transactions on Cybernetics
abstract: This article addresses the issue of multiasynchronous time–space sampled-data control (SDC) for switching reaction–diffusion neural networks (SRDNNs) under stochastic sampling. Unlike the well-known transition probabilities, sojourn probabilities (SPs) are introduced to more precisely represent the stochastic dynamics of SRDNNs. Instead of using a fixed sampling interval, a stochastic variable is employed to describe the aperiodic nature of the sampling period, leading to a stochastic-sampling-based event-triggered scheme to optimize the transmission frequency. To enhance flexibility, a novel time–space SDC strategy is developed that conducts sampling simultaneously in both temporal and spatial dimensions while employing switching gains. Finally, the efficacy and superiority of the proposed control strategy are confirmed through a numerical simulation.

title: LSGLM: An efficient threat detection method based on large-scale graph parallelized learning
authors: Yiqing Luo, Mingshu He, Xiaojuan Wang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Cognitive Communications and Networking
abstract: Network threat detection and identification remains a fundamental task in the defense of cyberspace. Graphs are capable of capturing rich structural information while retaining content, and they have wide applications in the field of threat detection. However, existing graph-based threat detection approaches are complex, time-consuming, and challenging to apply to real network scenarios with invisible or incomplete topologies. Therefore, we propose an efficient threat detection method based on large-scale graph parallelized learning to address these application difficulties. This method addresses the potential challenges of network topology invisibility and incompleteness by leveraging bidirectional inference between flow and behavior to construct a semantic similarity graph, where flows serve as nodes and similarity relationships between flows serve as edges. In order to improve the low efficiency of existing graph learning approaches, a non-linked subgraph sampling mechanism is introduced. This mechanism removes edge connections, induces nodes based on intimate relationships, and divides the constructed semantic similarity graph into subgraphs for parallel learning. To improve upon the issue that existing graph learning approaches embed single representations, we propose four feature embedding modes: original feature embedding, global role embedding, area position embedding, and balanced distance embedding. Subsequently, at the subgraph level, we input nodes’ four types of feature embeddings into a BERT model for learning behavior detection and classification based on updated node representations. We have validated the efficacy of the method across four publicly available datasets. Furthermore, the evaluation results demonstrate that the method achieved detection accuracies of 97.84%, 98.46%, 100%, and 96.59% on the ISCX IDS2012, CIC DDoS2019, CIC IOT2022, and USTC-TFC2016 public datasets respectively, while also exhibiting high efficiency as a characteristic. The code is available on: https://github.com/sixteen23333/LSGLM.

title:
R
                    <sup>2</sup>
                    GCN: Rich Relational Knowledge Graph Representation Learning
authors: Shuo Yu, Yingbo Wang, Junxiang Zhang, Jiaxi Chen, Yiliao Song, Feng Xia
publish: IEEE Transactions on Big Data
abstract: Knowledge graphs (KGs) effectively represent relations between real-world entities. However, many KGs suffer from a limitation in the diversity of relation types. When entities are linked with limited relation types, they tend to be highly similar. As a result, distinguishing between entities becomes particularly challenging, especially in larger KGs with numerous entities. Filling this gap, this paper proposes a novel representation model, Rich Relational Graph Convolution Network (R<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup>GCN) for KGs. Rather than using the pair-wise relation in most general knowledge graphs, R<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup>GCN fuses the high-order structural information and edge directions during representation learning. By this design, the types of relations are largely enriched, and similar entities can be distinguished. This can naturally alleviate the high similarity issue and thus overcome the representation difficulty. We evaluate R<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup>GCN on two downstream tasks with both general KGs and relation-limited KGs. The consistently good performance of our R<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup>GCN demonstrates its effectiveness on relation enrichment.

title: A Non-Learned Multi-Band Relative Contrastive Loss for Speech Enhancement
authors: Xingyu Shen, Wei-Ping Zhu, Benoit Champagne
publish: IEEE Signal Processing Letters
abstract: Conventional training objectives for speech enhancement, such as mean squared error (MSE) in the time frequency domain and scale-invariant signal-to-distortion ratio (SI-SDR) in the waveform domain, exhibit limited correlation with human perception. Recent perceptual objectives better align with perceptual metrics, but often rely on auxiliary assessment networks or pretrained encoders, increasing training complexity and potentially causing instability. To address these limitations, this letter proposes a non-learned, closed-form objective, termed the multi-band relative contrastive loss (MBRCL). MBRCL enforces a relative margin in perceptually informed frequency bands to constrain the enhanced speech to be strictly closer to the clean reference than to the noisy input, and incorporates a lightweight temporal envelope gradient consistency term to preserve temporal dynamics. The loss is non-learned, almost everywhere differentiable, and requires only paired noisy–clean utterances for training. Experiments on BLSTM, CNN-U-Net, and GTCRN show gains over the matched fidelity baseline in terms of PESQ, STOI, SI-SDRi, and DNSMOS-OVRL; for the BLSTM setting, MBRCL also outperforms PESQ-inspired PMSQE, Quality-Net-based learned-loss training, and a recent frozen WavLM feature-space objective.

title: Modified Tuna Swarm Optimization Based Volt-VAr Optimization in Radial Distribution System Using Energy Storage Systems
authors: Deepak Porwal, Manoj Fozdar, Rajive Tiwari
publish: 2025 IEEE 4th International Conference on Smart Technologies for Power, Energy and Control (STPEC)
abstract: Distributed Generation integrated with Volt-VAr Optimization(VVO) is crucial for dependable and cost-effective distribution grid operations. Energy management strategies are essential to ensure demand, enhance system performance, and guarantee the proper operation of renewable energy-based hybrid systems. In recent years, DG has rapidly gained recognition for its potential to optimize power demands. This work proposes the Modified Tuna Swarm Optimization (MTSO) based swarm intelligence technique to redesign the radial nature distribution networks for voltage magnitude profile enhancement and power losses reduction. With 0.9742 p.u. voltage, the proposed MTSO has successfully reduced real and reactive network losses to <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{1 0 5. 4 7 \text{ kW } \text { and } \mathbf { 8 8. 2 5 \text{ kVAr } } \text {, respectively in 33- }}$</tex> bus system. With 0.9913 p.u. voltage, the suggested MTSO reduced the 69-bus's active and reactive power losses to 68.47 kW and <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{4 7. 2 5 k V A r}$</tex>, respectively.

title: Evaluating PWM Strategies for Enhanced Performance in Three-Phase Modular Multilevel Converter
authors: Krunal Prajapati, Mohd Alam, Pradyumn Chaturvedi
publish: 2025 IEEE 4th International Conference on Smart Technologies for Power, Energy and Control (STPEC)
abstract: This study offers a thorough analysis and comparison of many pulse width modulation (PWM) methods used with a three-phase, five-level Modular Multilevel Converter (MMC). By comparing triangular carrier signals with a sinusoidal reference, the study focuses on the basic pulse and switching pattern production that is performed. The study investigates Nearest Level Control (NLC), Hybrid PWM, Phase Shift PWM (PSPWM), Phase Disposition PWM (PDPWM), Phase Opposition Disposition PWM (PODPWM), and Alternative Phase Opposition Disposition PWM (APODPWM). All methods are assessed using the same modulation index and carrier frequency to ensure a balanced comparison. A three-phase MMC functioning as an inverter is modeled and examined in-depth in simulation tests to confirm the efficacy of each modulation technique. The findings offer important information for choosing suitable modulation techniques for MMC systems for enhanced performance.

title: Intelligent ANN Control Technique for Grid Integrated Solar PV System
authors: Shruti Prajapati, Rachana Garg, Priya Mahajan
publish: 2025 IEEE 4th International Conference on Smart Technologies for Power, Energy and Control (STPEC)
abstract: This paper presents an adaptive artificial neural network (ANN) controller using synchronous reference frame theory (SRFT) for a grid-integrated solar photovoltaic (SPV) system. Traditional PI controllers are quite straightforward to design and deploy. They also operate well in steady-state settings however when dealing with variable nonlinear balanced/unbalanced loads and changing environmental conditions, these controllers exhibit slow dynamic response. This paper uses adaptive neural networks (ANNs) to improve the dynamic performance of a grid-connected solar PV system. The presented ANN controller's improved performance is validated by simulation results, which include comparisons with fuzzy-PI controller and conventional PI controllers in terms of PV power output, DC bus voltage regulation and total harmonic distortion, demonstrating the ANN-controller's robustness under varying irradiation, load, and grid disturbance conditions.

title: Analysis and Control of NIBB-Converter Assisted Improved Triple Active Bridge Converter for Two-Port Application
authors: Silpashree Sahu, Dipankar De, Alberto Castellazzi
publish: 2025 IEEE 4th International Conference on Smart Technologies for Power, Energy and Control (STPEC)
abstract: This paper presents a novel non-inverting buck boost converter (NIBB) integrated triple active bridge converter with improved performance in terms of peak-to-peak current ripple compared to conventional dual active bridge solutions. The choice of DC-DC converter along with the isolated bridge converter is made based on a comparative study within commonly used converter topologies. The closed-loop interaction between the dual active bridge converter and NIBB converter is analyzed in detail considering the four different modes of operation of the NIBB converter, namely (a) Boost mode, (b) Extended Boost mode, (c) Extended Buck mode, and (d) Buck mode. The control voltage gain, audio susceptibility, and output impedance transfer functions for the system are determined for NIBB and dual active bridge considering mutual effects between the converters for each of these modes of operation. The theoretically determined response is compared to the simulation model for different modes of operation. The proposed concept is verified through simulation and experimental measurements from a scaled-down prototype.

title: Smart Energy Management System for Green Microgrids with Different Energy Storage Devices
authors: Gupteswar Behera, Pallavi Bharadwaj
publish: 2025 IEEE 4th International Conference on Smart Technologies for Power, Energy and Control (STPEC)
abstract: This paper presents a comparative techno-economic analysis of pumped hydro energy storage (PHES) and battery energy storage systems (BESS) for low operational cost scheduling in a solar rich microgrid. A one-month load profile of an academic campus is considered, where the load is met using solar photovoltaic (PV) generation, grid power, and an energy storage device (ESD). Both load and solar PV availability are forecasted using a long short-term memory (LSTM) model. An optimization-based dispatch that minimizes the total operational cost under the constraint of meeting demand at all times is proposed here. Results show that the PHES configuration achieves a final operational cost of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$3.83 \text{INR} / \text{kWh}, 12.53 \%$</tex> lower than the 4.31 INR/kWh for the BESS configuration.

title: AI-Driven Predictive Maintenance for Boost-Luo Converter-Fed Motor Drives in Renewable Energy Systems
authors: G Jawahar Sagar, Rakesh Kumar, Manoj Kumar Kar, Hasmat Malik, Amit Kumar Yadav, Tarkeshwar Matho
publish: 2025 IEEE 4th International Conference on Smart Technologies for Power, Energy and Control (STPEC)
abstract: The increasing integration of renewable energy systems such as photovoltaic (PV) arrays and wind turbines demands highly efficient and reliable power conversion for motor drive applications. Boost-Luo converters, known for their high voltage gain and improved efficiency, are commonly used in such systems. However, power electronic components and motor drives are susceptible to faults due to thermal, electrical, and mechanical stress, leading to unplanned downtimes and maintenance challenges. This paper presents a comprehensive AI-based predictive maintenance framework for Boost-Luo converter-fed motor drive systems operating in renewable energy environments. The proposed system leverages Deep Neural Networks (DNN) for fault classification and Adaptive Neuro-Fuzzy Inference Systems (ANFIS) for Remaining Useful Life (RUL) prediction. Multisensor data including voltage, current, temperature, and vibration are processed through feature extraction and dimensionality reduction techniques. Additionally, a Grey Wolf Optimization (GWO) algorithm is employed to optimally tune the PI controller for voltage regulation and harmonic distortion minimization. Simulation results validate the system's ability to perform early fault detection, precise RUL estimation, and enhanced converter control, leading to improved system reliability and efficiency.

title: A Comparative Analysis of HDLC and TCP/IP Communication Profiles for DLMS/COSEM-Based Smart Metering Systems
authors: Dhanavath Shankar, Hari Prasad Musham, S. Sudha, P. Kaliappan
publish: 2025 IEEE 4th International Conference on Smart Technologies for Power, Energy and Control (STPEC)
abstract: This paper presents a comparative analysis of the High-Level Data Link Control (HDLC) and Transmission Control Protocol/Internet Protocol (TCP/IP) communication profiles used in Device Language Message Specification (DLMS) / Companion Specification for Energy Metering (COSEM) based smart metering systems. These profiles are essential for enabling reliable, secure, and interoperable data exchange within Advanced Metering Infrastructure (AMI). The HDLC profile is a connection-oriented, three-layer protocol optimized for low-bandwidth networks, while the TCP/IP profile supports modern IP-based communication, offering greater scalability and flexibility. This analysis examines key aspects such as frame structures, message formats, session handling, and the procedures for establishing and releasing Application Associations (AAs). It also explains how authentication and encryption are managed through Association Request (AARQ) / Application Response (AARE) messages in both profiles. A comparison of message exchange patterns highlights the practical differences in protocol behaviour. This analysis provides insights to help utilities and system integrators select the most suitable communication profile for efficient and scalable smart metering in India.

title: Grid-Tied Solar Water Pumping System Using Sensorless PMSM and IAVSLMS Algorithm
authors: Wazid Ali, Divyank Srivastava, Bhim Singh, Arunima Verma, Saurabh Mani Tripathi
publish: 2025 IEEE 4th International Conference on Smart Technologies for Power, Energy and Control (STPEC)
abstract: This paper presents comprehensive modeling and analysis of grid tied solar photovoltaic (PV) water pumping system (WPS) with a battery support. PV array operates with a perturb and observe <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$(\mathrm{P} \& \mathrm{O})$</tex> maximum power point tracking (MPPT) algorithm and interfaces to grid via a boost converter and a voltage source converter (VSC). A battery is associated with unified DC link to support power balance and enhance system stability. A permanent magnet synchronous motor (PMSM) drive, controlled via a back electromotive force model-reference adaptive system (MRAS) sensorless technique, is employed for water pumping. PMSM drive is powered through a voltage source inverter (VSI) from same DC link. Grid synchronization is achieved using an enhanced variable step least mean square (IAVSLMS) algorithm based on inverse hyperbolic tangent (artanh) function, providing robust performance in dynamic conditions. Proposed system is evaluated under two operational modes: grid tied mode (GTM) and islanded mode (IM), with seamless transition facilitated by a solid-state switch. Performance is analyzed at constant and varying solar irradiance conditions, and during mode switching between GTM and IM operations. Additionally, system response to a single phasing of a nonlinear load is examined. Throughout all scenarios, PMSM drive maintains stable and unaffected speed performance, demonstrating capability of suggested control strategies and system configuration.

title: Stability Analysis of GFLI and GFMI Integrated with Weak-AC Grid and Under SSO Phenomenon
authors: G. Ravi Kumar, N. V. Srikanth, Sudipta Mitra
publish: 2025 IEEE 4th International Conference on Smart Technologies for Power, Energy and Control (STPEC)
abstract: The continuous increase in renewable energy generation primarily depends on Voltage Source Converters (VSCs) for grid integration. VSCs primarily use Grid-Following Inverters (GFLIs), but they are now slowly shifting toward Grid-Forming Inverters (GFMIs) to overcome the inherent limitations of GFLIs especially when operating under weak grid conditions. This paper presents a comprehensive stability analysis of both GFLIs and GFMIs integrated with weak AC grids. The study specifically investigates their performance under weak-grid scenarios and sub-synchronous oscillation (SSO) conditions through detailed analytical models. These analytical models of the GFLI and GFMI were developed using MATLAB/SIMULINK in the dq-frame to measure eigenvalues and time responses for stability assessment under various operating conditions i.e Short-Circuit Ratio (SCR) of 2 and 5.

title: A Modified Hysteresis Controller for SRM Drive Fed from AHB Converter
authors: Sumit Kumar Singh, Amit Kumar Jain
publish: 2025 IEEE 4th International Conference on Smart Technologies for Power, Energy and Control (STPEC)
abstract: The conventional hysteresis current controller employed in the switched reluctance motor (SRM) drive fed from the asymmetric H-bridge converter (AHBC) does not fully exploit all available switching states. The controller proposed in this article exploits the redundant states to reduce the maximum switching frequency and redistribute losses and heat among switching devices, thereby improving overall reliability, without increasing the current ripple. However, the hysteresis controller inherently generates electromagnetic interference (EMI), which cannot be completely eliminated. The reduction in maximum switching frequency achieved by the proposed controller could help reduce EMI and electromagnetic compatibility (EMC) issues, thereby improving system performance.

title: Fuel Cell Integrated High Gain and Mitigated Device Stress Quadratic Boost Converter
authors: Ravi Kumar Kanaparthi, Jay Prakash Singh, Makarand Sudhakar Ballal
publish: 2025 IEEE 4th International Conference on Smart Technologies for Power, Energy and Control (STPEC)
abstract: This presented work offers a sophisticated quadratic boost converter capable of generating increased DC output voltage with improved voltage gain, making it especially useful for fuel cell-based energy systems. The suggested design improves on the standard quadratic boost configuration by using a switched capacitor-inductor network in combination with a voltage multiplier cell to achieve higher gains. In this structure, voltage stress is evenly spread among the intermediate capacitors, significantly reducing the stress on the primary switching component. Furthermore, the converter provides a constant input current with low ripple content. The steady-state performance is assessed utilizing continuous nodes of operation and compared to current converter configurations. The suggested system is validated by simulation in LTspice for a 200 W configuration with a switching frequency of 50 kHz.

title: Optimal Energy Management of Grid-Connected Microgrid, Including Conventional and Non-Conventional Energy Sources, using Demand Response
authors: Aashish Kumar Bohre, Debjit Maji, Hemant Patel
publish: 2025 IEEE 4th International Conference on Smart Technologies for Power, Energy and Control (STPEC)
abstract: A microgrid represents a compact, decentralized energy system that supplies power to localized communities, including villages and small-scale commercial setups. Its primary sources include distributed generators alongside renewable units such as solar and wind. Effective demand response (DR) energy management is critical for optimal operation and control. This study investigates advanced energy management strategies for a microgrid operating with the primary power grid. An incentivebased demand response framework, integrated with penalty mechanisms, is proposed to regulate energy consumption efficiently. The framework aims to maximize microgrid operator benefits through DR while concurrently minimizing conventional generator fuel costs and power transfer expenses. The proposed optimal dispatch strategy adheres to load demand requirements and operational constraints. The study evaluates a microgrid configuration consisting of multiple conventional generators and wind units on the generation side and rural consumers on the load side. Simulation results indicate that integrating a grid-connected microgrid with an incentive-driven DR scheme significantly reduces dependence on the primary grid and enhances energy management performance by balancing supply-side generation with demand-side consumption.

title: Impedance-Based Topology Identification for Radial Distribution Networks Using Smart Meter Data
authors: Ujjawal Jhajharia, Satish Sharma, N.P. Padhy
publish: 2025 IEEE 4th International Conference on Smart Technologies for Power, Energy and Control (STPEC)
abstract: Distribution network topology is required for proper power system operation, planning, and integration of distributed energy resources. In practical distribution systems, generally, there is incomplete or outdated connectivity information due to factors such as continuous network reconfiguration, unmonitored switching devices, and limited real quantity measurement. This paper presents a method for topology and line parameter identification in radial distribution networks using time-synchronised smart meter data. The method is based on the impedance invariant criteria, that is, the impedance of a line connecting a bus and its true parent remains stable or almost invariant over time at different loading conditions, while incorrect connections exhibit significant variations. By analysing the smart meter data collected at regular intervals, the algorithm reconstructs the parent-child structure of the network and calculates the respective line impedances. Unlike many existing methods, the proposed technique does not rely on prior topology knowledge or GIS data. The method is validated on the 18-bus radial distribution system, where it accurately recovers all connections and impedance values. The method is scalable and cost-effective for deployment in radial distribution networks equipped with smart meters.

title: Dual Solar Based Isolated Battery Charger for Vehicle Integrated Photovoltaics Application
authors: Sanam Agnihotri, Dipankar Debnath
publish: 2025 IEEE 4th International Conference on Smart Technologies for Power, Energy and Control (STPEC)
abstract: Electric vehicles (EVs) are increasingly being employed for various applications. However, charging these vehicles remains a significant challenge due to the inadequate availability of charging stations, particularly in rural or off-grid areas. To partially address this issue, this paper presents a vehicleintegrated photovoltaics (VIPV)-based isolated solar charger aimed at extending the range of EVs. The proposed topology features two input ports for two separate PV modules and one output port, integrated with a high-frequency transformer (HFT) and a voltage doubler circuit, making it suitable for high-voltage EV battery integration. The proposed scheme ensures battery charging using a single photovoltaic (PV) module, even when one panel experiences an open circuit fault (OCF) and is temporarily disconnected. Additionally, an overcharge protection mechanism is incorporated into the control strategy to prevent battery damage from excessive charging. A simple, traditional maximum power point tracking (MPPT) technique is employed to extract the maximum power from the two solar panels. The complete converter circuit and proposed scheme are simulated in the MATLAB/Simulink environment, and the results are validated under various operating modes and different irradiation levels.

title: Evaluating Hybrid Grid-Scale Energy Storage Solutions for India's 2025 Power Requirements
authors: Debanjan Mukherjee, Karuna Kalita, Subhash Kumar, Amaresh Mallick, Samar Chandra De
publish: 2025 IEEE 4th International Conference on Smart Technologies for Power, Energy and Control (STPEC)
abstract: India aims to source <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{5 0 \%}$</tex> of its grid capacity from renewables by 2030. To manage their variability and meet growing demand, hybrid grid-scale energy storage is vital for grid stability and inertia support. This manuscript thus provides different grid-scale energy storage solutions for the current electricity demand of India in 2025. These include lithium ion battery, pumped hydro, adiabatic compressed air and hydrogen. The study estimates techno-economically feasible over-generation and net present value of the cost needed to deploy such hybrid grid-scale energy storage solutions in India currently at 2025. The novelty lies in the methodology based on an hourly reserve power criterion to evaluate the best technoeconomical renewable hybrid grid-scale energy storage solution and dispatch schedule for the current Indian electricity demand. Among the six GS-ESS combinations studied, the HESS-Li-BESS-ACAESS mix offers the best over-generation requirement (32.91%) at a cost of ₹49,672 bn (<tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$₹ 9.1 / \text{kWh}$</tex>) for 86.1 TWh total capacity. The lowest NPV option is hybrid GSHESS at <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$₹ 43,027$</tex> bn (<tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$₹ 7.89 / \text{kWh}$</tex>) for 142.16 TWh, requiring 38.91% over-generation. This study thus, offers valuable insights into India's grid-scale energy storage mix thereby, helping policymakers reassess and plan hybrid grid-scale energy storage policies for 2030 and beyond.

title: Mathematical Modeling and Drying Kinetics in Thermal Energy Storage-Assisted Greenhouse Solar Dryer
authors: Vaibhav Saxena, Kartik Sanjeev Dubey, Vinay Arya, Abhinav Goyal, Chirodeep Bakli
publish: 2025 IEEE 4th International Conference on Smart Technologies for Power, Energy and Control (STPEC)
abstract: Integrating renewable technology in industry is a global challenge that can help us achieve energy security. Solar energy is the leading renewable technology, and this study examines its use in Indian solar drying industries. This study examines how greenhouse cover materials and location affect drying time. The addition of a low-temperature sand battery as a thermal energy storage (TES) system improves drying performance by allowing 24/7 operation. To predict system performance, a comprehensive mathematical model was developed integrating a greenhouse effect model, radiation heat transfer, and product-specific drying kinetics. Glass had the best thermal properties of the four materials simulated alongside Polycarbonate, Bubbled Polyethylene (BPE), and Low-Density Polyethylene (LDPE) Film, and it dried fastest at 26.16 hours. Overall drying efficiency correlated directly with materials' thermal performance, establishing a clear costefficiency trade-off and providing a quantitative basis for material selection.

title: Cross Diagonal Pattern Reconfiguration for Multilevel Inverter Fed Two-Stage PV Water Pumping System
authors: A Hemanth Kumar Raju, Mikkili Suresh, Venugopal Reddy Barry, G Harish Kumar Varma, Bss Santosh
publish: 2025 IEEE 4th International Conference on Smart Technologies for Power, Energy and Control (STPEC)
abstract: PV based water pumping system (WPS) is gaining importance in remote aquaculture applications in absence of power grid. Partial shading is inevitable problem in those applications due to shrubs, bushes, trees and bird droppings which drastically reduces the power output and increase the payback period. To analyze this issue, a two-stage PV WPS is considered for various partial shading conditions. To mitigate this, a cross diagonal pattern reconfiguration approach is used to enhance the power output under partial shading conditions. The boost converter with <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathrm{P} \& \mathrm{O}$</tex> is used to track the maximum power point. Further, a V/f control with carrier-based space vector modulation (CBSVM) is used to control the multilevel inverter fed induction motor for better total harmonic distortion (THD) and effective DC bus utilization. The entire system is designed and simulated in MATLAB for three shading conditions. The obtained results enhance the system performance during all the cases.

title: Energy Efficient Control of Photovoltaic Integrated Low Voltage Distribution Network
authors: Anuradha Tomar, Lakshya Bhatnagar
publish: 2025 IEEE 4th International Conference on Smart Technologies for Power, Energy and Control (STPEC)
abstract: This paper proposes an energy-efficient control strategy for a photovoltaic (PV) integrated low-voltage (LV) distribution network. It aims for enhanced energy efficiency with grid stability and system safety. The network architecture includes a dual-mode control scheme that enables smooth transitions between Maximum Power Point Tracking (MPPT) and Voltage Control Mode, based on real-time grid voltage conditions. When the grid voltage is higher than a predetermined voltage level, the system restricts PV extraction by switching to Voltage Control Mode to prevent possible overvoltage situations. Conversely, it employs MPPT to extract maximum solar power with the Perturb & Observe algorithm when the LV distribution network is in power-deficient mode. A cascaded control loop is utilized with proportional-integral (PI) controllers; the outer loop controls the target PV voltage, while the inner loop regulates the DC bus current. The generated reference signals are used to modulate the duty cycle of a DCDC converter with Pulse Width Modulation to provide efficient energy transfer. The proposed solution greatly enhances the operational flexibility of the LV distribution network under varying solar insolation and grid conditions, providing an extensible and programmable solution for implementation using PLC or embedded systems.

title: SoC Estimation of Li-Ion Batteries Using Golden Jackal Algorithm Tuned Support Vector Machine
authors: Saneep K, Sundareswaran K, P Srinivasa Rao Nayak, Sishaj P Simon, Josephine R L, Mithun T
publish: 2025 IEEE 4th International Conference on Smart Technologies for Power, Energy and Control (STPEC)
abstract: This paper proposes a Golden Jackel Optimized (GJO) Support Vector Machine (SVM) algorithm towards the State of Charge (SoC) estimation of Li-ion batteries. All five hyperparameters of the SVM algorithm are optimally tuned using Golden Jackal Optimization. The proposed approach is evaluated for various publicly available driving cycles namely, LA92, HWFET, and PDMHC. The first 70% of each dataset is employed for the optimization of hyperparameters and the remaining 30% is used for the testing. Then the SVM algorithm with optimally tuned hyperparameters is then employed for the SoC estimation of same driving cycle at different temperatures. The performance indices namely RMSE, MAE, MSE, MAPE, and <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$R^{2}$</tex> are computed and presented. Further, the percentage error in SoC is found to be below 2.5% for all test situations. The presented results demonstrate that the proposed approach is a promising tool for SoC estimation of Li-ion batteries.

title: Critical Voltage Stability Index Based μPMU Placement in Active Distribution Network
authors: Koushik Mandal, Manas Mukherjee, Biman Kumar Saha Roy
publish: 2025 IEEE 4th International Conference on Smart Technologies for Power, Energy and Control (STPEC)
abstract: This paper addresses the optimal <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\boldsymbol{\mu}$</tex> PMU placement problem by utilizing the Critical Voltage Stability Index (CVSI) of the nodes in the original network. CVSI is defined as the ratio between the deviation of a node's actual voltage from its critical voltage and the minimum critical voltage. A weak bus is a part of the power network where voltage is less stable and more likely to drop significantly if the system is disturbed or heavily loaded. Identifying weak buses is crucial for maintaining voltage stability and placing devices like PMUs or <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\boldsymbol{\mu}$</tex> PMUs for monitoring and control. So, the buses with lower values of the CVSI index are the weakest buses. Therefore, micro-PMU should be placed directly on those buses because they need direct monitoring by microPMU devices. The algorithm is modeled by considering three distinct scenarios. First, the simulation is performed under normal conditions for <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mu$</tex> PMU placement using the CVSI index. Second, a single <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mu$</tex> PMU outage scenario is analyzed. Finally, the method is applied within Zero Injection Bus (ZIB) modeling operations. In all three cases, the proposed algorithm is tested on the IEEE 33-bus system. The proposed method requires only 2 additional <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mu$</tex> PMUs, representing just an 18.18 % increase over existing approaches in the literature to achieve complete system observability under normal operation conditions to monitor the unstable buses directly. In the second scenario, complete system observability is achieved without the need for any additional <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\boldsymbol{\mu}$</tex> PMUs. For the last case, it requires <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{2}$</tex> micro-PMUs extra, which is <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{2 2. 2 2 \%}$</tex> more than the existing literature.

title: Na-IRSTD: Enhancing Infrared Small Target Detection via Native-Resolution Feature Selection and Fusion
authors: Qian Xu, Chi Zhang, Qiming Zhang, Xi Li, Haojuan Yuan, Mingjin Zhang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Geoscience and Remote Sensing
abstract: Infrared small target detection (IRSTD) faces the inherent challenge of precisely localizing dim targets amid complex background clutter. While progress has been made, existing methods usually follow conventional strategies to downsample features and discard small targets’ details, resulting in suboptimal performance. In this paper, we present Na-IRSTD, a native-resolution feature extraction and fusion framework for IRSTD. This framework elegantly incorporates native-resolution features to preserve subtle target cues, overcoming the resolution limitations of existing infrared approaches and significantly improving the model’s ability to localize small targets. We also introduce an effective token reduction and selection strategy, which selects target patches with high accuracy and confidence, boosting the low-level details of the feature while effectively reducing native-resolution patch tokens compared to dense processing, thereby avoiding imposing an unbearable computational burden. Extensive experiments demonstrate the robustness and effectiveness of our token reduction and selection strategy across multiple public datasets. Ultimately, our Na-IRSTD model achieves state-of-the-art performance on four benchmarks.

title: Design of a Multi-Angle Multi-Depth Flow Phantom for Ultrasound Flow Calibration
authors: Piero Tortoli, Francesco Lagonigro, Alessandro Ramalli, Giulio Bonciani, Billy Y. S. Yiu, Alfred C. H. Yu
publish: IEEE Transactions on Ultrasonics
abstract: Ultrasound flow phantoms are essential tools for the development of new flow imaging algorithms and for performing quality assurance in medical imaging. A comprehensive system calibration, from shallow to deep flow regions, is increasingly needed to test novel velocity imaging methods. This study describes the design, implementation, and testing of a novel multi-angle, multi-depth flow phantom. Such key features are achieved by offsetting the two flow channels from the surface and incorporating acoustic wedges into the design. Our experiments show that the phantom was effective in evaluating the accuracy of Doppler velocity measurements attainable by sonicating vessels located at different depths with a commercial linear array probe whose acoustic lens was focused on elevation at 20 mm. An experimental multigate spectral Doppler analysis highlights the following behavior: 1) although the F-number was kept constant, spectral broadening increased with depth; 2) mean velocity was increasingly underestimated at greater depths. Both effects were significantly reduced by repeating the experiments with a custom-modified version of the same probe, not including any elevation lens, i.e., characterized by an approximately constant elevation beamwidth over the depth range of interest. Using the novel multi-depth flow phantom, it was thus possible to demonstrate experimentally that spatial velocity gradients determine performance drops that are not compensated by dynamic focusing, and appropriate arrays must be used when large depth ranges have to be investigated.

title: Enhancing Cognitive Apprenticeship Through Generative AI: An Empirical Study of Student Perceptions and Learning Outcomes

title: Confined Space Underwater Positioning Using Collaborative Robots: A Proposed System_supp1-3683271.mp4
authors: Xueliang Cheng
publish: N/A
abstract: Positioning of underwater robots in enclosed or congested aquatic environments remains unsolved for field operations. Existing systems are generally suited for use in large open marine environments, not the enclosed and congested aquatic facilities common to industrial settings. In such environments, existing systems can suffer from strong echoes, multipath effects, poor coverage, and the reliance on new infrastructure and an assortment of features. Accurate and readily deployable positioning is a prerequisite for performing repeatable autonomous missions. The Collaborative Aquatic Positioning (CAP) system presented in this article uses a mixture of collaborative robotics and sensor fusion to solve this problem. The proposed positioning system is deployed in a large water tank, and repeatable autonomous missions are performed. Experimental results show that the system achieves real-time performance with mean Euclidean distance (MED) errors of 85.5–123.4 mm.

title: New Sphere-Packing Bounds for Finite-Blocklength Coding over Additive Noise Channels
authors: Kaixuan Bao, Wei Xu, Xiaohu You, H. Vincent Poor
publish: IEEE Transactions on Information Theory
abstract: Ultra-reliable low-latency communication (uRLLC) is of increasing importance in the evolution of 5G and beyond wireless communication networks. Since the communication latency is bounded by the blocklength of codewords, channel coding theory at finite blocklength is important in the design and analysis of uRLLC systems. Based on the concept of sphere-packing introduced by Shannon in 1959, in this paper, we introduce a new codebook design, namely the volume-uniform random coding codebook, and derive a corresponding achievability result in closed form. Comparing to conventional sphere-packing codebook designs where all codewords are located on the surface of the sphere, the proposed design allows codewords to lie inside the sphere, which provides greater freedom in the codebook. A concise and explicit approximation bound is obtained as ϵ ≈ exp { −n[E0(P) − R + O( 1/n )] } , where ϵ is the decoding error probability, n is the blocklength, P is the signal-to-noise ratio (SNR), and E0 is a simple explicit function of only P. It is shown by both simulation and analysis that the derived bounds are much tighter compared to existing bounds, especially when the blocklength is short, e.g., n ≤ 100.
