title: SSDiff: A Contrast-Free Virtual LGE Generator for Acute Myocardial Infarction with Joint Segmentation via Diffusion Model
authors: Jing Qi, Xiuzheng Yue, Miao Hu, Xin Wen, Yinyin Chen, Hang Jin, Chengyan Wang, Tao Li, Kunlun He
publish: IEEE Journal of Biomedical and Health Informatics
abstract: Myocardial infarction (MI) remains a major cause of death and disability. Although late gadolinium enhancement (LGE) cardiac MRI is the reference for assessing myocardial viability, it requires contrast injection, complex protocols, and added cost. Prior virtual LGE approaches-mostly GAN-based-mainly use cine or T1 mapping and ignore T2-weighted short-tau inversion recovery (T2-STIR), which is highly sensitive to edema in acute MI. They also typically require manual post-hoc delineation of infarcts. We propose SSDiff (Synthesis joint Segmentation Diffusion), a multitask conditional diffusion framework that synthesizes contrast-free virtual LGE from routine cine + T2-STIR for acute infarct assessment and simultaneously segments myocardium, ventricular blood pool, and infarct. SSDiff introduces a feature-disentangled attention module that isolates sequence-specific cues to steer the diffusion process, and a cross-fusion module that aligns synthesis and segmentation decoders for mutual optimization. Evaluated on a multi-center, multi-vendor dataset of 409 subjects (2,177 aligned cine-T2-STIR-LGE triplets), SSDiff yields significant gains in synthetic image quality and downstream segmentation accuracy over strong baselines. Beyond serving as a clinically feasible alternative when LGE is unavailable or contraindicated, SSDiff also generates paired image-mask samples that augment LGE-scarce training, highlighting its practical utility and translational potential. Code is available at: https://github.com/QijingGJ/SSDiff.

title: Time-Frequency Feature Analysis of Underwater Borehole Blasting Shock Wave Based on CEEMDAN-VMD-SST
authors: Linna Li, Mengru Xue, Haohao Tao, Xiaoxiao Xu, Leibin Li
publish: IEEE Access
abstract: To address the challenge of extracting clear time-frequency features from complex shock waves in underwater borehole blasting, this study proposes an integrated CEEMDAN-VMD-SST method. The original signal is first adaptively decomposed using Complete Ensemble Empirical Mode Decomposition with Adaptive Noise (CEEMDAN), followed by refined noise component processing through Variational Mode Decomposition (VMD). High-resolution time-frequency analysis is then achieved via the Synchrosqueezed S-Transform (SST). Experimental results demonstrate superior performance over conventional methods, with an 18.90% increase in SNR, a 41.98% reduction in reconstruction error, a 1.67% higher correlation coefficient, and a 4-fold improvement in time-frequency energy concentration. Ke y findings reveal that blasting scale dominantly affects energy distribution, with three-hole millisecond-delay blasting exhibiting significantly lower energy concentration and more dispersed time-frequency patterns than single-hole initiation. Peak pressure attenuation also varies substantially across blasting methods. This approach provides a robust tool for dynamic effect identification, parameter optimization, and safety assessment in blasting engineering.

title: When CNNs Truly Outperform PCE on Source Camera Attribution?
authors: A. Montibeller, M. Masciopinto, F. Pérez-González, G. Boato
publish: IEEE Access
abstract: Source camera attribution is a central problem in multimedia forensics, traditionally addressed using the Peak-to-Correlation Energy (PCE) statistic. A recent study by Mandelli et al. claimed that trainable CNN-based detectors substantially outperform the PCE. In this work, we show that this conclusion is affected by a methodological issue: the evaluation setup adopted for the PCE places it at an inherent disadvantage, leading to an unfair performance comparison. After clarifying the correct use of the PCE detector and re-establishing a fair baseline, we introduce two improvements—a weighted cross-entropy loss to mitigate class imbalance, and a finetuning procedure that better reflects real-world scenarios—to enhance the CNN-based approaches. With these enhancements, some of the architectures proposed by Mandelli et al. indeed surpass a properly implemented PCE across a broader range of operating conditions. The gains are particularly pronounced for medium and large test image crop sizes.

title: Time-frequency spectrum analysis and CNN for automatic radar emitter signal recognition under impulse noise
authors: Yong Guo, Feng Lan, Lidong Yang
publish: IEEE Sensors Journal
abstract: Radar emitter signal recognition, as a critical component of modern electromagnetic spectrum sensing systems, plays a pivotal role with significant strategic importance and practical value in the domains of national defense, military applications, and civilian technology. Impulse noise, characterized as a typical non-Gaussian noise, these existing radar emitter signal recognition methods with Gaussian assumption exhibit a significant degradation or even failure in the presence of impulse noise. To address this issue, this paper proposes a high-precision automatic recognition method for radar emitter signal under impulse noise. Firstly, an impulse noise suppression technique based on outlier detection algorithm is developed, and a comprehensive analysis of the noise suppression capabilities for five outlier detection algorithms is conducted. Secondly, multisynchrosqueezing transform (MSST) is employed to transform the denoised signal into a time-frequency image, and fuzzy C-Means (FCM) clustering is utilized to further eliminate redundant noise. Finally, a dataset is constructed using the time-frequency images of radar emitter signal, and convolutional neural network (CNN) is trained to establish the intelligent recognition model of radar emitter signals. The simulation results indicate that, compared to existing nonlinear transform methods, the impulse noise suppression technique based on outlier detection demonstrates superior noise suppression performance, and effectively enhances the time-frequency characteristics of radar emitter signals under impulse noise. Moreover, under an impulse noise with a GSNR≥ −3 dB, the proposed signal recognition method achieved an average PSR of 100% for six signals. This outcome confirms that the method is capable of accurately recognizing radar emitter signals in the presence of impulse noise, thereby successfully addressing the challenge of unreliable signal recognition under such environment.

title: Chemo-Hydrodynamic Transceivers for the Internet of Bio-Nano Things
authors: Shaojie Zhang, Ozgur B. Akan
publish: IEEE Internet of Things Journal
abstract: The Internet of Bio-Nano Things (IoBNT) requires mobile nanomachines that navigate fluidic environments while exchanging molecular signals under external control. We propose a chemo-hydrodynamic transceiver for catalytic Janus particles in which a single optical input jointly controls molecular emission and self-propulsion, unlike standard models that decouple communication from mobility. The resulting stochastic channel contains an actuation-dependent variance term scaling as I4, which creates a non-monotonic reliability trade-off. Stronger actuation increases emission but also amplifies motion-induced fading. In a reduced single-link setting, exact-BEP analysis reveals a finite optimal actuation level, and comparison with Brownian-mobility baselines shows that neglecting active motility can markedly underestimate BEP, especially at high actuation and short link distances. These results provide physical-layer guidance for mobility-aware actuation and link design in IoBNT.

title: Multi-UAV Resource Allocation and Trajectory Planning via Hierarchical Coalition Game for Radar Network Suppression
authors: Ruiqing Han, Tianxian Zhang, Baozhu Hu, Caipin Li
publish: IEEE Transactions on Aerospace and Electronic Systems
abstract: This paper investigates the problem of resource allocation and trajectory planning for multi-unmanned aerial vehicle (UAV) suppressive jamming. A system model is established, which includes a motion model to describe the trajectories of UAVs and the high-value target (HVT), a resource allocation model to represent beam and power allocation of UAVs, and a radar detection model to quantify the detection probability under suppressive jamming. A utility function is designed as a weighted sum of multiple reward and cost terms, and the optimization problem is formulated as a mixed-integer programming (MIP) model that maximizes the utility subject to motion dynamics and resource allocation constraints. To solve this problem, a hierarchical coalition formation game–particle swarm optimization (CFG-PSO) algorithm is proposed, which alternately iterates between CFG-based jamming beam allocation and PSO-based power allocation and trajectory planning. Furthermore, potential game theory is employed to prove that the constructed CFG converges to a Nash-stable coalition partition. Simulation results demonstrate that the proposed algorithm exhibits adaptability to various threat-region configurations, UAV initial positions, and HVT maneuvers, and outperforms traditional fixed allocation and trajectory strategies.

title: Outflanking bandwidth limitations for Terahertz generation in silicon metasurfaces
authors: Andrea Tognazzi, Davide Rocco, Paolo Franceschini, Alfonso Carmelo Cino, Costantino De Angelis
publish: IEEE Photonics Technology Letters
abstract: Terahertz (THz) radiation is of utmost interest for applications ranging from communications to safety and biological spectroscopy. However, the generation of THz frequencies above 5 THz is challenging due to absorption in crystals and pump bandwidth requirements. Here, a silicon-based metasurface, featuring resonant modes in the near-infrared (NIR), is employed as nonlinear platform for generation of THz radiation via degenerate four wave mixing process. We show that it is feasible to achieve on demand frequency generation by sweeping the central wavelength of a visible narrowband pulse and keeping the NIR pump beam unaltered, thus avoiding the need of broadband pump pulses. The usage of a metasurface allows to drastically reduce the absorption in the THz region. Our results pave the way to broadband spectroscopy in the THz region.

title: A Novel Multitask and High-Generalization Method for Variable-Length Complex Power Quality Disturbances Signals Classification
authors: Yi Luo, Kaicheng Li
publish: IEEE Transactions on Industrial Informatics
abstract: Against the backdrop of renewable energy integration and the large-scale integration of power electronic devices into power systems, the coupling of power quality issues within the system has become increasingly complex, leading to the formation of complex power quality disturbances (PQDs) characterized by the combined occurrence of multiple disturbances. Reliable and effective identification of disturbance types forms the foundation for the classified mitigation strategies of PQDs. This article proposes a data processing method, the variable-length distribution transfer field (VLF), which processes variable-length PQDs signal inputs, overcoming the limitations of fixed-length signal processing. VLF can process variable-length PQDs signals in a single pass, enhancing the algorithm’s tolerance to fluctuating computation times and enabling effective utilization of signals of arbitrary lengths. It thereby resolves the discontinuity issues caused by signal overwriting or loss due to fixed-length constraints. Concurrently, targeting the characteristics of PQDs, this article designs an edge-enhanced encoding method capable of achieving enhanced boundary discrimination and noise-robust encoding for disturbances. Furthermore, this article proposes a high-generalization self-balanced multitask classification architecture that enables loss balancing among different tasks within a multilabel and multitask classification framework. Through the above optimization, simulation results under 30 dB to 50 dB noise environments and twentyfold downsampling intensity demonstrate a classification accuracy of 99.57%. Meanwhile, experimental simulations combined with real-world grid validations still achieve a high accuracy of 99.43%. The classification algorithm exhibits strong robustness and generalization capability under highly downsampling and noisy conditions.

title: Dynamic Event-Based Fuzzy Quantized Fault-Tolerant Control for Cascaded ODE-Belt Systems With Actuator Failures and Quantization
authors: Yukan Zheng, Xiangqian Yao, Yu Liu
publish: IEEE Transactions on Fuzzy Systems
abstract: In this article, an adaptive boundary quantized fault-tolerant control scheme is developed for cascaded systems characterized by an axially moving belt and ordinary differential equations (ODEs). For the cascaded systems under investigation, the output of actuator dynamics serves as the input signal of the axially moving belt, such that the boundary control involving states of two subsystems is designed for vibration suppression. To mitigate the jittering during signal quantization, a sector-bounded quantizer is implemented. Moreover, the uncertainties in the actuator dynamics are approximated using fuzzy logic systems. The mixed effects of actuator failure and quantization are estimated via the bounded estimation approach, smoothing function, and adaptive technique. A dynamic event-triggered mechanism is introduced, incorporating an auxiliary dynamic variable to optimize communication resources. Based on this, a fuzzy event-based quantized fault-tolerant controller is developed for cascaded ODE-belt systems. Using the Lyapunov function method, the semi-global uniform boundedness of the closed-loop signals is guaranteed. Finally, simulation studies are conducted to validate the proposed control scheme.

title: Achieving Information Equilibrium: An Integrated Economic-Sociotechnical-Institutional Framework for Governing Artificial Intelligence Asymmetries
authors: Siddharth Nandagopal
publish: IEEE Transactions on Technology and Society
abstract: Artificial intelligence systems now scores credit, routes trucks, and curates news, yet their decisions remain largely inscrutable to users, regulators, and even the firms that deploy them. Such opacity creates information asymmetries that invite principal–agent conflicts, adverse selection, and moral hazard, undermining both market efficiency and public trust. Existing remedies are fragmented: information-economics studies prescribe incentives but overlook design; sociotechnical research clarifies human–AI interaction but neglects market power; and institutional work codifies norms without detailing engineering trade-offs. This paper weaves those strands into a single AI Information-Equilibrium Framework (AIIEF). The framework integrates three governance layers. At the micro layer, explainable dashboards, model cards, and user-training loops reveal how an algorithm works and calibrate reliance. At the meso layer, liability clauses, reciprocal data trusts, and tiered transparency labels align incentives so that actors share and use information truthfully. At the macro layer, risk-tiered regulation, auditable standards such as ISO/IEC 42001, and global principles issued by the OECD and UNESCO transform ethical aspirations into enforceable obligations. I show how AIIEF adapts across AI modalities—agentic versus tool-like, large versus small models—and deployment contexts—enterprise versus consumer. I also address common objections—gaming, innovation chill, free-riding, and superficial oversight—by coupling tiered disclosure with regulatory sandboxes, data-trust reciprocity, and decision-right-calibrated explanations. Finally, I outline a research agenda on asymmetry metrics, longitudinal capability evolution, ecosystem simulations, and institutional diffusion, positioning AIIEF as both a cumulative theory for scholars and a pragmatic blueprint for managers.

title: Constant Power Wireless Charging with Active Load Impedance Matching Network
authors: Rohan Burye, Sheron Figarado
publish: 2025 IEEE 4th International Conference on Smart Technologies for Power, Energy and Control (STPEC)
abstract: One potential solution to the problems with electric vehicles is inductive power transfer (IPT) technology. Additionally, it makes charging wire-free, aesthetically pleasing, and user-friendly. Degradation in efficiency during battery charging is one of the main issues with IPT converters. The fluctuation in equivalent battery resistance during charging is the cause of this. This research proposes a new load matching technique that tracks optimal efficiency against load variation during constant power (CP) charging by using an active impedance matching network on the secondary side. The steady-state characteristics of the proposed wireless charger topology is obtained analytically and the proposed scheme is validated on a <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$147 W$</tex> experimental setup. Furthermore, the operation of the charger during open-circuit (OC) fault on the load is discussed through simulation results. The proposed topology allows power transfer at high efficiency throughout CP charging mode for stationary charging applications.

title: Modelling of Partial Discharge Detection in Power Transformer Using ZnO Material Based Piezoelectric Sensor
authors: Sorokhaibam Nilakanta Meitei, Saibal Chatterjee
publish: 2025 IEEE 4th International Conference on Smart Technologies for Power, Energy and Control (STPEC)
abstract: Transformers are critical components in electrical power systems, and their malfunction may disrupt power supply. Both industry and research have endeavored to provide sensors and cost-effective solutions for accurate failure diagnosis. Consequently, the use of piezoelectric sensors in diagnosing partial discharge (PD) in power transformers has increased markedly. The acoustic emission piezoelectric detecting technology is favored because of its cost-effectiveness and small dimensions. This work presents a ZnO based piezoelectric sensor for PD acoustic signal detection models in power transformer. Zno is chosen here as the piezoelectric material due to its high piezoelectric coupling and its ecofriendliness; this will help promote green technology, as opposed to the other materials that were considered, some of which are hazardous while producing excellent performance. The proposed ZnO piezoelectric sensor has been validated with the well-known PZT-5H material-based piezoelectric sensor to check its PD detection capability. It is obtained the proposed ZnO material piezoelectric sensor is quite suitable for acoustic PD detection in power transformers.

title: Accurate State of Charge Estimation of Li-Ion Battery Pack in Real Driving Conditions Using Safe Reinforcement Learning
authors: Reeta, Anju Bala, Mukesh Singh
publish: 2025 IEEE 4th International Conference on Smart Technologies for Power, Energy and Control (STPEC)
abstract: Precise assessment of the state of charge (SOC) in lithium-ion batteries (LiBs) is crucial for optimizing performance, extending lifetime, and assuring safety in battery-powered automobiles and energy storage systems. Conventional model-based and exclusively data-driven methodologies often encounter difficulties in reconciling estimating precision with operational safety in dynamic and unpredictable environments. This research introduces an innovative Safe Reinforcement Learning (Safe-RL) framework combined with Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) networks for real-time SOC estimates. The Safe-RL framework, which is the Soft Actor-Critic (SAC), integrates Lyapunov-based safety restrictions to ensure that policy actions do not breach operational limitations for voltage, temperature, and SOC, while enhancing estimate performance. LSTM networks include the nonlinear temporal dependence of battery dynamics, hence improving resilience to model uncertainties and aging effects. The suggested hybrid technique enables the SAC to dynamically adjust LSTM properties during execution, hence maintaining consistent accuracy under fluctuating load and environmental circumstances. Experimental findings and simulations indicate that hybrid Safe RL with LSTM strategy attains enhanced estimate accuracy and adheres to safety regulations more effectively than traditional unconstrained RL methods. This technology is especially well-suited for applications in next-generation battery management systems that need both accuracy and real-time operating safety.

title: Hybrid Deep Learning Models for Real-Time Anomaly Detection in Electric Vehicle Systems
authors: Seema, Ashima Anand, Amit Kumar, Mukesh Singh, Prasant Singh Rana
publish: 2025 IEEE 4th International Conference on Smart Technologies for Power, Energy and Control (STPEC)
abstract: The rapid growth of electric vehicles (EV) and their extensive connectivity raises cybersecurity concerns. Our research is based on a dual deep learning (DL) models where a long short-term memory (LSTM) setup processes a time-series vehicle data, and then its output is utilized by Recurrent Neural Network (RNN). This integration enhances the anomaly detection accuracy, achieving 98.%. The results shows an effective approach for intrusion detection system, making EVs safer and more reliable. The core idea behind this study to protect the entire EV communication networks, particularly, for sensitive systems like the CAN bus.

title: Differentiation Method Based Reduction of Interconnected Hybrid Power System
authors: V. P. Sharma, Akhilesh Mathur, V. P. Singh, T. K. Bashishtha
publish: 2025 IEEE 4th International Conference on Smart Technologies for Power, Energy and Control (STPEC)
abstract: Interconnected hybrid power system is made up of a number of subsystems that operate collectively to form a higher-order system. The present research applies model minimization techniques (MMT), specifically the differentiation technique (DT) to simplify complicated hybrid power systems. In DT, the denominator and numerator of higher-order transfer function of complex hybrid power system are reduced to a lowerorder model. The complex thirteenth-order transfer function of the interconnected hybrid power system has been simplified to models of the fourth, third, and second orders. All three reduced transfer function models accurately maintain the properties of system parameters and are highly helpful for analytical study. A comparative study between the higher-order system and its reduced-order models is presented through tables and graphs. The reduced-order models exhibit stability, steady-state response, and other characteristics closely matching those of original higher-order system, validating the accuracy of the approximation.

title: Modelling and Control of Fourth-Order Zeta Converter for Electric Vehicle Charging
authors: Yasir Bashir, Shantanu Chatterjee, Farhat Manzoor, Amarendra Edpuganti
publish: 2025 IEEE 4th International Conference on Smart Technologies for Power, Energy and Control (STPEC)
abstract: This paper presents a Modelling and Control of a Fourth-Order Zeta Converter for Electric Vehicle Charging. The Zeta converter, a fourth-order dc-dc power converter, operates in both boost and buck modes, making it versatile for EV charging applications. The converter's architecture comprises two inductors and two capacitors, which facilitate efficient energy transfer while maintaining stable output characteristics. In this study, a dynamic model of the Zeta converter is developed, using the state-space averaging (SSA) technique, which is essential for designing an effective closed-loop control system. To achieve the desired constant current-constant voltage(CC-CV) charging profile, a proportional-integral (PI) controller is implemented, regulating the output voltage based on the control-to-output transfer function obtained from the modeling process. The performance of the proposed system is evaluated through simulations conducted in the MATLAB/Simulink, where closed-loop responses for both resistive and battery load are examined. The results demonstrate excellent steady-state performance and dynamic performance under all operating conditions.

title: A Modified Smith Predictor Approach for Controlling Unstable Cascade Processes
authors: Prabir Singha, Sudipta Chakraborty, Vipin Chandra Pal
publish: 2025 IEEE 4th International Conference on Smart Technologies for Power, Energy and Control (STPEC)
abstract: This manuscripts introduces a Modified Smith Predictor (MSP) scheme for the controlling unstable cascade processes, effectively mitigating the inherent limitations of conventional approaches in dealing with process gain variations and time-delay effects. The suggested cascade control design is analytically formulated within the Internal Model Control (IMC) framework, where the IMC parameters are tuned using maximum sensitivity analysis to achieve optimal performance. The overall configuration consists of a PI-type inner-loop controller, a PD-type stabilizing controller for disturbance rejection and an outer-loop PI controller to ensure closed-loop stability. Simulation investigations demonstrate that the proposed approach delivers significant improvements in standard performance measures, including IAE, ITAE, and ISE, when compared with existing design techniques.

title: Interval Modelling and Model Order Reduction of WTG Connected Power System
authors: Salik Ram Dewangan, P.D. Dewangan, V.P. Singh
publish: 2025 IEEE 4th International Conference on Smart Technologies for Power, Energy and Control (STPEC)
abstract: In this research article, interval modelling of the wind turbine generator (WTG) connected power system is presented by considering <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\pm 10 \%$</tex> variation in system parameters. This variation of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\pm 10 \%$</tex> is considered to incorporate the effect of change in system parameters. The variations in fixed coefficients of the system are depicted by lower bounds and upper bounds to represent the minimum and maximum deviations, respectively. To analyze the effect of the uncertainties interval, modelling is very effective. With the help of interval modelling, the uncertainties involved in the power system are defined in term of interval model of power system. Additionally, the given third-order transfer function of the WTG-connected power system model is reduced to first-order and second-order systems, respectively, via the direct truncation method. Time domain specifications (TDSs) for the lower and upper bounds of the reduced-order transfer function are given, together with a plot of the impulse and step response. The outcomes demonstrate the usefulness and efficacy of the suggested approach.

title: Comparative Analysis of Power Performance Enhancement in Solar PV Systems Using Phase Change Materials Across Distinct Indian Climate Zones
authors: Nagaraju Jadi, Kalpitkumar Solanki, Mohammed Rehan Raza, Ishika Agarwal, Chirodeep Bakli
publish: 2025 IEEE 4th International Conference on Smart Technologies for Power, Energy and Control (STPEC)
abstract: In high solar irradiance regions, photovoltaic (PV) panels suffer from efficiency losses due to excessive heat buildup, as a significant portion of incident solar radiation is converted into thermal energy. This temperature-induced degradation, often resulting in a <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{0. 5 \%}$</tex> drop in efficiency per °C rise, is particularly concerning in India's diverse climatic zones. Despite the widespread adoption of PV technology, limited attention has been given to passive thermal regulation strategies tailored to regional climates. This study investigates the integration of Phase Change Materials (PCMs) with PV panels to mitigate thermal losses and enhance annual energy yield. Using location-specific meteorological data, simulations were conducted for various climate zones across India, including hot deserts (Kutch), continental zones (Delhi), high-altitude cold deserts (Leh), mild highaltitude regions (Ooty), and coastal areas (Kolkata). The results demonstrate that the use of optimally selected PCM melting temperatures can improve PV energy output by over nearly 33% in hot deserts, 30% in continental climates, 1.6% in cold deserts, 20% in mild hill stations, and in coastal cities. These findings highlight the potential of PCM-enhanced PV systems as a climateadaptive and passive solution to improve solar energy performance in thermally challenging environments.

title: Electricity Theft Detection Model Using CNN-BiLSTM with Attention Mechanism in Smart Grid Environment
authors: Ravivarma Kamireddy, Lokeshgupta Bhamidi, Ramanjaneya Reddy U
publish: 2025 IEEE 4th International Conference on Smart Technologies for Power, Energy and Control (STPEC)
abstract: Electricity theft is a severe non-technical losses for electricity provider, as it reduces supply quality, forces legitimate consumers to pay higher bills, and negatively impacts on the economy. The development of electricity theft detection models play a major role in identification of electricity theft consumers. The main issue with current anomaly detection models are the misclassification of imbalanced energy consumption data, high false positive rates and overfitting issues. An Intensive research is essential for the precise identification of electricity theft and for recovering significant economy losses faced by utility companies. To this end, a hybrid convolutional neural network- bidirectional long short-term memory (CNN-BiLSTM) with attention mechanism based deep learning model for anomaly detection is proposed in this work. The proposed model addresses the data imbalance problem by employing two data balancing approaches: K-means Synthetic Minority Over-sampling Technique (K-SMOTE) and K-means Adaptive Synthetic Sampling (K-ADASYN). The proposed model utilizes real time data I.e, State Grid Corporation of China (SGCC) to analyze and address electricity theft, and also it compares with different classification and deep learning based detection models such as Support vector machine (SVM), Logistic regression (LR), Long shortterm memory (LSTM), Bidirectional long short-term memory (BiLSTM), Convolutional neural network (CNN), and hybrid models such as CNN-LSTM, CNN- BiLSTM, CNN-LSTMAttention mechanism. To verify the proposed model different performance metrics are considered such as precision, recall, F1 score, accuracy, Matthews Correlation Coefficient (MCC), and Area Under Curve (AUC). From the results, it is clearly evident that the proposed CNN-BiLSTM-Attention mechanism with Kmeans ADASYN has better accuracy of 95%, F1 score of 95%, Recall of 95%, Precision of 95%, MCC of 90% and AUC of 95% When compared with other balancing approach and different algorithms.

title: A Novel THF-NLFXLMS Control Approach for PMSG-Integrated Three-Phase Microgrid Intended to G2V and V2G EV Charging Infrastructure
authors: Sanjay Kumar, Shailendra Kumar, Bhim Singh
publish: 2025 IEEE 4th International Conference on Smart Technologies for Power, Energy and Control (STPEC)
abstract: This paper presents a hybrid renewable energy-based micro-grid charging infrastructure for electric vehicle (EV), incorporating a three-phase grid-tied solar photovoltaic array (SPVA) and a permanent magnet synchronous generator (PMSG) driven by a wind turbine. A key contribution is the development of a control scheme for grid side voltage source converter (GSVSC) to regulate the common DC bus voltage at 400 V and ensure dynamic power sharing between sources using tangential hyperbolic function nonlinear filtered X-least mean square (THF-NLFXLMS) controller as a indirect adaptive filter. The system employs maximum power point tracking (MPPT) to optimize energy extraction from both solar and wind sources. Proposed control strategy includes primary and secondary control to improve grid integration, DC bus voltage regulation, and power quality under IEEE 519 standard. Field oriented control (FoC) is applied to the machine side voltage sources converter (MSVSC) for efficient PMSG operation. Proposed control approach applied to DC voltage regulation, grid power integrated charging infrastructure, and hybrid operating modes. It ensures reliability microgrid operation and enables an ample amount of control. Complete system is modelled in MATLAB/Simulink, and simulation results validate effectiveness of proposed approach in both transient and steady state conditions under grid-connected and hybrid modes for grid to vehicle (G2V) and vehicle to grid (V2G) application.

title: Thermal Phase Sensitivity Reduction in Hollow-Core Photonic Bandgap Fibers Enabled by a Stress-Buffering Coating Architecture
authors: Jirong Wang, Lingke Wang, Lidong Wang, Weian Nie, Langlang Gong, Diping An, Jijun Feng, Meisong Liao, Tang Li
publish: IEEE Photonics Journal
abstract: Low thermal sensitivity of the optical propagation phase in optical fiber is critical for high-precision fiber systems, including applications such as ultra-low noise lasers, highly sensitive interferometric sensing, and time-frequency transfer. Although Hollow-Core Photonic Bandgap Fibers reduce the thermo-optic noise compared to conventional single-mode fibers (e.g., SMF-28), their ultimate performance is constrained by the polymeric coating. Thus, minimizing the coating’s mechanical influence on the fiber is crucial for enhancing thermal performance. This study investigates strain transfer between the bare fiber and outer coating layer, as well as the resulting impact on thermal sensitivity, using numerical simulations and delay interferometry experiments. We compare thermal sensitivity of fibers with a high-modulus coating to those featuring a buffered low-modulus architecture. Experimental results show that the optimized fiber achieves a sensitivity of 3.3 rad/m/°C, representing a twofold improvement over single-layer coatings with identical standard coating dimensions. This result shows good agreement with simulations. By balancing thermal stability with engineering-grade coating thickness, this work provides optical fibers with both low thermal phase sensitivity and mechanical robustness.

title: Vibration Based Incipient Inter Turn Fault Diagnosis in Permanent Magnet Synchronous Machine Using Deep Learning Approach
authors: Khushboo Ramawat, Bharat S. Rajpurohit, Adil Usman, Pramod J. Chaudhary
publish: 2025 IEEE 4th International Conference on Smart Technologies for Power, Energy and Control (STPEC)
abstract: Early and accurate detection of stator inter-turn fault (SITF)in Permanent Magnet Synchronous Machines (PMSMs) is essential for maintaining the reliability and performance of modern electric drive systems. Traditional fault diagnosis methods, based on current and flux signals, face limitations in early severity estimation due to noise sensitivity and insufficient feature extraction. This paper proposes a deep learning-based framework for estimating the severity of SITF through vibration signal analysis. Deep learning hybrid models Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) with Gated Recurrent Unit (GRU) and CNN with Long Short Term Memory (LSTM) are developed to capture both temporal and spatial characteristics of the vibration data. The models are trained on segmented time-domain signals obtained using a sliding-window technique. Among the architectures evaluated, the CNN-GRU and CNN-LSTM hybrid models achieved the highest classification accuracies of 99.00% and 98.53%, respectively, outperforming the standalone CNN, LSTM, and GRU models. Although they are more effective, hybrid frameworks have relatively low computation costs, and total training and test times of about 132.25 and 137.79 seconds respectively, which reflect its applicability in time sensitive and resource constrained applications. These findings highlight the promise of convolutional and recurrent layers coupled together to diagnose faults and the results indicate that smart and datadriven solutions can be used to add fault tolerance abilities to PMSM. The designed methodology can be utilized in PMSMbased industrial and commercial implementations, where fault diagnosis at early stages is crucial to safety of people and property.

title: Field-Oriented Control of Synchronous Reluctance Motor for Electric Vehicle
authors: Vaishnavi U M, Bindu V R
publish: 2025 IEEE 4th International Conference on Smart Technologies for Power, Energy and Control (STPEC)
abstract: This paper presents the development and execution of a Field-Oriented Control (FOC) strategy for a Synchronous Reluctance Motor (SynRM) used in electric two-wheeler applications. The study begins with the development of the vehicle's dynamic model, followed by a systematic motor selection process tailored to the performance requirements. A mathematical model of SynRM is developed and simulated in MATLAB Simulink. An open-loop drive is initially developed to analyze the basic performance, which is then extended to a closed-loop FOC scheme. The FOC strategy enhances the torque and speed response by independently controlling the d- and q-axis currents. Simulation results demonstrate effective speed regulation and improved dynamic performance, making SynRM a viable solution for lightweight electric vehicle drives.

title: Addressing Cyber-Physical Vulnerabilities in V2G and G2G Systems Through a Hybrid Recovery Framework
authors: Aditya Rastogi, Anubhav Agrawal, Mehak Bhardwaj, Sejal Gupta
publish: 2025 IEEE 4th International Conference on Smart Technologies for Power, Energy and Control (STPEC)
abstract: The accelerating global shift towards electrification and renewable integration demands smarter, more resilient grid infrastructures. The concept of Smart Grids within the field of power has led to greater advancement within the field. It utilizes several technologies for the transmission and storage of energy. Technologies such as V2G leverages electric vehicles as mobile energy storage units through bidirectional power electronics and predictive control, while G2G connects regional grids via HVDC links for macro-level load balancing. These systems increasingly rely on Digital Twins and AI/ML frameworks for real-time simulation, optimization, and autonomous control. Their cyber-physical nature exposes them to multi-vector attacks including data poisoning, model hijacking, protocol spoofing, and ransomware. This paper explores Vehicle-to-Grid and Grid-to-Grid architectures as pivotal enablers of distributed, bidirectional energy flow and grid flexibility. The authors categorize and analyze these threats across DTs and AI/ML systems, identifying vulnerabilities in federated learning, update mechanisms, and sensor integration. Existing countermeasures ranging from PKI and blockchain to twin-based anomaly detection are evaluated for effectiveness. Addressing the gap in cross-layer recovery, the authors propose a novel hybrid recovery architecture combining ML-driven detection, twin-based fallback, dynamic resource reallocation, and blockchain-backed autonomous restoration. This review establishes a critical foundation for secure, intelligent, and scalable energy systems in the V2G-G2G era.

title: Hydrogen as a Decarbonization Vector: Classification, Production Insights, and its Range
authors: Vima Mali, Kundan Kumar, Pankaj Verma
publish: 2025 IEEE 4th International Conference on Smart Technologies for Power, Energy and Control (STPEC)
abstract: Green hydrogen is increasingly recognized as a key contributor towards global carbon neutrality. It provides a clean, adaptable, and scalable option for reducing emissions, particularly in energy-intensive sectors where decarbonization remains challenging. This paper examines the expanding role of hydrogen in the transition to sustainable energy systems and its alignment with international climate goals. An overview of hydrogen production pathways namely grey, blue, and green hydrogen is presented to illustrate the technological variations and their respective environmental impacts. The study also reviews global and Indian projections for hydrogen production, with special emphasis on India's geographical and renewable energy advantages that position the country favorably for large-scale green hydrogen generation. Further, the paper identifies key sectors in India where hydrogen deployment holds significant promise, including transportation, industrial applications, and power storage. It also evaluates the cost benchmarks required to enable widespread adoption. A comparative assessment of hydrogen-powered transport across different vehicle segments is included to analyze range capabilities and energy storage considerations. Finally, the challenges associated with establishing a comprehensive hydrogen ecosystem in India are discussed. These include infrastructure development, regulatory frameworks, and economic viability. Addressing these issues will be essential for positioning hydrogen as a cornerstone of India's clean energy transition.

title: Gaussian Process Regression-Assisted Sliding Mode Control for Trajectory Tracking of Unmanned Surface Vehicles
authors: Sidharthan S, Santhakumar Mohan
publish: 2025 IEEE 4th International Conference on Smart Technologies for Power, Energy and Control (STPEC)
abstract: Unmanned surface vehicles (USVs) emerged as one of the important classes of marine vehicles, and have a wide range of applications ranging from transportation to ocean research. Model or system uncertainties and disturbances from the harsh marine environment make the operation of USVs on the water surface more challenging. The robust trajectory tracking problems of USVs are still an emerging control engineering research domain. Most of the works already conducted in the field only considered the efficacy of the control schemes without addressing the actuator characteristics like energy efficiency, chattering, and saturation. This study proposes a sliding mode control (SMC) in which disturbance estimation is enhanced by Gaussian process regression (GPR). The proposed control scheme reduces the tendency of overestimation with the help of GPR while ensuring the accuracy of trajectory tracking by SMC in the presence of model uncertainties and external perturbations. The stability of the proposed SMC-GPR control method has been confirmed using Lyapunov's theory.

title: AC-Powered Dual-Active-Bridge (DAB) Based EV Charger with Reduced Current Stress Converter Using an Adaptive DC Link
authors: Ranjeet Kumar Sah, Shashank Kurm, Shailendra Kumar
publish: 2025 IEEE 4th International Conference on Smart Technologies for Power, Energy and Control (STPEC)
abstract: This article proposes an AC-DC converter based on an AC-SEPIC topology for electric vehicle (EV) charging systems. Conventional architectures employs an active front-end converter (AFEC) for generating a constant voltage DC link to power a dual active bridge (DAB) DC-DC converter for charging the EV. This architecture suffers from high current stress in the DAB, leading to lower efficiency. The presented AC-SEPIC converter replaces the AFEC and integrates power factor correction (PFC), AC-DC conversion, and voltage regulation into a single stage. Unlike conventional systems, the modified SEPIC generates a variable DClink voltage that adapts dynamically to the battery conditions, to match the voltages of primary and secondary windings of the DAB transformer. The ACSEPIC controls the DC link voltage both above and below the peak of the input AC voltage. Thus, a load-dependent DC link can be created, which helps reduce the RMS current value by approximately 37 %. This thereby minimising conduction losses and improving the overall system efficiency. MATLAB/Simulink simulations validate the performance of the SEPIC-based system against the conventional AFEC-DAB configuration. Key advantages include reduced component count, inherent compatibility with grid voltage fluctuations, and costeffective operation.

title: Improved PEM Fuel Cell Parameter Identification Through Polynomial Regression Assisted PSO
authors: Omkar Singh, Anjan Kumar Ray, Geeta Srivastava
publish: 2025 IEEE 4th International Conference on Smart Technologies for Power, Energy and Control (STPEC)
abstract: This paper presents a hybrid parameter estimation framework for proton exchange membrane fuel cells (PEMFCs) based on the Larminie-Dicks semi-empirical model. The proposed method integrates polynomial regression with Particle Swarm Optimization (PSO) to accurately estimate six critical model parameters using reference current density-voltage data. By iteratively refining the parameter estimates through alternating regression and optimization steps, the framework achieves high accuracy in modelling the nonlinear voltage response of the PEMFC. The effectiveness of the approach is evaluated across multiple polynomial degrees, with results showing a significant reduction in mean squared error (MSE) compared to standalone optimization techniques. Additionally, a detailed analysis of convergence behavior and model fitting accuracy is provided, supporting the potential of this hybrid technique for robust and flexible PEMFC modelling applications.

title: A Unified Approach for Mitigating Loss of Excitation Using Adaptive Sliding Mode Control
authors: Ankush Sharma, Bhavesh R. Bhalja
publish: 2025 IEEE 4th International Conference on Smart Technologies for Power, Energy and Control (STPEC)
abstract: This paper proposes an Adaptive Excitation based Sliding Mode Controller for synchronous generators (SGs), aimed at improving fault resilience and enhancing dynamic stability under sudden Loss of Excitation (LOE) events, field degradation, and/or external disturbances. An adaptive nonlinear control law is formulated that incorporates real-time fault estimation, allowing dynamic adjustment to variations in the state variables of the sliding surface function. This augmented control law strategy can mitigate the chattering effect of the control input and ensures rapid convergence of the sliding surface to the equilibrium point, thereby guaranteeing system stability. In addition, a sliding mode disturbance observer is developed to continuously update the real-time fault parameter during severe disturbances, thereby improving overall controller performance. The effectiveness of the proposed scheme is verified through various test scenarios on the phase-domain model of the SG in a real-time digital simulator (RTDS). The simulation results confirm that the proposed controller reduces chattering, shortens response time, and minimizes overshoot, demonstrating its advantages over traditional control strategies.

title: Time Domain Dielectric Response of Transformer Oil-Paper Insulation Retrofilled With Natural Ester Oil
authors: Brahma Swarup Laha, Soumya Chatterjee, Sandip Kumar Ojha
publish: 2025 IEEE 4th International Conference on Smart Technologies for Power, Energy and Control (STPEC)
abstract: The present work investigates the time domain dielectric response of aged mineral oil-paper insulation retrofilled with natural ester (NE) oil under controlled environment. Real life miniature form of oil-paper insulation analogous to transformer insulation system has been prepared to perform polarization and depolarization current (PDC) measurement as it is widely adopted as a non-invasive electrical diagnostic technique to assess the insulation condition. In this study, the prepared insulation sample was initially immersed in fresh mineral oil (MO) and subjected to accelerated aging at 100°C for 400 hours, exceeding the typical thermal aging conditions of transformer insulation. The PDC measurement was carried out using the IDAX-300 to assess the dielectric response of a sufficiently aged mineral oil (MO) sample. After recording the initial dielectric response, the aged MO was entirely replaced with the fresh natural ester (NE) oil. The PDC measurements were then repeated at ambient temperatures to examine the influence of NE retrofilling. Changes in polarization and depolarization current (PDC), polarization index (PI), dielectric absorption ratio (DAR), polarization function parameters were analyzed from PDC measurements at ambient temperature for unaged MO, aged MO, and aged MO retrofilled with NE. It has been observed that different dielectric parameters showed significant variations when MO of transformer is retrofilled with NE oil.

title: Design, Analysis and Control of Dual-Mode Voltage Equalizer using Switched-Capacitor and Fly-Back Converter for Series-Connected Battery String
authors: Abhaya Kumar Sahoo, Sashwathkumar S, Lizanne Fernandes, Keerthi Saladi, V. Sankaranarayanan
publish: 2025 IEEE 4th International Conference on Smart Technologies for Power, Energy and Control (STPEC)
abstract: Conventional active voltage equalizers, such as Any-Cell-to-Any-Cell (AC2AC) and Direct-Cell-to-Cell (DC2C) topologies, exhibit significant limitations under varying imbalance conditions. AC2AC equalizers suffer from reduced equalization speed when the potential difference between cells is minimal, while DC2C equalizers become inefficient at high voltage differences due to limited energy transfer capability. To supress these limitations, this article proposes a dual-mode active voltage equalizer that seamlessly integrates both AC2AC and DC2C modes, enabling adaptive operation based on real-time cell voltage conditions. The proposed architecture dynamically switches between modes to optimize equalization speed across a wide range of imbalance scenarios, enhancing overall system efficiency and response time. A detailed simulation model is developed and tested using four series-connected lithium-ion cells to validate the significance of dual-mode strategy. Results confirm that the proposed multi-mode equalizer attains superior balancing performance compared to conventional single-mode approaches, making it highly suitable for next-generation battery management systems.

title: Efficient Method for Maximizing Power Output from a Hexagonal Solar Pyramid Structure Compared to a Planar Surface
authors: Ajit Singh Rajawat, Anurag Agarwal, Kunwar Aditya
publish: 2025 IEEE 4th International Conference on Smart Technologies for Power, Energy and Control (STPEC)
abstract: Pyramid Solar panel is a novel approach to harnessing solar energy, characterized by their unique three-dimensional structure. The solar pyramid structure features a multi-angled design that captures sunlight from various directions, significantly improving energy generation during low-angle sunlight periods like early morning and late afternoon. It is cost-effective, requires minimal maintenance due to its fixed nature, and is well-suited for compact installations. Testing and validation of a hexagonal pyramid module with a 90° vertex angle, demonstrating a 50% increase in output at 3:00 PM and a 20% increase at 1:00 PM under identical solar conditions compared to conventional planar panels. Additionally, the study compares power output data for hexagonal pyramid structures with varying vertex angles—30°, 90°, and 120°—to evaluate their performance. These results confirm the module's efficiency, reliability, and suitability for commercial applications.

title: Parametric Analysis of Grid Forming Inverter with Inertia Emulation for Microgrid Applications
authors: Raghavendra Bandaru, Sreeraj E. S., Puneet Kumar Goel
publish: 2025 IEEE 4th International Conference on Smart Technologies for Power, Energy and Control (STPEC)
abstract: Microgrids composed of inverter-based power resources have limited capability to limit frequency deviations during a disturbance due to unavailability of physical inertia. The droop control mechanism of the grid-forming inverters can be suitably modified so that it can emulate the inertial characteristics needed for frequency support during such events. However, improper values of the controller parameters, like virtual moment of inertia, damping coefficient, and low-pass filter time constant, lead to suboptimal performance of the support feature. This work evaluates the impact of these parameters on transient performance metrics such as the rate of change of frequency, frequency nadir, and settling time. Furthermore, this work conducts a parametric analysis to identify the feasible and optimal operating regions within the parameter space. The superior dynamic performance of the optimized microgrid under disturbance conditions is demonstrated through detailed numerical simulations using MATLAB/Simulink.

title: Improving Active Power Generation With Interconnected Power System with Artificial Electric Field Algorithm Supported by SMES and UPFC
authors: Nookala Shruthi, Chandan Kumar Shiva, B. Vedik, Sachidananda Sen, Shubhashish Bhakta
publish: 2025 IEEE 4th International Conference on Smart Technologies for Power, Energy and Control (STPEC)
abstract: In the interconnected power systems, controlled power generation is a very critical task specifically when sudden load is applied. To solve this problem, this paper uses UPFC with SMES, a control method to boost frequency stability in a 2-area system. Further, to boost the performance, the method implemented is AEFA, inspired by Coulomb's law, to optimize PID controller's gains. The simulation results show reduced overshoot, settling time, and frequency issues, enhancing overall system stability.

title: The Efficiency-Quality Dilemma: Real-Time HIL Validation of MPPT Algorithms for Resilient Rural PV-Grid Integration
authors: UmaVani M, Pravalika Phani D, Kishore Babu B
publish: 2025 IEEE 4th International Conference on Smart Technologies for Power, Energy and Control (STPEC)
abstract: Grid-connected solar PV systems are vital for sustainable rural electrification but suffer efficiency losses from environmental intermittency, demanding advanced Maximum Power Point Tracking (MPPT). This study leverages real-time Hardware-in-the-Loop (HIL) simulation on Typhoon HIL 402 to evaluate three MPPT algorithms-Incremental Conductance (INC), Particle Swarm Optimization (PSO), and Perturb & Observe (P&O)-under dynamic irradiance. Critically, we assess both tracking efficiency and grid power quality, revealing a paradigm shift: INC simultaneously achieves near-perfect efficiency <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$(99.98 \%)$</tex> and superior harmonic performance (lowest THD at 29 %), delivering 312 W to the grid. By contrast, PSO and P&O exhibit efficiency-power quality trade-offs (PSO: 97.43 % efficiency, 30% THD, 182 W; P&O: 96.73% efficiency, 31% THD, 145 W). Real-time HIL validation confirms INC's dual advantage in maintaining DC-link stability and power quality during transients-countering conventional trade-off assumptions. These results demonstrate INC's unique suitability for rural grid applications where harmonic limits and energy yield are co-critical. The study establishes HIL as indispensable for holistic MPPT validation in weak-grid environments.

title: Renewable Energy and Load Demand Forecasting Using Multivariate Time Series Foundation Model
authors: Nipun Bansal, Sudhindra Pai, Yashasvi Bansal
publish: 2025 IEEE 4th International Conference on Smart Technologies for Power, Energy and Control (STPEC)
abstract: Residential complexes have witnessed a substantial rise in energy consumption in recent years, heightening the concern for the environmental impact and finite nature of traditional fossil fuels. The trend has underscored the need for intelligent energy planning and increased reliance on renewable energy sources. Addressing these challenges requires accurate forecasting of both load demand and renewable energy generation, a problem characterized by high volatility, seasonal variability, and multivariate dependencies over time. This paper tackles the forecasting multivariate time series data encompassing both load consumption and renewable energy production by extending the xLSTM (Extended Long Short-Term Memory) architecture as a unified foundation model. Experimental validation is performed on a publicly available residential energy dataset from the United Kingdom. The forecasting performance is benchmarked against conventional LSTM, Gated Recurrent Unit (GRU), and transformer-based models using evaluation metrics such as Mean Absolute Error (MAE), Mean Squared Error (MSE), Root Mean Squared Error (RMSE), Mean Absolute Percentage Error (MAPE) and Coefficient of Determination (<tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{R}^{\mathbf{2}}$</tex>). Results show that the xLSTM-based model outperforms state-of-the-art methods consistently, proving its efficacy for energy-conscious residential infrastructure planning.

title: Multi-Channel LSTM Framework for Intermittent and Incipient Fault Detection & Isolation in Nuclear Reactor Core Instrumentations
authors: Neha Rani Yadav, Suryakant Gautam, Siddhartha P. Duttagupta
publish: 2025 IEEE 4th International Conference on Smart Technologies for Power, Energy and Control (STPEC)
abstract: Nuclear Power Plants (NPPs) rely on a network of sensors and actuators to ensure safe and reliable operation. Failures in these components can compromise system integrity, underscoring the need for a robust fault detection and diagnosis (FDD) framework. This study proposes a data-driven methodology using Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) networks to detect incipient-intermittent faults in sensors and bias faults in actuators. The LSTM networks, trained with a sliding time window of length <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\tau$</tex>, capture temporal dependencies in reactor dynamics to predict power and temperature outputs under nominal conditions. Residuals, computed as differences between predicted and actual measurements, serve as fault detection statistics (FDS). Fault signatures such as constant and intermittent residual slopes are analyzed to identify fault type and source. The approach is validated on a simulated Pressurized Water Reactor (PWR) core point kinetics model using 6000 time-step data samples, with 70 % used for training, 15 % for validation, and 15 % for testing. Fault scenarios, including single and simultaneous sensor-actuator faults-are introduced in the test set. The model demonstrates high sensitivity in isolating fault patterns, offering a scalable and effective FDD solution for safety-critical NPP instrumentation systems.

title: AMIGO Method Based Control Strategy for Frequency Stabilization in Marine Microgrid
authors: A. V. Waghmare, V. P. Singh
publish: 2025 IEEE 4th International Conference on Smart Technologies for Power, Energy and Control (STPEC)
abstract: The trend towards lowering greenhouse gas emissions by decreasing the use of conventional energy sources in marine power grids, as well as the substantial boost in the integration of renewable energy sources, have motivated the concept of marine microgrids. In ensuring smooth and reliable operation of marine microgrids, maintaining frequency stability plays a significant role. Considering this concern, this study provides proportional integral (PI) controller based control strategy to regulate frequency in marine microgrids. Marine microgrid considered in this study comprises of renewable energy sources, conventional sources and energy storage units. The model is approximated to first order plus dead time (FOPDT) model for simplifying analysis. The parameters obtained from FOPDT model are then exploited for tuning the PI controller parameters. Two PI controllers, implemented and analysed in this study, are tuned using AMIGO tuning criteria. To evaluate the controller performances, frequency deviation responses are plotted and to perform comparative analysis error indices based evaluation is also provided.

title: Hydrodynamic Modeling and Matrix Converter-Based Control of a PMLG for Ocean Wave Energy Harvesting
authors: Anupa Gugulothu, Sharmili Das
publish: 2025 IEEE 4th International Conference on Smart Technologies for Power, Energy and Control (STPEC)
abstract: A Permanent Magnet Linear Generator (PMLG) in conjunction with a unique matrix converter-based wave energy converter is presented in this study for an effective power conversion with fewer components and lower losses. This article presents a complete hydrodynamic model of Wave energy systems, which is coupled to a floating body and translator of the PMLG. For wave to electrical energy conversion the output of PMLG is conditioned through the matrix converter. A hydrodynamic model simulates the interaction between ocean waves and a floating body, producing translational mechanical motion. This motion is directly converted into variable-frequency electrical power by the PMLG. Due to the random nature of ocean waves, the generator output voltage and frequency are unstable and fluctuates constantly and unsuitable for direct use. To address this, a matrix converter is employed to regulate the voltage and frequency, ensuring a stable three-phase AC output compatible with grid or load requirements. Simulation results confirm the system's capability to efficiently convert wave energy into usable electrical power, demonstrating its potential for practical application in renewable energy systems.

title: Dynamic Effects on Flux Linkage Versus Current Contours in Single-Pulse-Operated High-Speed Switched Reluctance Machine
authors: Samrat Das, G. Narayanan
publish: 2025 IEEE 4th International Conference on Smart Technologies for Power, Energy and Control (STPEC)
abstract: This paper presents experimental investigations on flux linkage versus current contours of a high-speed switched reluctance machine (SRM) operating in single-pulse mode. Tests are performed at three rotor speeds <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$(10,000,20,000$</tex>, and 30,000 rpm) and at a fixed speed of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$25,000 \text{rpm}$</tex> with three DClink voltage and switching angle combinations. Results show that current extinction occurs earlier than predicted by static flux linkage characteristics-based simulations, with up to 44 % reduction in free-wheeling duration. At current extinction, significant non-zero flux linkage persists and decays gradually due to induced EMF, likely due to eddy currents at high speeds and mutual flux linkages. Cases with higher peak flux linkage show greater extinction angle advancement, shorter free-wheeling, and higher remnant flux linkage. Marked differences in voltage, flux linkage, and current waveforms between experiments and simulations indicate that dynamic effects - particularly eddy currents and possible variation in back-EMF at higher speeds are not captured by models based on static characteristics. The findings underscore the need for improved SRM electromagnetic models incorporating high-speed dynamic effects for accurate performance prediction and improved controller design.
