authors: N/A
publish: 2025 IEEE 4th International Conference on Smart Technologies for Power, Energy and Control (STPEC)

title: Countering Disturbances in Single Phase Grid Integrated PV System Under Abnormal Grid Conditions
authors: Ilka Shawl, Ikhlaq Hussain
publish: 2025 IEEE 4th International Conference on Smart Technologies for Power, Energy and Control (STPEC)
abstract: This paper deals with a rooftop solar photovoltaic (SPV) system interfaced with a single-phase grid. The double-stage topology is employed to regulate maximum power point tracking (MPPT) and the power fed to the grid. In the proposed control, the grid voltage is monitored in real time and the DC link voltage <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$(\mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{d c}})$</tex> of the voltage source converter (VSC) is adjusted accordingly. The adjustable Vdc takes the grid voltage fluctuations in the weak grid system - a persistent problem in rural India- into account. The reference <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$V_{d c}$</tex> is regulated by a proportional integral (PI) controller and leads to reduced power losses. The system is analysed for non-ideal grid conditions-voltage sags, swells, and the simulation results are added, and the hardware prototype verified the system's performance. The analysis of the proposed system falls well under the IEEE-519 standard (THD<5%) for the abnormal grid conditions.

title: Smart Controller for Energy-Efficient AHU Operation in Academic Buildings
authors: Sushil Kannan, Maya Vijayan, Thanga Raj Chelliah, Sudhakar Subhudhi
publish: 2025 IEEE 4th International Conference on Smart Technologies for Power, Energy and Control (STPEC)
abstract: This paper proposes a novel control strategy for Air Handling Units (AHUs) in educational buildings that integrates timetable-based Grey Wolf Optimizer (GWO) with camera-based gesture recognition for changing the set point temperature. This method improves the energy efficiency by controlling the switching operation of the AHUs in an optimal way based on the cooling load without compromising the occupant comfort. The proposed method was simulated in MATLAB and compared with a conventional rule-based control approach and the manual mode approach over one month period. Data of a real classroom in an academic building has been used for the analysis. The results shows that the proposed technique is having a substantial energy saving of 33.01% and the rule based technique also saves a considerable amount of 15.83% energy compared to the existing manual mode operation. These outcomes demonstrate the potential of metaheuristic optimization based control of AHUs in improving HVAC energy efficiency and user satisfaction in smart building environments. Future work will focus on integrating real-time gesture recognition with the controller and carrying out hardware implementation and testing the effectiveness in the real environment.

title: Performance Analysis of INC, VCPSO, CFPSO, CS and SSA MPPT Methods for Maximizing Power Output of Solar PV Arrays Under Dynamic Shading
authors: Abhinav Bhattacharjee, Suresh Mikkili, Gaurav Kumar
publish: 2025 IEEE 4th International Conference on Smart Technologies for Power, Energy and Control (STPEC)
abstract: This paper provides a performance analysis of four metaheuristics based MPPT algorithms - Variable Coefficient Particle Swarm Optimization (VCPSO), Constriction Factor Particle Swarm Optimization (CFPSO), Cuckoo Search (CS) and Salp Swarm Algorithm (SSA) with regards to their ability to maximize power output from solar PV (SPV) arrays under dynamically changing partial shading conditions (PSCs). Testing has also been done with the Incremental Conductance (INC) algorithm to provide a comparative baseline. Tests are performed with a Quadra Tied (QT) SPV array in Matlab/Simulink by considering four commonly encountered PSCs. The parameters considered for analysis are tracking speed, MPPT efficiency and steady state oscillation of the operating point. The study shows that in case of fast changing PSCs, conventional MPPT algorithms such as INC are more suitable than the metaheuristics based ones, while in conditions where PSCs change every few seconds or minutes, the latter are more effective at maximizing power output.

title: Feasibility and Economic Assessment of a Rooftop Solar Photovoltaic System Using Homer Pro Software
authors: Jay Rajay Tembhekar, P S Kulkarni, A Nehemiah Joshi Varma
publish: 2025 IEEE 4th International Conference on Smart Technologies for Power, Energy and Control (STPEC)
abstract: This paper presents the feasibility and economic analysis of a 58.5kWp Rooftop Solar Photovoltaic System, which is installed on roof of library building at VNIT Nagpur campus, Maharashtra in India. Using licensed version of Hybrid Optimization Model for Electric Renewable Pro software, feasibility and economic assessment is evaluated for 58.5kWp RTSPVS under two different configurations, stand-alone mode and grid-connected mode. Homer Pro is widely recognized optimization tool for designing hybrid renewable energy systems. Its selection strengthens the analysis by ensuring accurate simulation of the system configurations, cost estimations, and performance metrices, making the results both reliable and practical for real-world application.

title: Modeling and Control of a Single-Phase CHB Inverter-Based Grid-Tied EV Charging Station with a Dual Active Bridge Interface
authors: Manish Kurre, Ranjeet Kumar Sah, Shailendra Kumar
publish: 2025 IEEE 4th International Conference on Smart Technologies for Power, Energy and Control (STPEC)
abstract: In this paper, cascaded H-bridge (CHB) based grid-tied photovoltaic (PV) assisted converter for electric vehicle (EV) charging station using dual active bridge (DAB) DC-DC converter is presented. The consolidation of various energy resources and EV necessitates an effective and adaptable power management technique. A single-stage implementation of incremental conductance MPPT is used to remove the requirement for a separate boost converter stage. Here, PV arrays provide separate DC link control for ease of deployment and isolation without the need for bulky transformers at the grid end. Reference voltage control signals are generated separately for each H bridge based on a fundamental voltage reconstruction, which is further compared to identical phase-shifted triangular carrier signals. Furthermore, to regulate power transfer between the two active H-bridge stages of the DAB converter and facilitate battery charging, the design of the DAB has a current control loop that uses proportional integral (PI) controllers. The simulation results under different conditions are presented.

title: Investigation on Electric Vehicle Charging and Strategies to Mitigate its Negative Impact on Distribution Grid
authors: Laxmi Narayan, Krishan Chopra, Mukesh Kumar Shah
publish: 2025 IEEE 4th International Conference on Smart Technologies for Power, Energy and Control (STPEC)
abstract: Electric vehicles(EVs), which have unique properties such as lower noise, energy efficiency, and pollution-freeness Because of lower carbon emissions, are considered a great option for the future The industry of automobiles. When driving an electric vehicle (EV) in an urban location, the battery must be recharged at a charging station because many electric vehicles will use the charging stations at the same time. If there are few electric vehicles, the placement of charging stations may have little impact on the existing distribution system. However, as number of EVs increases, unusual characteristics of EVs' charging patterns may have a significant impact that came on distribution system (DS), which are primarily affected by the charging location of the electric vehicle. Power loss and overloading on grid could have serious consequences. Strategic planning and optimum placement of EVCS in the existing distribution system can help alleviate these impacts. This study identifies the optimum location of the EVCS in a distribution network while simultaneously analyzing the real power losses and the system voltage profile. This study presents a heuristic Particle Swarm Optimization (PSO) method to enhance the operational efficiency of the IEEE 33-bus distribution network incorporating electric vehicle charging infrastructure. The primary goal is to locate the EVCS in the best place within the existing DS, while taking into account real power losses and voltage at the system buses.

title: An Incremental MPPT-Controlled Soft-Switched LED Driver Using a PV-Fed Boost Converter for Street Lighting
authors: Banoth Chandrasingh, S Porpandiselvi, Vansh Sharma
publish: 2025 IEEE 4th International Conference on Smart Technologies for Power, Energy and Control (STPEC)
abstract: This paper presents a soft-switched LED driver configuration tailored for street lighting applications powered by photovoltaic (PV) sources. The proposed system integrates an Incremental Conductance Maximum Power Point Tracking (MPPT) technique to extract maximum energy from the PV source under a fixed irradiance level of 1000 W/m<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup>. Unlike conventional paralleled boost converters, the presented topology simplifies the magnetic design by employing a single boost inductor while maintaining Zero Voltage Switching (ZVS) for improved efficiency and reduced electromagnetic interference. The output of the soft-switched boost converter is cascaded with a buck-boost stage to regulate the LED current and enable fine dimming control. The system is evaluated under multiple dimming levels ranging from 80% to 20%. Simulation results demonstrate a high efficiency, maximum power point tracking and reduced component count, making the design well-suited for reliable and energy-efficient LED street lighting applications.

title: Impact Analysis of EV Drive Cycles and Types on the Optimal Management of Smart Homes
authors: Giriwar Chaurasia, Subho Paul, Mitresh Kumar Verma
publish: 2025 IEEE 4th International Conference on Smart Technologies for Power, Energy and Control (STPEC)
abstract: For the last few years, the transportation sector has been witnessing a paradigm shift by introducing electric vehicles (EVs) in place of combustion engine based vehicles to reduce carbon emissions. The amount of required energy during EV charging is influenced by the choice of EV and the driving cycle. Therefore, uncoordinated charging of the EVs enhance the energy cost of the owners. In this regard, this article investigates the impact of drive cycles and types on the optimal management of smart homes equipped with battery associated rooftop solar panel, a heating ventilation and air conditioning (HVAC) unit, a clothes dryer (CD), and a washing machine (WM). By analyzing EV usage patterns, battery charge levels, and charging behaviors, an intelligent framework is developed to minimize electricity expenses, and reduce peak demand. The proposed model integrates smart home energy management systems (SHEMS) with dynamic electricity pricing and renewable energy sources, enabling better load distribution. The home energy management problem is developed to minimize the energy cost and framed as a mixed integer linear programming (MILP). Simulation results indicate that incorporating drive cycle insights leads to significant cost savings. This research underscores the potential of EV-aware smart homes in achieving a more sustainable and cost-efficient energy ecosystem.

title: Techno-Economic Analysis of Integrating Optimized Hydrogen Fuel Cell as an Energy Buffer in Green Energy Microgrid
authors: Harikrishnan K, Rajesh Kumar Mahto, Pallavi Bharadwaj
publish: 2025 IEEE 4th International Conference on Smart Technologies for Power, Energy and Control (STPEC)
abstract: This paper focuses on integrating a 200 kW hydrogen fuel cell (HFC) system with community load to support a cleaner, more reliable, and cost-effective energy supply. As electricity demand continues to rise, the study explores how HFC can reduce the dependence on conventional grid power while improving sustainability. A rule-based decision algorithm is developed to decide when the HFC should operate based on real-time load and electricity price data. This study further checks the potential cost benefits of day-ahead market power planning along with HFC integration. To ensure the HFC runs at its best performance, three maximum power point tracking (MPPT) methods Perturb & Observe, Fuzzy Logic, and the bioinspired Jellyfish search algorithm are implemented and tested using MATLAB Simulink. Theoretical analysis and simulations are used to test how well a system works. This offers insights into the cost savings, operational efficiency, and future scalability of HFC-based power systems in campus environments.

title: Optimized Operational Strategy for Integrated Multi-Carrier Energy Systems
authors: Ankit Garg, K. R. Niazi, Md Fahim Ansari, S.S. Sahoo
publish: 2025 IEEE 4th International Conference on Smart Technologies for Power, Energy and Control (STPEC)
abstract: Increasing instability with energy market prices along with the intermittent characteristics of renewable energy sources creates difficulties for a multi-energy system operator to make effective decisions. To address these complexities, the current research develop a robust decision-making style based on a single max-min-max optimization approach. The model incorporates uncertainties in both electricity market prices and wind power generation. The energy hub (EH) engages with both electricity and gas markets-procuring natural gas to operate combined heat and power (CHP) units and boilers, as well as to meet direct gas demand. To mitigate the impact of electricity price volatility, an electric energy storage system is integrated. Furthermore, A proposed uncertainty budget mechanism considers both upward and downward deviations in market prices, strengthening the robustness of the system against forecasting errors. The nonlinear components of the model are linearized using practical linearization techniques. The GAMS tool is then used to solve the resulting problem. Ultrahigh temperature heating (EH) is also linked to a pwer-togas (P2G) system that skills sector coupling by converting electricity that would otherwise be wasted to hydrogen and synthetic natural gas (SNG), which links the power and gas network.

title: A Multiport Charging Station Based on Parallel Hybrid Converters with Grid and Load Unbalance Compensation
authors: Ibhan Chand Rath, Jayant Kumar, Siba Kumar Patro
publish: 2025 IEEE 4th International Conference on Smart Technologies for Power, Energy and Control (STPEC)
abstract: The medium voltage parallel hybrid converter (PHC) and dual active bridge (DAB) converter based electric vehicle (EV) multiport charging station (MCS) is proposed, which in addition is capable of providing reactive power support to the AC grid. The PHC is advantageous over the existing multilevel converter topologies in terms of number of capacitors, energy storing elements and zero voltage switching. The PHC converts the AC grid voltage into DC, which is distributed across its half bridge sub-module (HBSM) capacitors. In order to charge EV, a DAB converter is connected across each of the SM capacitor available with the PHC. However, the HBSM capacitor voltage of the PHC belonging to different phases tends to divert from reference value during uneven loading conditions (UELCs). The UELC may arise in PHC due to unbalanced EV loading on its three phases and also while compensating an unbalanced AC grid/load. This paper performs a detailed power analysis of the PHC while considering all the aforementioned unbalances and subsequently proposes a capacitor voltage control technique using zero sequence voltage (ZSV). The generalized expression of the reference ZSV is established using both the feed-back and feedforward control terms. The operating principles of the proposed PHC and DAB based EV MCS and the HBSM capacitor voltage control technique is verified using simulation results.

title: Control of Dual Transformer Triple Active Bridge with NN-Based Link Current Optimization
authors: Varri Chandra Sekhar Pavan Kumar, Harikrishnan A, Silpashree Sahu, Dipankar De, Alberto Castellazzi
publish: 2025 IEEE 4th International Conference on Smart Technologies for Power, Energy and Control (STPEC)
abstract: This paper presents an optimization of the peak link current in a dual transformer-based triple active bridge converter. The modulation technique considered for the converter is the most general penta-phase-shift(PPS) modulation. The optimization is carried out in two stages: (a) for different inner phase shifts of the common H-bridge, the individual dual active bridge optimization is carried out, and (b) the best inner phase shift for the common bridge is determined for a given power transfer profile. Different modes of operation are described, and a mathematical analysis is provided for the optimized performance. The implementation of the proposed concept is carried out through an artificial neural network (ANN). A cascaded feedforward Neural Network is used for the implementation of the optimization. The training data for optimized NN network are generated for different port voltage magnitudes and for different power transfer levels. The concept is verified through simulation and scaled-down experimental prototype.

title: Analysis and Comparison of Control Strategies for Improved Dynamic Response in DC-DC Luo Converters
authors: Riju Nandi, Arnab Ghosh, Krishna Roy
publish: 2025 IEEE 4th International Conference on Smart Technologies for Power, Energy and Control (STPEC)
abstract: This paper presents a comparative study of different control strategies applied to a DC-DC Luo converter to enhance its voltage regulation performance. The controllers investigated include PID, Type II, Type III, and Internal Model Control (IMC). Each control technique is examined under varying input voltages and load conditions to evaluate its ability to maintain stable output voltage and improve converter dynamics. The results indicate that all control methods effectively intensify the converter's performance; however, the IMC controller demonstrates superior behavior, providing a faster transient response, reduced overshoot, and excellent steady-state accuracy compared to the other controllers.

title: Matrix Converter-Fed DFIG for Variable-Speed Wind Energy Systems: A Vector Control Approach
authors: Soumyabrata Das
publish: 2025 IEEE 4th International Conference on Smart Technologies for Power, Energy and Control (STPEC)
abstract: This study proposes a vector control approach for regulating the active and reactive power of a wind turbine-driven doubly fed induction generator (DFIG) employing a direct ACAC matrix converter integrated with space vector modulation. The DFIG speed is controlled using an active power PI controller that generates the <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$q$</tex>-axis component of the rotor current in a synchronously rotating reference frame. The d-axis rotor current component is derived from a reactive power PI controller to ensure independent reactive power control. The PI controller parameters are tuned to achieve stable and satisfactory dynamic performance. The reference active power is computed from the maximum available power at a given wind speed using an online Simulink S-function implementation, enabling optimal speed tracking. Simulation results validate effective maximum wind energy extraction and precise active and reactive power regulation across varying operating conditions.

title: A New Wide-Range ZVS Enabled Single-Stage AC–AC Converter for Solid-State Transformers (SST)
authors: Rupesh Kumar Yadav, Jahangeer Ahmad Dar, Mukesh Kumar Pathak
publish: 2025 IEEE 4th International Conference on Smart Technologies for Power, Energy and Control (STPEC)
abstract: This article provides a bidirectional half-bridge series resonant high-frequency isolation AC-AC converters are becoming more popular for solid state transformer, owing to their compact size and high-power density. The proposed topology utilizes two active half-bridge circuits composed of bidirectional switches, integrated with a high-frequency transformer. A series resonant tank is linked in series with the transformer's main winding to enable soft switching. This configuration offers a simplified circuit design with fewer active switches while eliminating the need for folding and unfolding bridges and bulky electrolytic capacitors. Moreover, the proposed topology becomes less complex and more cost-effective. The control strategy, based on fundamental harmonic analysis (FHA), integrates variable-frequency modulation (VFM) with phase shift modulation (PSM). VFM enables effective zero-voltage switching (ZVS) during both step-up and step-down modes, while PSM enhances power transfer capability by minimizing rms tank current and ensuring unity power factor. The converter's operating modes are analyzed in detail. along with the derivation of conditions required for achieving soft-switching. Experimental results from a 500 W scaled-down laboratory prototype tested under steady-state conditions are used to validate the efficacy of the proposed converter.

title: VSG-Based Grid Forming Inverter Control for Solar PV and Electric Vehicles to Enhance the Frequency Stability in LFC Systems
authors: Suriya Sharif, Asadur Rahman, Abdullah Ahmad
publish: 2025 IEEE 4th International Conference on Smart Technologies for Power, Energy and Control (STPEC)
abstract: The rising integration of inverter-based resources (IBRs) into modern power systems has led to a decline in rotational inertia typically provided by conventional generation units. This reduction in inertia exacerbates frequency fluctuations, causing potential system instability and deterioration. To address this issue, grid-forming (GFM) inverters have emerged as a vital solution for grids with high renewable energy penetration. A notable feature of GFM inverters is their ability to regulate frequency actively, which enables them to sustain system frequency and efficiently handle load variations. The presented single-area LFC model comprises a conventional thermal, inertialess inverter-based solar photovoltaic (SPV) and electric vehicle (EV). This is developed and simulated in MATLAB Simulink® environment for analysis. This work incorporates grid-forming (GFM) inverter control through a virtual-synchronous generator (VSG) mechanism for SPV and EVs. This enhances inertial emulation and improves frequency stability in single-area load frequency control (LFC) systems. To optimally adjust the PID controller gains for LFC and configure network parameters, the magnetotactic bacteria optimization (MBO) technique is applied. The effectiveness and superiority of the VSG-based GFM inverter-controlled LFC system are demonstrated by comparing it with conventional LFC systems under different load variations, system uncertainties, and various levels of inverter penetration. The simulation results confirm that this approach enhances overall system performance and reliability, providing frequency support for low-inertia modern power grids during disturbances.

title: Modeling of Mazzilli ZVS Flyback Converter Coupled with Cockcroft-Walton Voltage Multiplier Using MATLAB Simulink
authors: Balanagu Sushma, Srikanth Allamsetty, Ambrish Devanshu
publish: 2025 IEEE 4th International Conference on Smart Technologies for Power, Energy and Control (STPEC)
abstract: The design and implementation of a Mazilli ZVS (Zero Voltage Switching) flyback circuit in conjunction with a Cockcroft-Walton voltage multiplier for high-voltage generation is demonstrated in this paper. The Mazzilli flyback converter produces a high voltage using a comparatively high amount of electrical power. The Cockcroft-Walton voltage multiplier (CWVM) is a device that raises the DC voltage level of an AC or pulsating DC electrical source from a low voltage level. It is composed of a voltage multiplier ladder network of capacitors and diodes to generate high voltages. A 12 V DC input is used, and an approximately 36 kV high voltage direct current (HVDC) output is produced. A pulsing DC is produced by the Mazilli ZVS flyback circuit and is multiplied by the Cockcroft-Walton circuit. This configuration leads to improved efficiency, minimum losses, and stable high-voltage output, which is applicable to various applications such as scientific experiments, electrical testing, and plasma generation. This paper explores the circuit topology, operation, and also demonstrates the theoretical analysis, provided with simulation results for achieving high-voltage outputs with a low input voltage.

title: $\alpha$-Blend MPC: A Curvature-Adaptive Dual-Weight Model Predictive Control for Autonomous Vehicle Navigation
authors: Abhishek Thakur, Venya Gupta, Aditya Joshi, Sudhansu Kumar Mishra, Subrat Kumar Swain
publish: 2025 IEEE 4th International Conference on Smart Technologies for Power, Energy and Control (STPEC)
abstract: In this paper, we address the challenge of maintaining robust lateral control of autonomous vehicles across varying speeds and road curvatures. Conventional Model Predictive Control (MPC) strategies typically rely on a fixed set of cost-function weights, which often results in a trade-off between tracking accuracy and vehicle stability. To overcome this limitation, we have introduced an <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\alpha$</tex>-Blend MPC scheme that dynamically fuses two pre-optimized cost matrices: one tailored for stability in straight-line motion and the other for agility in curved segments. The blending factor, <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\alpha$</tex>, is computed online based on the curvature of the upcoming path, enabling real-time adaptation of the control objective. Simulation results on singlelane change, serpentine, and racecourse trajectories demonstrate that the proposed controller significantly enhances both pathtracking accuracy and lateral stability compared to traditional fixed-weight MPC approaches.

title: Tensile Strength and Thermal Degradation Analysis of EPDM Composites for High Voltage Insulation
authors: Pradeepkumar P. Joy, V. Sreelekha, B. Aryanandiny, Binu Sankar
publish: 2025 IEEE 4th International Conference on Smart Technologies for Power, Energy and Control (STPEC)
abstract: Ethylene Propylene Diene Monomer (EPDM) has wide applications in high voltage systems due to its excellent electrical insulation, mechanical properties, thermal behavior and high resistance to ultraviolet radiation. In the present study, EPDM clay composites were prepared by two-roll mill mixing with different compositions of clay. Prepared samples were analyzed for thermal stability and mechanical properties. To study the thermal decomposition of EPDM and EPDM-based clay composites, thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) was carried out. The results showed that the EPDM composite with 30 phr of clay exhibited higher thermal stability.

title: Coordinated Three-Axis Linear Slide Stage Control: A Case Study
authors: R Mithun Kumar, Setti Suresh, T Narendra Reddy, Prakash Vinod, Nagahanumaiah
publish: 2025 IEEE 4th International Conference on Smart Technologies for Power, Energy and Control (STPEC)
abstract: This paper presents a coordinated three-axis linear slide stage control system for precise and reliable control over linear motions across <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathrm{X}, \mathrm{Y}$</tex>, and Z axes. The system integrates stepper motors, micro stepping drivers, rotary encoders, and microcontrollers to enable both open-loop and closed-loop control architectures. The initial phase involved implementing open-loop motion control with siemens S7-1200 PLC hardware using technology objects module and ladder logic. The functionalities such as jogging, homing, and coordinated two-axis motion control were simulated and validated on the prototype. Following that, to enable coordinated three-axis stage position control, embedded microcontroller was used for closed-loop control by integrating rotary encoders for real-time feedback and optimized to a compact PCB. The proposed low-cost embedded system provides a costeffective, modular platform with potential applications in CNC systems, robotic manipulators, and precision automation, forming a solid foundation for advanced motion control research and development.

title: Performance Investigation of CLLC and DAB Modules for Configurable 400 V and 800 V EV Charging System
authors: Shashank Dabral, Satish Belkhode
publish: 2025 IEEE 4th International Conference on Smart Technologies for Power, Energy and Control (STPEC)
abstract: The rapid growth of electric vehicles (EVs) is creating unprecedented demand for adaptable and efficient charging infrastructure. A key challenge is supporting both 400 V and 800 V battery systems, which necessitates a wide charging voltage range to ensure compatibility across different EV platforms. This paper proposes a reconfigurable power conversion architecture designed to meet this requirement. In this paper, three different module sizes as <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$3.3 ~\text{kW}, 7.5 ~\text{kW}$</tex>, and 30 kW are considered to address modular and scalable charging applications. Further, two of the most widely adopted DC-DC converter topologies are analyzed for the suitability in proposed architecture. A comprehensive design methodology is presented for the optimal selection of component values, ensuring high efficiency and reliable operation across the voltage range. Simulation results validate the proposed design approach and provide a detailed performance comparison of the different architectures.

title: Emerging Trends in Voltage Source Converters: A Topological Review on Thyristor Integration
authors: Ayushi Gupta, Shourya Sharma, Siba Kumar Patro
publish: 2025 IEEE 4th International Conference on Smart Technologies for Power, Energy and Control (STPEC)
abstract: The resurgence of thyristors used in multilevel converters in high power applications has steered a new era of modular, resilient and cost effective converter systems. This paper offers an overview of multilevel converter configurations powered by thyristors, highlighting their advancements in technology, fault tolerance mechanism, and their application within High Voltage Direct Current (HVDC) system. The incorporation of thyristors provides converters with enhanced characteristics, including remarkable voltage blocking capacity, robust operational reliability, high surge current tolerance, superior powerhandling efficiency, and minimal conduction losses. Discussed topologies offer various advantages in terms of functionality and cost unlike Modular Multilevel Converter with Half Bridge Sub Modules, which lack fault blocking capability and are costly too. This article explores emerging hybrid converter topologies combining IGBTs and thyristors, offering a comparative analysis of their strengths, drawbacks and application scope of thyristordriven multilevel converter topologies within the HVDC domain.

title: Power Grid Failures in the Renewable Era: the April 2025 Blackout in Spain and Portugal
authors: Veenol Dhiraj Naik, Krity Kumari Rao, Ankeshwarapu Sunil
publish: 2025 IEEE 4th International Conference on Smart Technologies for Power, Energy and Control (STPEC)
abstract: On April 28, 2025, a major blackout affected the Iberian Peninsula, impacting more than 60 million people in France, Portugal, and Spain. Inter-area oscillations and a sudden 2.5 GW loss of solar power was the root cause of the blackout, which caused the frequency to drop below 48 Hz. Because more than 70% of renewable energy sources are inverter-based, the system's low inertia made frequency instability worse and the protection system's reaction time was slowed down. High rate of change of frequency (RoCoF), insufficient synthetic inertia, poor relay coordination, and inefficient under-frequency load shedding are some of the main issues noted. The outage demonstrated the necessity for advanced controls and revealed vulnerabilities in traditional electrical grid protection schemes. The event made clear how urgently technical, legislative, and infrastructure changes are required to guarantee safe and reliable power networks in the age of renewable energy.

title: Cost Efficient Charging Scheduling Methodology for Electric Vehicles in Multi-Type Charger Environments
authors: Krishan Chopra, Mukesh kumar Shah, K.R. Niazi
publish: 2025 IEEE 4th International Conference on Smart Technologies for Power, Energy and Control (STPEC)
abstract: Rapid deployment of electric vehicles (EVs) and different type of preferences by users have force to convert traditional charging station (CS) into smart charging stations to fulfil the needful requirements. It is important to reduce the service drooping rate by using optimum utilization of charging capacity. In this study we intend to model a queuing network for the working of a CS with multiple type of charging outlets (CO). An optimal pricing scheme is developed to manage EV charging processes aiming to reduce the drooping rate to the minimum of the CS having different type of charging outlets. Simulation results shows that the drooping rate is reduce by 76.39 % in compare to AC mode charging and drooping rate is reduced by 67.21 % in compare to DC mode charging.

title: Modelling and Analysis of a PV-Powered DC Wireless UAV Charging Station Using DIgSILENT PowerFactory
authors: Gopalakrishnan Renganathan, Nilanjan Tewari
publish: 2025 IEEE 4th International Conference on Smart Technologies for Power, Energy and Control (STPEC)
abstract: With the increasing utilization of Unmanned Aerial Vehicles (UAVs) across diverse applications, the challenge of efficient charging has been intensified due to the high-power demands of their motors and payload capacities. In this study, a PV-based wireless charging system for UAV modules has been analyzed through simulations conducted using DIgSILENT PowerFactory software. A constant DC voltage source of 30V has been employed to emulate the behavior of a photovoltaic array. The system configuration includes an MPPT-controlled buck converter, a high-frequency inductive power transfer (IPT) circuit, and a UAV load integrated with a dynamic battery unit. Power ratings between 25W and 60W have been considered for the source, with voltage levels observed to stabilize within the range of 11V to 30V. The performance of the proposed charging system has been evaluated in terms of its ability to wirelessly deliver energy from the emulated PV source, through the inductive coupling and converter stages, to a resistive load equipped with a rechargeable battery.

title: A Modified Virtual Impedance Based Current Limiting Control of Grid Connected PMSG Under Distorted Grid Conditions
authors: Amit Kumar, M. A. Asha Rani
publish: 2025 IEEE 4th International Conference on Smart Technologies for Power, Energy and Control (STPEC)
abstract: Under unbalanced grid conditions, wind farms face the potential risk of losing synchronism with the utility grid due to the mismatch between generated and consumed active power. This imbalance can lead to overvoltage at the DC-link, causing the converter to operate beyond its rated current limits and potentially leading to its failure. To address this issue, the virtual impedance technique offers an effective mitigation strategy. In this study, an adaptive control method incorporating virtual impedance is developed based on conventional Virtual Synchronous Generator (VSG) principles, aiming to limit the peak fault current within safe operating bounds. Furthermore, an analytical expression for the converter's output active power under the influence of adaptive virtual impedance is derived. The damping effect introduced by the virtual impedance helps suppress power oscillations and overshoot, thereby enhancing system stability and improving the dynamic response of the converter. The proposed control strategy is validated through simulations conducted on a 2.5 kW PMSG system using PSCAD/EMTDC.

title: ISE-Tuned Control for Frequency Regulation of Renewable-Integrated Airport Microgrid
authors: Sameer Singh, V. P. Singh, A. Mathur
publish: 2025 IEEE 4th International Conference on Smart Technologies for Power, Energy and Control (STPEC)
abstract: In this paper, issue related with the frequency regulation in airport microgrid (AM) is taken into account. For the mitigation of frequency deviation, proportional integral derivative (PID) controller is designed utilizing integral of squared error (ISE) criterion. Frequency deviation is mainly due to the combined effect of load variation and variable output of some renewable energy sources like wind turbine unit (WTU) and solar photovoltaic cells (SPVC). Designed controller is applied to AM and tested via various qualitative and quantitative analytical approaches. For comparative qualitative analysis, frequency responses are plotted for AM without and with PID controller tuned with various error criteria. Time domain specifications are also plotted for the purpose of quantitative analysis for AM. Further, various error indices are tabulated for AM without and with controllers for comparative analysis. Error indices are also analyzed through pie chart to represent the individual performance. Based on the discussed frequency plots with tabulated data, efficacy of ISE-tuned PID controller is verified among other controllers for frequency regulation of AM.

title: Robust Position Tracking Control of Underwater Vehicle-Manipulator System: Nonsingular Fast Terminal Quasi Sliding Mode
authors: Vikas L. Karade, Girish V. Lakhekar
publish: 2025 IEEE 4th International Conference on Smart Technologies for Power, Energy and Control (STPEC)
abstract: This study presents a control method for an underwater vehicle-manipulator system (UVMS) capable of achieving finite-time trajectory tracking in the task space while addressing system uncertainties and environmental disruptions. Factors such as ocean currents, wave dynamics, and alterations in physical qualities are recognised to influence the system erratically. A control rule employing a non-singular fast terminal sliding mode control (NFTSMC) and a time-delay estimating method is formulated to address unknown dynamics and external influences. To mitigate the chattering phenomenon in sliding mode control, we employ a smooth quasi-sliding mode approach that provides robustness without necessitating complex adaptation methods or extensive system identification. The stability of the system is analysed by Lyapunov-based methods, and an exact formula for the convergence time is established. Simulation results indicate that the proposed technique is effective, demonstrating that the system adheres to reliable paths despite the presence of uncertainties.

title: Adaptive Step Size Implementation of P&O MPPT Algorithm Using Particle Swarm Optimization
authors: Seema P. Diwan, Rajin M. Linus, Pradeep M. Diwan, Prathamesh S. Sawant, Aryan D. Singh
publish: 2025 IEEE 4th International Conference on Smart Technologies for Power, Energy and Control (STPEC)
abstract: While the Perturb and Observe (P&O) algorithm is a common choice for maximum power point tracking (MPPT) in photovoltaic (PV) setups due to its simple implementation, it frequently struggles with the conflict between rapid response times and steady-state chatter. This research introduces an adaptive step-size P&O strategy, refined through Particle Swarm Optimization (PSO), to enhance tracking capabilities across fluctuating environmental settings. By utilizing the PSO mechanism to dynamically adjust the P&O controller's perturbation steps, the system achieves accelerated convergence toward the maximum power point (MPP) while suppressing undesirable oscillations. The study incorporates a modeled boost converter interface to assess MPPT effectiveness via simulations across three distinct operating conditions: (1) uniform irradiance and thermal levels, (2) fluctuating irradiance at a fixed temperature, and (3) steady irradiance with shifting temperatures. Critical metrics, specifically tracking efficiency and ripple magnitude, are benchmarked against the standard fixed-step <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathrm{P} \& \mathrm{O}$</tex> approach. The findings indicate that the PSO-tuned adaptive P&O provides superior tracking precision and a marked reduction in steady-state oscillations, outclassing traditional P&O methods particularly during abrupt shifts in solar intensity.

title: Performance Analysis of a Dynamic Multi-Loop Thermal Management System for EVS in Diverse Ambient Conditions
authors: Vikas Verma, Bhavnesh Kumar
publish: 2025 IEEE 4th International Conference on Smart Technologies for Power, Energy and Control (STPEC)
abstract: Effective thermal management plays a vital role in maintaining the performance, safety, and durability of electric vehicles (EVs), especially in harsh environmental conditions. The thermal management system (TMS) plays a crucial role in controlling the temperatures of essential subsystems, including the battery, power electronics, electric motor, and cabin HVAC. The TMS operation is the most significant for the energy usage among auxiliary systems that directly impact the range of electric vehicles. Furthermore, the TMS design and analysis are complex, which poses considerable limitations for achieving optimal performance in real-time scenarios. The proposed analysis offers an extensive simulationdriven assessment of a dynamically regulated, multi-loop integrated TMS intended to function effectively in various ambient scenarios. The proposed system evaluates the temperature and energy aspects of each critical subsystem of the vehicle with the NEDC drive cycle in response to hot (40°C), standard (30°C, and cold (-10°C conditions. The system also adeptly coordinates dual cooling control strategies across sensitive components to maintain the desired aspect. The results indicate that the proposed modelling and analysis successfully uphold thermal balance in each ambient scenario. The energy consumption in each case is also analyzed to evaluate the correlation between the power requirement and the environmental scenario. This will foster reliability and wider acceptance of electric vehicles in various weather conditions.

title: Modeling and Control of Hybrid Fuel Cell-Battery System using a Bidirectional DC-DC Converter for EV and Microgrid Applications
authors: G Shri Meenakshi, Akashkumar R, Hemavathi S
publish: 2025 IEEE 4th International Conference on Smart Technologies for Power, Energy and Control (STPEC)
abstract: This paper presents the design, modeling, and simulation of a hybrid energy management system that integrates a Proton Exchange Membrane Fuel Cell (PEMFC) with Li-ion battery via a bidirectional DC-DC converter to achieve efficient and dynamic power flow control. The converter operates in buck mode to charge the battery when the fuel cell output exceeds the load demand and to discharge the battery when the fuel cell is inactive. A dual-loop Proportional-Integral (PI) control scheme ensures stable voltage regulation and smooth mode transitions under varying load conditions. The entire system was developed and validated in MATLAB/Simulink, demonstrating consistent load-voltage delivery of 46 V during fuel cell operation and 20 V during battery discharge. Simulation outcomes indicate enhanced transient response, minimized voltage ripple, and a 9.5 % improvement in energy efficiency compared to conventional unregulated systems. The proposed architecture offers a compact, scalable, and fault-tolerant solution suitable for electric vehicles (EVs), plug-in hybrid electric vehicles (PHEVs), microgrid integration, and renewable energy storage applications.

title: Smart, Sustainable Wireless EV Charging with Harris Hawks-Enhanced Control Strategy
authors: G Jawahar Sagar, Pillarisetty Varshitha, Sachidananda Sen, Chandan Kumar Shiva, B. Vedik, Tarkeshwar Matho
publish: 2025 IEEE 4th International Conference on Smart Technologies for Power, Energy and Control (STPEC)
abstract: The growing adoption of electric vehicles (EVs) demands compact, efficient, and isolated charging solutions. Conventional chargers often rely on bulky low-frequency transformers and lack flexibility in dynamic environments. To overcome these limitations, this paper proposes a unidirectional high-frequency wireless EV charging system with real-time monitoring and optimized control using Harris Hawk Optimization (HHO). The system includes a single-phase AC source, a passive diode rectifier, an LC filter, and a half-bridge inverter operating at 30 kHz. A CLCL resonant wireless power transfer (WPT) stage ensures efficient and isolated power delivery. On the receiver side, a diode-based rectifier charges a lithiumion battery while monitoring voltage, current, and state-of-charge (SOC). The optimization of the Proportional-Integral (PI) controller is achieved through HHO, which enhances the inverter's performance, DC-link stability, and overall system response. The HHO-based approach dynamically adjusts the PI gains to improve the charging process efficiency while minimizing overshoot and settling time. The system is modeled and simulated in MATLAB/Simulink. Results confirm efficient power transfer, low DC ripple, accurate SOC estimation, and superior control performance due to the HHO-optimized PI controller. The proposed architecture provides a compact, reliable, and adaptable solution for wireless EV charging applications.

title: Optimal Estimation of the State-of-Energy and Surface Temperature of Li-Ion Batteries Using an Extended Kalman Filter with Cramer-Rao Lower Bound
authors: Nilima Gadkar, Arijit Guha, Bikky Routh, Swastik Acharya
publish: 2025 IEEE 4th International Conference on Smart Technologies for Power, Energy and Control (STPEC)
abstract: Lithium-ion batteries (LIBs) are essential and have a large variety of applications, yet their safety and performance critically depend on temperature regulations. This study presents a compact electro-thermal model that integrates an electrical equivalent circuit representation with a thermal network description of the battery. Within this framework, a joint estimation of the battery's state-of-energy (SoE) and surface temperature is achieved using an extended Kalman filter (EKF). To rigorously assess the estimator's optimal performance, the Cramer-Rao Lower Bound (CRLB) has been utilised. The CRLB provides the theoretical lower limit on the variance of unbiased estimators. A comparative analysis of the estimated SoE with the generalized SoE calculated from the Watt-hour (Wh) method has been carried out. Simulation and experimental results prove that the EKF improves the estimation accuracy and also follows the CRLB criteria.

title: Design and Comparative Analysis of a Fractional Slot Concentrated Winding Interior PM V-Shaped Motor for Electric Vehicle Applications
authors: C. Bharatiraja, V S Nagarajan, V Rajini, Sanjeev Guhan K S, Dilsha Purushothaman, Mahesh Krishnamurthy
publish: 2025 IEEE 4th International Conference on Smart Technologies for Power, Energy and Control (STPEC)
abstract: This paper presents the design and comparative analysis of a Fractional Slot Concentrated Winding (FSCW) Interior Permanent Magnet (IPM) motor tailored for electric vehicle (EV) traction applications. A 24 -slot, 20 -pole FSCW-IPM motor is developed and benchmarked against a conventional 48-slot, 8-pole Integral Slot Distributed Winding (ISDW) IPM motor, both sharing identical geometric constraints. The motor designs incorporate V-shaped embedded magnets and are optimized using a machine learning-assisted design approach to explore a wide design space efficiently. Key aspects such as torque capability, copper utilization, power factor, and manufacturability are evaluated through finite element analysis. The study aims to demonstrate the advantages of FSCW topologies in terms of electromagnetic performance, cost-efficiency, and suitability for automated production. The findings support the growing relevance of FSCW motors, combined with intelligent design techniques, in next-generation EV propulsion systems.

title: Design of Axial Flux Ferrite Permanent Magnet Dc Motor for E-Bike Application
authors: Sagar Gupta, Amit Kumar Jain
publish: 2025 IEEE 4th International Conference on Smart Technologies for Power, Energy and Control (STPEC)
abstract: This paper presents the conceptualization and design of a cost-effective axial flux ferrite permanent magnet direct current (PMDC) motor tailored for e-bike applications. The proposed motor configuration is driven by the need for compactness, high torque density, and minimal control complexity, making it ideal for low-speed, high-torque scenarios such as electric bicycle propulsion. The axial flux topology is selected due to its superior torque-to-weight ratio and its ability to conform to strict axial length constraints, which are critical in hubmounted applications. Unlike conventional radial flux motors, axial flux machines offer a planar structure that facilitates integration within limited spatial envelopes while maintaining mechanical robustness and electromagnetic efficiency. The PMDC configuration eliminates the need for complex power electronics, thereby reducing system cost and enhancing reliability. The motor is designed to deliver an rated torque of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$4.2 ~\mathrm{N}-\mathrm{m}$</tex> and a stall torque of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$20 ~\mathrm{N}-\mathrm{m}$</tex> within a constrained envelope of 200 mm diameter and 40 mm axial length. Analytical calculations are employed to estimate the electromagnetic parameters, and the design is validated against performance requirements derived from real-world cycling conditions, including acceleration, rolling resistance, and aerodynamic drag. This work contributes to the growing body of research on sustainable and affordable electric mobility solutions by demonstrating a viable motor design that balances performance, cost, and manufacturability.

title: Hybrid Deep Q-Learning with Tabular Updates for Speed Prediction of Heavy Duty Vehicles
authors: Priyankaa Sarkar, Rajan Chaudhary, Nalin Kumar Sharma, Rahul Kala, Sri Niwas Singh
publish: 2025 IEEE 4th International Conference on Smart Technologies for Power, Energy and Control (STPEC)
abstract: This paper presents a hybrid reinforcement learning framework for predicting the speed of the leading heavy-duty vehicles (HDVs) operating over diverse terrains. Combining Qlearning with a Deep Q-Network (DQN), the method enables accurate speed prediction without requiring inter-vehicle communication. A feedforward neural network with ReLU activation models terrain features like slope and distance to guide optimal speed prediction. Achieving 93.59% prediction accuracy over a 0-48,200 meter range, the model demonstrates faster convergence than conventional Q-learning. The simulation results show that this approach is ideal for scalable and decentralised fleet management systems and supports energy-efficient driving in hilly terrain.

title: Whale Optimisation Algorithm for Optimum Sizing and Location of Solar-Based DGs in a Radial Distribution System
authors: Prakash D B, Venkatesh Boddapati, Chethan Raj D
publish: 2025 IEEE 4th International Conference on Smart Technologies for Power, Energy and Control (STPEC)
abstract: In a distribution network, the optimal location of Distributed Generations (DGs), particularly solar-based DGs, offers significant advantages such as a decrease in power losses, enhancement in voltage profiles, reduction of operating costs, enhanced system reliability and deferment of system upgrades. This study aims to employ the Whale Optimisation Algorithm (WOA) to fix the ideal size and location of solar DG units within a distribution system. The effectiveness of the proposed optimisation approach is validated using standard IEE-69 bus and 33-bus radial distribution systems. The investigation compares the network performance indicators, including overall power loss, voltage profile and operating cost, which are systematically evaluated. Results indicate that optimally placed solar DG units meaningfully enhance voltage profiles, reduce line losses and minimise operating costs. These improvements collectively lead to a marked decrease in overall power loss and an increase in net savings for the system. Furthermore, the study extends the analysis to scenarios with multiple solar DGs in both test systems (IEE-33 and IEE-69), presenting optimal solutions and corresponding performance metrics.

title: Social Spider Algorithm Assisted LSTM Network Towards the SoC Estimation of Li-ion Batteries
authors: Saneep K, Sundareswaran K, P Srinivasa Rao Nayak, Gireesh V Puthusserry, Kannan M, Midhun E K
publish: 2025 IEEE 4th International Conference on Smart Technologies for Power, Energy and Control (STPEC)
abstract: This paper proposes a Social Spider Algorithm (SSA) assisted Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) network towards State of Charge (SoC) estimation of Li-ion batteries. The accuracy of the SoC prediction can be improved by optimal tuning of the hyperparameters. The optimal values of hyperparameters are identified using a biologically inspired algorithm, namely the Social Spider Algorithm. The proposed algorithm is validated using publicly available datasets, namely CSHVC, HWFET, and US06, at different temperatures. The first 70% data of a typical dataset from each driving cycle is used for the optimization of hyperparameters, and the rest 30% data is used for model testing. Then, optimally tuned hyperparameters are used for the SoC estimation for the remaining datasets of the same driving cycle at various temperatures. The performance indices such as RMSE, MAE, MSE, MBE and <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$R^{2}$</tex> are computed and presented. The prediction error in SoC is always under 0.15 for all driving cycles. The presented results show that the proposed method is a promising tool for the SoC estimation.

title: Active Disturbance Rejection Control for Power Quality Improvement in Islanded Microgrids
authors: Revannath B. Kakade, S. Kayalvizhi
publish: 2025 IEEE 4th International Conference on Smart Technologies for Power, Energy and Control (STPEC)
abstract: Islanded microgrids with three-phase three-wire inverters face significant power quality challenges, including harmonic distortions and voltage imbalances, particularly under non-linear loads driven by the variability of renewable energy sources, compromising system stability and reliable power delivery in off-grid environments. This work proposes an Active Disturbance Rejection Control (ADRC) strategy to enhance power quality. By employing an extended state observer, ADRC dynamically estimates and compensates for internal uncertainties and external disturbances caused by non-linear loads, enabling robust voltage regulation without requiring detailed system modeling. The approach ensures high-quality, balanced out-put voltages across diverse operating conditions, significantly reducing total harmonic distortion compared to conventional control methods, while offering superior adaptability, resilience, and efficiency. Simulation results validate the effectiveness of ADRC, demonstrating improved power quality and reliable power delivery, highlighting its potential for sustainable energy solutions in remote microgrid applications.

title: A Hybrid Regression-PCNN Model for Detecting False Data Injection Attacks in Smart Grids using Image Encoding
authors: Makhavarapu Yaswanth, Kamal Singh, Sailaja Kumari M
publish: 2025 IEEE 4th International Conference on Smart Technologies for Power, Energy and Control (STPEC)
abstract: This article presents a technique for detecting cyberattacks in smart grid networks using regression analysis and deep learning methods. With the increasing digitalization of the electrical grid, cybersecurity has become a major concern. The proposed scheme is validated using real-time data extracted from Phasor Measurement Units (PMUs) to assess its effectiveness. The research primarily focuses on detecting False Data Injection (FDI) attacks, a type of cyberattack that manipulates system state variables, leading control operators to make incorrect assumptions about the system's state. These attacks can significantly disrupt power system operations. To detect such intrusions and enhance the security and reliability of grid operations, this study employs a hybrid approach of using regression analysis and deep learning-based approach using Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs). The proposed method is tested on real-time PMU measurements from the standard IEEE 39-bus system, demonstrating that the PCNN model achieves a high accuracy rate in detecting anomalous events.

title: Analysis and Quasi Sliding Mode Synchronization of Quadratic Hyperbolic Tangent Memductance Function Based Lorenz System
authors: Surajit Suklabaidya, Dathewbhalang Tariang, Piyush Pratap Singh, Binoy Krishna Roy
publish: 2025 IEEE 4th International Conference on Smart Technologies for Power, Energy and Control (STPEC)
abstract: This study explores the analysis of a memristive Lorenz system and synchronization between memristive Lorenz systems using a Quasi Sliding Mode Control (QSMC) approach. The memristive element is modeled with an internal statedependent memductance function based on a quadratic hyperbolic tangent profile, which introduces strong nonlinearity and enriches the system's dynamic behavior. The dynamical characteristics are examined using Lyapunov exponents and bifurcation diagrams, confirming its hyperchaotic nature and sensitivity to parameter variations. A robust QSMC strategy is designed to achieve master-slave synchronization between the Lorenz systems. The controller employs a boundary layer method to mitigate chattering while ensuring rapid convergence within a finite time. Simulation results validate that the proposed QSMC strategy achieves precise and stable synchronization, despite the presence of complex and highly nonlinear Lorenz system dynamics. These outcomes demonstrate the robustness of QSMC for high-order memristive systems.

title: Enhancement of Model Order Reduction for Discrete-Time Systems with Guaranteed Time-Domain Performance
authors: Kalyana Kiran Kumar, Kurman Sangeeta, Kranthi Kumar Saila, R.S.R Krishnam Naidu, Gandi Ramarao, Kanaka Raju Kalla, Neelima Ambati, Sivasai Tangudu, Pramod Kumar Gouda
publish: 2025 IEEE 4th International Conference on Smart Technologies for Power, Energy and Control (STPEC)
abstract: Model order reduction plays a critical role in simplifying complex discrete-time linear time-invariant (LTI) systems while preserving essential system dynamics. Traditional reduction techniques primarily focus on frequency-domain characteristics, often neglecting specific performance criteria crucial to control system design. This manuscript presents a novel method for reducing discrete-time systems with an explicit emphasis on matching key prominent parameters i.e., rise, settling times, over-shoot, and steady-state error. The proposed approach formulates model reduction as a constrained optimization problem, where the reduced model is derived to minimize time-domain response error relative to the original system (OS). Extensive numerical experiments on benchmark systems demonstrate the advantage of the projected method compared to traditional techniques.

title: Advanced Delay Architectures for Accurate Timing Control in High-Speed Automated Systems
authors: Narendra Singh Beniwal, Shashwat Singhal, Shruti Kalra, Ruby Beniwal
publish: 2025 IEEE 4th International Conference on Smart Technologies for Power, Energy and Control (STPEC)
abstract: This paper presents a comparative study of advanced Time-to-Digital Converter (TDC) architectures implemented in 45nm CMOS technology, focusing on their applicability in high-resolution, low-power control and automation systems. The evaluated designs achieve single-stage delays ranging from 11 ps to 13 ps and multi-stage delays between 43 ps and 50 ps, illustrating key trade-offs between resolution, complexity, and power efficiency. These performance characteristics are critical for precise timing and synchronization tasks in high-speed communication, clock control, and data acquisition systems. Furthermore, the optimized TDC architectures support energy-efficient operation, making them suitable for automation-oriented applications such as smart grids, renewable energy integration, and energy harvesting systems. The analysis provides design guidelines for selecting and implementing TDC architectures that balance accuracy, speed, and power efficiency, enabling reliable operation in modern control and automation environments.

title: Table of Contents
authors: N/A
publish: IEEE Transactions on Neural Networks and Learning Systems

title: Generative Semantic Communication: Diffusion Models Beyond Bit Recovery
authors: Eleonora Grassucci, Sergio Barbarossa, Danilo Comminiello
publish: IEEE Transactions on Cognitive Communications and Networking
abstract: Semantic communications have the potential to play a key role in next-generation AI-native communication systems, especially when combined with the expressivity power of generative models. In this paper, we focus on image transmission and present a novel generative-based semantic communication framework, whose core is a generative model operating at the receiver side. This model regenerates images suitable for downstream applications such as detection, reconstruction, and positioning of semantically relevant objects in the scene observed from the sensors present at the sender side. We devise the encoding rule to transmit only what is strictly relevant to trigger the generative model to fulfill the scope of the transmission. Furthermore, we propose a training strategy to make the generative model robust against additive noise due to propagation through the communication channel. We prove, through an in-depth assessment of multiple scenarios, that our method outperforms existing solutions in generating high-quality images with preserved semantic information even in cases where the received conditioning content is significantly degraded or compressed. More specifically, our results show that objects, locations, and depths are still recognizable even in the presence of highly noisy conditions of the communication channel or at very low bits per pixel.

title: DynEformer: a Unified Framework for Robust Workload Prediction under Dynamic Environment
authors: Shaoyuan Huang, Zheng Wang, Heng Zhang, Xiaofei Wang, Cheng Zhang, Wenyu Wang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Knowledge and Data Engineering
abstract: Workload prediction in multi-tenant edge cloud platforms (MT-ECP) is crucial for efficient application deployment and resource provisioning. However, the heterogeneous application patterns, variable infrastructure performance, and frequent deployments in MT-ECP pose significant challenges for accurate prediction. Existing clustering-based methods often incur excessive costs due to maintaining multiple data clusters and models, while end-to-end time-series prediction methods struggle with dynamic environments. To address these challenges, we perform a comprehensive analysis on a large-scale workload dataset in real-world MT-ECP and propose DynEformer, an end to-end framework with global pooling and static context aware ness, offering a unified workload prediction scheme for dynamic MT-ECP. Meticulously designed global pooling and information merging mechanisms can effectively identify and utilize global application patterns to drive local workload predictions. The integration of static content-aware mechanisms enhances model robustness in real-world scenarios. We also extend DynEformer's capabilities to Long-term workload forecasting (LTLF) and Long-period service (LPS) tasks. Experiments on six real-world datasets demonstrate that DynEformer achieves state-of-the-art performance, with a 32% relative improvement on nine baselines and a 52% improvement in application switching and new entity scenarios. Additional experiments on long-term prediction and online learning further confirm its effectiveness for LTLF and LPS tasks.

title: Real-time Tracking in a Pull-based Status Update System with Unreliable Command Channel
authors: Saeid Sadeghi Vilni, Abolfazl Zakeri, Mohammad Moltafet, Risto Wichman
publish: IEEE Internet of Things Journal
abstract: We consider a pull-based status update system consisting of a finite-state Markov source, an energy-harvesting-enabled transceiver, and a sink. Both data and command channels are error-prone. The sink is interested in real-time tracking of the source and sends commands for updates to the transceiver. However, due to noise in the command channel, the transceiver may experience false alarms or miss-detections. We study the problem of minimizing the long-term time average of a (generic) distortion subject to a constraint on the average number of commands. Due to the pull-based nature of the system, the controller (i.e., sink) has only partial observability of the source state and the available energy at the transceiver. Therefore, we model the problem as a constrained partially observable Markov decision process (C-POMDP), which is then cast as a constrained belief-MDP (C-belief-MDP) problem. The infinite belief-state space makes solving the C-belief-MDP difficult. Thus, we effectively truncate the belief-state space and formulate a finite-state C-belief-MDP problem. We develop a stationary randomized policy (SRP) to solve the problem by employing Lagrangian relaxation, the relative value iteration algorithm (RVIA), and bisection search. Due to the curse of dimensionality in deriving the SRP, we also develop a low-complexity policy applying the drift-plus-penalty method, which transforms the C-belief-MDP problem into a sequence of per-slot optimization problems. Simulation results show the effectiveness of the proposed policies and their superiority compared to baseline policies.

title: Physics-Guided Machine Learning for FMCW Radar-Based Human and Metal Target Classification
authors: Adil Karimov, Jawad Ahmad, Mohammad Hashmi
publish: IEEE Sensors Journal
abstract: Non-contact detection of humans and metal objects remains a key challenge in security systems. To address this, a physics-guided machine learning approach is proposed for human and metal target classification using a custom-built, batterypowered S-band (2.3–2.6 GHz) frequency-modulated continuouswave radar. The radar system is implemented using discrete components, incorporating designed Vivaldi antennas and an active low-pass filter for target detection. From 117 measurement scenarios across three target classes: person, metal, and person-metal combine, a comprehensive framework of 51 base physics-guided features was developed and systematically aggregated across temporal windows to yield 199 calibration-independent features. Feature selection via Random Forest importance, Relief-F, mRMR, and Boruta analysis identifies 10-80 features maintaining baseline performance, with minimal subsets enabling resourceconstrained deployment. Nested cross-validation with hyperparameter tuning across five classifiers achieved 96.66% accuracy using Random Forest, with 100% recall for human-containing classes. Comparative evaluation demonstrated that physics-guided features outperform an optimized 17.3M-parameter CNN by 12.9% (96.66% vs. 83.8%). The calibrationindependent design, validated through ablation analysis, enables proposed radar deployment without specialized equipment and provides a standalone, battery-powered system suitable for practical security and surveillance applications.

title: Self-Balanced Hexagon-Type Multilevel Inverter with Low Total Standing Voltage and High Efficiency
authors: Abdul Hadi, Jul-Ki Seok
publish: IEEE Open Journal of Power Electronics
abstract: This paper presents a seven-level switched-capacitor bipolar multilevel inverter topology that offers a minimum number of whole components. The proposed topology generates an output waveform with a single DC source, three capacitors with a rating equal to the DC source, and eight active switches. Key features include a reduced voltage stress on switches, the triple voltage gain for enhanced energy conversion efficiency, the absence of bidirectional switches, a lower total standing voltage of 14<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$V\_{dc}$</tex-math></inline-formula>, and an inherent capacitor self-balancing mechanism. The study explores the fundamental operation, the strategy for voltage balancing, and the adopted control strategy. The proposed structure is evaluated against the prevailing seven-level topologies in terms of performance, structural complexity, implementation parameters, and efficiency. Matlab/Simulink-based simulations are conducted to assess the steady-state and dynamic response of the proposed topology, and these results are corroborated by experimental evaluation.

title: Accurate Carrier Frequency Offset Estimation Using Modulation Phase Cancellation for PCM/FM Telemetry Signals
authors: Jinlong Ren, Guohua Wei, Jiaqi Zhang, Wenjing Wang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Aerospace and Electronic Systems
abstract: Carrier frequency offset (CFO) estimation is a fun damental task in pulse code modulation/frequency modulation (PCM/FM) telemetry. In addition to its conventional role in frequency correction for data symbol detection, CFO inherently carries Doppler information associated with target motion, making it po tentially useful for assisting radial velocity measurement. However, existing CFO estimators are mainly designed for data detection, and their estimation accuracy is often limited. To enable precise velocity measurement, this paper proposes a high-precision CFO estimation method based on modulation phase cancellation. Unlike conventional statistics-based approaches, the proposed method constructs the modulation phase by performing symbol detection and premodulation filter estimation. This allows effective cancellation of the modulation phase induced by transmitted data symbols, thereby producing a single-tone CFO signal. Furthermore, a recursive least squares-based framework is developed to estimate the response of the premodulation filter, where a suitable filter order is determined by minimizing the designed residual phase error. Finally, the performance of the proposed method is validated using both numerical simulations and experimental data. The results demonstrate that the proposed method substantially improves CFO estimation accuracy and achieves performance closer to the modified Cramer-Rao bound (MCRB).

title: Radiation-Hardened Design and System-Level Validation of a Sub-20 nm FinFET 1–32 Gbps SerDes Against 5 Mrad(Si) TID Effects
authors: Yi Wen, Jianjun Chen, Bin Liang, Yaqing Chi, Jun Huang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Nuclear Science
abstract: This study systematically characterizes the system-level response of a 1–32 Gbps Serializer/Deserializer (SerDes) fabricated in a sub-20 nm FinFET technology under Total Ionizing Dose (TID) environments, focusing on its applicability in high-radiation aerospace applications. Radiation testing uses Co⁶⁰ gamma rays with a cumulative dose up to 5 Mrad(Si) at 100 rad(Si)/s, with the chip powered during irradiation to simulate real operating conditions. Performance of core sub-modules (Phase-Locked Loop (PLL), Transmitter (TX), Receiver (RX)) is evaluated via eye diagrams, jitter, and Bit Error Rate (BER) before and after irradiation. Prior to irradiation, the 32 Gbps SerDes meets PCIe 5.0 requirements, with a system BER better than 1×10⁻¹⁴ at 32.68 dB channel loss. After 5 Mrad(Si) TID irradiation and 48-hour annealing, key metrics (eye height/width, BER) degrade moderately (≤18.7%) while the system remains functionally stable. The proposed radiation-hardened design introduces approximately 23.8% area overhead and 31.4% power overhead compared with the unhardened implementation. This work achieves system-level verification of a 32 Gbps sub-20 nm FinFET SerDes under 5 Mrad(Si) TID. Integrating Radiation-Hardened-by-Design (RHBD) with adaptive calibration, it provides a cost-effective radiation-hardened solution for high-speed data links in high-radiation aerospace applications and fills the gap in system-level radiation verification for advanced-process high-speed SerDes.

title: Elastic Multi-Gradient Descent for Parallel Continual Learning
authors: Fan Lyu, Wei Feng, Yuepan Li, Qing Sun, Fanhua Shang, Liang Wan, Liang Wang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Pattern Analysis and Machine Intelligence
abstract: Learning a single shared model across multiple tasks is a long-standing goal in machine learning, traditionally addressed through Multi-Task Learning (MTL) or Serial Continual Learning (SCL). However, MTL assumes fixed task availability, limiting adaptability to new tasks, while SCL suffers from delayed response due to its strictly sequential learning protocol. This paper studies the novel paradigm of Parallel Continual Learning (PCL) in dynamic multi-task scenarios, where a diverse set of tasks is encountered at different time points. PCL inherits the challenges of both MTL and SCL, namely, task conflict and catastrophic forgetting, compounded by the dynamic and asynchronous arrival of tasks. To address these challenges, we propose Elastic Multi-Gradient Descent (EMGD), which formulates PCL as a dynamic multi-objective optimization problem. EMGD introduces task-specific elastic factors to guide gradient updates toward Pareto-optimal directions, ensuring balanced learning across tasks. Additionally, we develop a gradient-guided memory editing mechanism that aligns rehearsal data with the optimized descent direction, mitigating memory-induced interference. Theoretical analysis shows that accumulation points of the EMGD update rule are Pareto critical under the proposed formulation, and extensive experiments on image classification benchmarks demonstrate that EMGD significantly outperforms existing methods, including state-of-the-art PCL, MTL, and SCL approaches.

title: Secure Formation Control for Heterogeneous UAV-UGV System With Switching Topologies Under Injection Attacks
authors: Meijie Zhang, Xinsong Yang, Xingxing Ju
publish: IEEE Transactions on Industrial Informatics
abstract: This article investigates secure formation control of a heterogeneous multi-unmanned aerial vehicle (UAV)-unmanned ground vehicle (UGV) system (HMUUS) under nonidentical malicious injection attacks and switching topologies. A unified motion description framework is established to address the system's heterogeneity. By decomposing attacked output signals into nonattacked and attack-related components, two observers are designed to estimate the leader and follower states based solely on nonattacked components, while simultaneously estimating the attack signal for each agent. Based on the estimated states, a distributed controller is designed to ensure that HMUUS can achieve formation. Sufficient conditions are derived to ensure the monotonic decrease of the Lyapunov–Krasovskii functional (LKF) over the entire time domain of HMUUS with communication delays by constructing a piecewise linear time-varying LKF, which significantly reduces conservatism. Simulations and experiments for the HMUUS are conducted to validate the formation under switching topologies and injection attacks.

title:
Effect of Mg Doping on β-Ga
                    <sub>2</sub>
                    O
                    <sub>3</sub>
                    UVC Photodetectors Deposited by Ultrasonic Spray Pyrolysis
authors: Shun-Cheng Shih, Chien-Sheng Cheng, Han-Yin Liu, Hao-Chun Hung, Rong-Ming Ko, Wei-Chou Hsu
publish: IEEE Sensors Journal
abstract: This study investigates a comparative analysis of metal–semiconductor–metal (MSM) photodetectors (PDs) based on β-Ga₂O₃ thin films with varying levels of Mg doping. Thin films with Mg / (Mg + Ga) ratios of 0%, 5%, and 10% were deposited on c-plane sapphire substrates using ultrasonic spray pyrolysis deposition (USPD). Structural, chemical, optical, and morphological properties were systematically characterized using X-ray diffraction (XRD), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), UV–visible (UV–Vis) spectroscopy, scanning electron microscopy (SEM), atomic force microscopy (AFM), and photoluminescence (PL) spectroscopy. Results indicate that increasing Mg doping level significantly reduces the oxygen vacancy concentration and improves the surface smoothness, while simultaneously widening the optical bandgap from 4.82 to 5.25 eV. Device measurements show that the PD based on 5% Mg-doped β-Ga₂O₃ achieved the highest photoresponsivity of 2.54 × 10⁴ A/W. In contrast, the 10% Mg-doped PD exhibited the lowest dark current (1.1 × 10⁻¹¹ A), along with a high photo-to-dark current ratio (PDCR) of 4.4 × 10⁶, a UV-to-visible rejection ratio of 4.8 × 104, and a detectivity of 1.4 × 10¹⁷ Jones. These findings demonstrate strong potential of USPD-grown Mg-doped β-Ga₂O₃ thin films for next-generation UVC PDs.
